0.text: PART 1 Citation, Commencement, Application And The Overriding Objective

3.text:
1.1   
These Rules may be cited as The Rules of the Dubai International Financial Centre Courts 2014 and may be abbreviated to “RDC $2 0 1 4 ^ { \prime \prime }$ .

5.text:
1.2   
These Rules are made by the President of the Dubai International Financial Centre on the date specified in the Enactment Notice in respect of these Rules .

6.text: 1.3 These Rules come into force on the date specified in the Enactment Notice in respect of these Rules .

8.text: Any rule amendments subsequent to the enactment of law No. 7 of Year 2011 on 10 July 2011 are made by the Chief Justice of the DIFC Courts.

10.text: 1.5 These Rules apply to all proceedings in:

11.text: (1) the Court of First Instance; and (2) the Court of Appeal

12.text: established in accordance with Article 3 of the Law of the Judicial Authority at Dubai International Financial Centre, Law No.12 of 2004, and

13.text: (3) tribunals established in accordance with Article 14(3) of the DIFC Courts Law of 2004 which are collectively called the DIFC Courts .

15.text:
1.6   
These Rules have the overriding objective of enabling the Courts to deal with cases justly. Dealing with a   
case justly includes, so far as is practicable:

16.text: (1) ensuring that the parties are on an equal footing;

18.text: (3) dealing with the case in ways which are proportionate—

19.text: (a) to the amount of money involved;

20.text: (b) to the importance of the case;

21.text: (c) to the complexity of the issues; and (d) to the financial position of each party;

22.text: (4) ensuring that it is dealt with expeditiously and fairly; and (5) allotting to it an appropriate share of the Courts’ resources, while taking into account the need to allot resources to other cases.

23.text: Application By The Courts Of The Overriding Objective

25.text: The Courts must seek to give effect to the overriding objective when it—

26.text: (1) exercises any power given to it by the Rules ; or (2) interprets any Rule or Practice Direction subject only to the public interest.

28.text: 1.8 The parties are required to help the Courts to further the overriding objective.

31.text: The Courts must further the overriding objective by actively managing cases including:

35.text: Authentic Text And Language Of Proceedings

36.text: 2.1 The English text is the authentic text of these Rules.

37.text: 2.2 All proceedings before the Courts shall be conducted in the English language.

38.text: 2.3 All proceedings before the Courts shall be conducted in the English language.

40.text: Where any judgment , order or direction is to be served outside the DIFC in the UAE, unless the Courts otherwise direct:

41.text: (1) in proceedings on notice the parties must agree and lodge with the Courts an Arabic translation of the judgment , order or direction within 2 days of same being given, made or handed down as the case may be, in default of which the Courts may accept one party’s translation or suspend or revoke the judgment , order or direction on such terms as it thinks fit;

42.text: (2) in without notice proceedings the party seeking relief must provide an Arabic translation of the order sought and made;

43.text: (3) in the event of any inconsistency between the Arabic and English versions of any judgment , order or direction the English version shall prevail.

45.text: 2.5 Unless the context otherwise requires, the words and expressions set out in the Schedule to this Rule shall have the respective meanings assigned to each of them for all purposes in connection with these Rules.

48.text: In these Rules , a reference to:

49.text: (1) a person includes any natural person, body corporate or body unincorporate, including a company, partnership, unincorporated association, government or state;

50.text: (2) the masculine gender includes the feminine;

51.text: (3) an obligation to publish or cause to be published a particular document shall, unless expressly provided otherwise in these Rules , includes publishing or causing to be published in printed or electronic form.

53.text: The headings in these Rules shall not affect their interpretation.

55.text: References to legislation in these Rules shall be construed in accordance with the following provisions:

58.text: Pending the enactment of a DIFC Interpretation Act and subject to the express provisions of these Rules and any other DIFC legislation, the English Interpretation Act 1978 shall apply as far as necessary for the interpretation of these Rules as it applies to English subordinate legislation made after the commencement of that Act.

59.text: Application Of The Cpr And The Guide

61.text: If no provision is made or no appropriate form is provided by the Rules or any law in force in the DIFC , the following rules of practice and procedure shall be followed and adopted:

62.text: (1) Such Rules as shall have been enacted;

63.text: (2) To the extent that no Rule or Practice Direction dealing with the matter shall have been enacted, with regard to the Court of First Instance, the Guide together with any prescribed forms with such changes as the Court considers appropriate to be applied in the circumstances;

64.text: (3) Insofar as the Guide does not deal with the matter or makes reference to the CPR, the CPR together with any prescribed forms with such changes as the Court considers appropriate to be applied in the circumstances;

65.text: (4) With regard to the Court of Appeal, Part 52 of the CPR and any associated practice directions, as therein defined, together with any prescribed forms with such changes as the Court considers appropriate to be applied in the circumstances.

66.text: (5) With regard to insolvency proceedings, the English Insolvency Rules 1986.

68.text: 2.11 All dates shall be ascertained in accordance with the Gregorian calendar.

70.text: A period of time expressed as a number of days shall be computed as clear days. “Clear days” means that in computing the number of days—

71.text:
(1) the day on which the period begins; and   
(2) If the end of the period is defined by reference to an event, the day on which that event occurs are not included.

74.text: (1) is 5 days or less; and (2) includes a day which is not a business day that day does not count.

76.text: Where the specified period is 6 days or more, both normal working days in the DIFC and non-working days in the DIFC shall be counted.

80.text: (2) by any judgment or Courts order,

81.text: for doing any act at the Registry ends on a day on which the Registry is closed, that act shall be in time if done on the next day on which the Registry is open.

82.text: Dates For Compliance To Be Calendar Dates And To Include Time Of Day

84.text: Where the Courts give a judgment , order or direction which imposes a time limit for doing any act, the last date for compliance must, wherever practicable

85.text: (1) be expressed as a calendar date; and

86.text: (2) include the time of day by which the act must be done.

88.text: Where a party is required by a Rule, Practice Direction, Judgment or Order to do any act on or before a particular date, the act must be done by 4pm on that date.

90.text: Where the date by which an act must be done is inserted in any document, the date must, wherever practicable, be expressed as a calendar date.

93.text: Where ‘month’ occurs in any judgment , order, direction or other document, it means a calendar month.

94.text: Time Limits May Be Varied By Parties

96.text: Unless these Rules provide otherwise or the Court orders otherwise, the time specified by a Rule or Practice Direction or by the Court for a person to do any act may be varied by the written agreement of the parties and the Court.

100.text: 5.1 The Chief Justice may by Practice Direction prescribe the forms to be used in proceedings before the Court.

101.text:
5.2   
A form may be varied by the Court or a party if the variation is required by the circumstances of a particular case.

103.text: A form must not be varied so as to leave out any information or guidance which the form gives to the recipient.

105.text: Where these Rules require a form to be sent by the Court or by a party for another party to use, it must be sent without any variation except such as is required by the circumstances of the particular case.

109.text:
6.1   
Where under these Rules, a document is to be prepared by the Court , the document may be prepared by the party whose document it is, unless a Court Officer otherwise directs. 6.2   
Nothing in these Rules shall require a Court Officer to accept a document which is illegible, has not been duly authorised, or is unsatisfactory for some other similar reason.

110.text: Signature Of Documents By Mechanical Means

112.text: Where these Rules require a document to be signed, that requirement shall be satisfied if the signature is printed by computer or other mechanical means.

114.text: Where a replica signature is printed electronically or by other mechanical means on any document, the name of the person whose signature is printed must also be printed so that the person may be identified.

117.text: Statements of case and other documents drafted by a legal representative should bear his signature and if they are drafted by a legal representative as a member or employee of a firm they should be signed in the name of the firm.

119.text: Every document prepared by a party for filing or use at the Court must—

120.text:
(1) unless the nature of the document renders it impracticable, be on A4 paper,   
(2) be fully legible and should normally be typed,   
(3) where possible be bound securely in a manner which would not hamper filing or otherwise each page should be endorsed with the case number,   
(4) have the pages numbered consecutively,   
(5) be divided into numbered paragraphs,   
(6) have all numbers, including dates, expressed as figures, and   
(7) give the reference of every document mentioned that has already been filed.

122.text: 6.7 The Court shall keep an online publicly accessible register of claims which have been issued.

123.text: Supply Of Documents To A Party From Court Records

125.text: A party to proceedings may, unless the Court orders otherwise, obtain from the records of the Court a copy of any of the following documents:

126.text: (1) a claim form or other statement of case together with any documents filed with or attached to or intended by the claimant to be served with such claim form;

127.text: (2) an acknowledgment of service together with any documents filed with or attached to or intended by the party acknowledging service to be served with such acknowledgement of service ;

128.text: (3) a certificate of service , other than a certificate of service of an application notice or order in relation to a type of application mentioned in sub-paragraph (5) below;

130.text: (5) an application notice , other than in relation to:

131.text: (a) an application by a legal representative for an order declaring that he has ceased to be the legal representative acting for a party; or

132.text: (b) an application for an order that the identity of a party or witness should not be disclosed;

133.text: (6) any written evidence filed in relation to an application, other than a type of application mentioned in sub-paragraph (5) above;

134.text: (7) a judgment or order given or made in public (whether made at a hearing or without a hearing);

137.text: (10) a notice of payment into Court ;

139.text: (12) a notice of change of legal representative ; or

140.text: (13) an appellant’s or respondent’s notice of appeal.

142.text: A party to proceedings may, if the Court gives permission, obtain from the records of the Court a copy of any other document filed by a party or communication between the Court and a party or another person.

143.text: Supply Of Documents To A Non-Party From Court Records

145.text: Save where Rule 6.11 applies, the general rule is that a person who is not a party to proceedings may obtain from the Court records a copy of:

146.text: (1) a statement of case , but not any documents filed with or attached to the statement of case , or intended by the party whose statement it is to be served with it;

147.text: (2) a judgment or order given or made in public (whether made at a hearing or without a hearing).

149.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise, a non-party may not obtain information or documents from the Court

150.text: records relating to insolvency proceedings before a winding-up petition has been advertised.

152.text: A non-party may, if the Court gives permission, obtain from the records of the Court a copy of any other document filed by a party, or communication between the Court and a party or another person.

154.text: A non-party may obtain a copy of a statement of case or judgment or order under Rule 6.11 only if:

157.text: The Court may, on the application of a party or of any person identified in a statement of case :

158.text:
(1) order that a non-party may not obtain a copy of that statement of case under Rule 6.11; (2) restrict the persons or classes of persons who may obtain a copy of that statement of case ; (3) order that persons or classes of persons may only obtain a copy of that statement of case if it is edited in accordance with the directions of the Court ; or   
(4) make such other order as it thinks fit.

160.text: A person wishing to apply for an order under Rule 6.14 must file an application notice in accordance with Part 23.

162.text: Where the Court makes an order under Rule 6.14, a non-party who wishes to obtain a copy of the statement of case , or to obtain an unedited copy of the statement of case , may apply on notice to the party or person identified in the statement of case who requested the order, for permission.

163.text: Supply Of Documents From Court Records — General

165.text: A person wishing to obtain a copy of a document under Rules 6.9 to 6.12 must pay any prescribed fee and if the Court’s permission is required, file an application notice in accordance with Part 23.

167.text: An application for an order under Rule 6.14 or for permission to obtain a copy of a document under Rules 6.9 to 6.12 may be made without notice, but the Court may direct notice to be given to any person who would be affected by its decision.

169.text: Rules 6.7 to 6.16 do not apply in relation to any proceedings in respect of which a rule or practice direction makes different provision.

172.text: All documents to be filed in the DIFC Courts should be submitted to the Court in electronic form only preferably through the E-Filing facility available on the DIFC Courts website, with the following exception:

175.text: Electronic documents may be filed with the Courts by:

176.text: (1) The E-Filing facility — this should be used unless it is impossible in the circumstances; or (2) Emailing the documents to the Registry email account at registry@difccourts.ae; or (3) Delivering any form of data storage media containing the electronic files to the Registry .

178.text: Where documents are filed by email:

181.text: Where documents are filed by delivering any form of data storage media to the Court :

182.text: (1) The data storage media must be accompanied by a covering letter listing all of the documents contained in the disk;

183.text: (2) The Registry will acknowledge the covering letter as received once the contents of the data storage media have been checked to confirm the presence of all of the documents listed in the covering letter.

185.text: Where a document is filed in hard copy, the date on which the document was filed at Court must be recorded on the document. This may be done by a seal or a receipt stamp.

187.text: A surcharge may be imposed by the Court for documents not submitted using the E-Filing facility as per the fee schedule as amended from time to time.

189.text: Particulars of the date of delivery at Court of any document for filing and the title of the proceedings in which the document is filed shall be entered in Court records, on the Court file or on a computer record kept for the purpose. Except where a document has been delivered at the Registry through the mail, the time of delivery should also be recorded.

191.text: Applications may be made by filing an application notice by email. Save in cases of extreme urgency, where the application attracts a fee, the application will not be considered filed until the fee is paid.

193.text: Where the Court orders any document to be lodged in Court , the document must, unless otherwise directed, be deposited in the Registry .

196.text: A document filed, lodged or held in the Registry shall not be taken out of the office without the permission of the Court except in accordance with Rules 6.30 to 6.35 below.

198.text: Where a document filed, lodged or held in the Registry is required to be produced to any court, tribunal or arbitrator, the document may be produced by sending it by courier (together with a Certificate as set out below) to the court, tribunal or arbitrator.

200.text: On receipt of the request the Court Officer will submit the same to the Registrar who may direct that the request be complied with. Before giving a direction the Registrar may require to be satisfied that the request is made in good faith and that the document is required to be produced for the reasons stated. The Registrar may also direct that, before the document is sent, an official copy of it is made and filed in the

201.text: Registry at the expense of the party requiring the document to be produced.

203.text: On the direction of the Registrar the Court Officer shall send the document by courier addressed to the court, tribunal or arbitrator, with:

204.text: (1) an envelope stamped and addressed for use in returning the document to the Court ;

205.text: (2) a Certificate as set out below;

206.text: (3) a covering letter describing the document, stating at whose request and for what purpose it is sent and containing a request that the document be returned to the Court in the enclosed envelope as soon as the court, tribunal or arbitrator no longer requires it.

208.text: It shall be the duty of the court, tribunal or arbitrator to whom the document was sent to keep it in safe custody, and to return it by courier to the Court as soon as the court, tribunal or arbitrator no longer requires it.

210.text: A record shall be kept of each document sent and the date on which it was sent and the court, tribunal or arbitrator to whom it was sent and the date of its return. It shall be the duty of the Court Officer who has signed the certificate as set out below to ensure that the document is returned within a reasonable time and to make inquiries and report to the Registrar if the document is not returned, so that steps may be taken to secure its return.

212.text: Notwithstanding the above, the Registrar may direct a Court Officer to attend the court, tribunal or arbitrator for the purpose of producing the document.

214.text: I, of , an officer of the court/tribunal at /an arbitrator of /the Claimant /Defendant /Legal Representative of the Claimant/ Defendant [describing the Applicant so as to show that he is a proper person to make the request] in the case of v.

215.text: REQUEST that the following document [or documents] be produced to the court/tribunal/arbitrator on the day of 20xx [and following days] and I request that the said document [or documents] be sent by courier to the proper officer of the court/tribunal/arbitrator for production to that court/tribunal/arbitrator on that day.

219.text: I, A.B., an officer of the DIFC Courts certify that the document sent herewith for production to the court/tribunal/arbitrator on the day of $2 0 \times \times$ in the case of v. and marked ‘A.B.’ is the document requested on the day of $2 0 \times \times$ and I FURTHER CERTIFY that the said document has been filed in and is produced from the custody of the Court.

222.text: PART 7 How To Start Proceedings — The Claim Form

225.text:
7.1   
Except as provided by a Practice Direction proceedings must be started by a claim form under Part 7 or Part 8 using Form P7/01 or Form P8/01 respectively.

227.text: If a claimant wishes his claim to proceed under Part 8, or if the claim is required to proceed under Part 8, the claim form should so state. Otherwise the claim will proceed under Part 7.

228.text: 7.3 Proceedings are started when the Court issues a claim form at the request of the claimant .

230.text: A claim form is issued on the date entered on the form by the Court .

232.text: 7.5 Where the claim form as issued was received in the Registry on a date earlier than the date on which it was issued by the Court , the claim is ‘brought’ for the purposes of limitation on that earlier date.

234.text: The date on which the claim form was received by the Registry will be recorded by a date stamp either on

235.text: the claim form held on the Court file or on the letter that accompanied the claim form when it was received by the Court .

237.text: An enquiry as to the date on which the claim form was received by the Registry should be directed to a Court Officer.

239.text: Parties proposing to start a claim which is approaching the expiry of the limitation period should recognise the potential importance of establishing the date the claim form was received by the Registry and should themselves make arrangements to record the date.

240.text: Claims By And Against Partnerships Within The Difc Or Dubai

242.text: Rules 7.9 to 7.11 apply to claims that are brought by or against two or more persons who:

244.text: (2) carried on that partnership business within the DIFC or Dubai,

245.text: at the time when the cause of action accrued.

247.text: For the purposes of Rules 7.9 and 7.11, “partners”rdquo; includes persons claiming to be entitled as partners and persons alleged to be partners.

249.text: Where that partnership has a name, unless it is inappropriate to do so, claims must be brought in or against the name under which that partnership carried on business at the time the cause of action accrued.

252.text: In Rules 7.13 and 7.14 a “partnership membership statement” is a written statement of the names and last known places of residence of all the persons who were partners in the partnership at the time when the cause of action accrued, being the date specified for this purpose in accordance with Rule 7.14.

254.text: If the partners are requested to provide a copy of a partnership membership statement by any party to a claim, the partners shall do so within 14 days of receipt of the request.

256.text: In that request the party seeking a copy of a partnership membership statement must specify the date when the relevant cause of action accrued.

257.text: Persons Carrying On Business In Another Name

262.text: Where this Rule applies, the claim may be brought against the business name as if it were the name of a partnership.

263.text: Right To Use One Claim Form To Start Two Or More Claims

265.text: A claimant may use a single claim form to start all claims which can be conveniently disposed of in the same proceedings.

268.text: The claim form and every other statement of case must be headed with the title of the proceedings. The title should state:

269.text:
(1) the number of proceedings,   
(2) the circuit or division of the Court (if any) or Tribunal in which they are proceeding, (3) the full name of each party,   
(4) his status in the proceedings (i.e. claimant /defendant ).

271.text: Where there is more than one claimant and/or more than one defendant , the parties should be described in the title as follows:

278.text: After a claim form has been issued, it must be served on the defendant by the claimant :

279.text: (1) within 4 months after the date of issue where the claim form is to be served within the DIFC or Dubai; and (2) within 6 months after the date of issue where the claim form is to be served out of the DIFC or Dubai.

280.text: Extension Of Time For Serving A Claim Form

281.text: 7.21 The claimant may apply for an order extending the period within which the claim form may be served.

283.text: The general rule is that an application to extend the time for service must be made:

284.text:
(1) within the period for serving the claim form specified by Rule 7.20; or   
(2) where an order has been made extending the period within which the claim form may be served, within the period for service specified by that order.

286.text: If the claimant applies for an order to extend the time for service of the claim form after the end of the periods specified by Rule 7.22 the Court may make such an order only if:

287.text:
(1) the Court has been unable to serve the claim form; or   
(2) the claimant has taken all reasonable steps to serve the claim form but has been unable to do so; and   
(3) in either case, the claimant has acted promptly in making the application.

289.text: An application for an order extending the time for service must be made in accordance with Part 23 and supported by evidence stating:

290.text:
(1) all the circumstances relied on,   
(2) the date of issue of the claim,   
(3) the expiry date of any Rule 7.21 extension, and   
(4) a full explanation as to why the claim has not been served.

292.text: The application may be made without notice.

293.text: Application By Defendant For Service Of Claim Form

295.text: Where a claim form has been issued against a defendant , but has not yet been served on him, the defendant may serve a notice on the claimant requiring him to serve the claim form or discontinue the claim within a period specified in the notice.

297.text: The period specified in a notice served under Rule 7.26 must be at least 14 days after service of the notice.

299.text: If the claimant fails to comply with the notice, the Court may, on the application of the defendant :

300.text: (1) dismiss the claim; or (2) make any other order it thinks just.

303.text: The claim form and, where they are not included in the claim form, the particulars of claim, must be verified by a statement of truth in accordance with Part 22.

305.text: The form of the statement of truth is as follows:

306.text: ‘[I believe][the claimant believes] that the facts stated in [this claim form] [these particulars of claim] are true.’

309.text: If particulars of claim are not contained in or served with the claim form:

310.text: (1) the claim form must state that, if an acknowledgment of service is filed which indicates an intention to defend the claim, particulars of claim will follow;

311.text: (2) the claimant must serve particulars of claim within 28 days of the filing of an acknowledgment of service which indicates an intention to defend.

313.text: If the claimant is claiming interest, he must:

314.text: (1) include a statement to that effect; and

315.text: (2) give the details set out in Rule 17.18,

316.text: in both the claim form and the particulars of claim.

318.text: Where the claimant serves particulars of claim separately from the claim form in accordance with Rule 7.31(2) he must within 7 days of service on the defendant , file a copy of the particulars together with a certificate of service .

320.text: All claim forms and counterclaims must state:

321.text: (a) the law which the claimant maintains governs the dispute. The defendant must expressly agree or disagree with that assertion (in the acknowledgment of service form); and

322.text: (b) the law which the claimant maintains gives rise to the jurisdiction of the DIFC Courts in respect of their claim. The defendant must expressly agree or disagree with that assertion (in the acknowledgment of service form).

324.text: When the claim form is served on a defendant , it must be accompanied by a cover letter indicating that: (a) a form for defending the claim; (b) a form for admitting the claim or; (c) a form for acknowledging service must be filed by the defendant using the Court’s e-filing system.

326.text: Where the claimant is using the procedure set out in Part 8 (alternative procedure for claims), the claimant must serve with the claim form a cover letter indicating that a form for defending the claim, a form for admitting the claim or a form for acknowledging service must subsequently be filed by the defendant using the Court’s e-filing system.

333.text: Types Of Claim In Which Part 8 Procedure May Be Followed

335.text: A claimant may use Part 8 procedure where:

336.text:
(1) he seeks the Court ’s decision on a question which is unlikely to involve a substantial dispute of fact; or   
(2) a Rule or Practice Direction in relation to a specified type of proceedings requires or permits the use of the Part 8 procedure.

338.text: Rule 8.1 does not apply if a Rule or Practice Direction provides that the Part 8 procedure may not be used in relation to the type of claim in question.

340.text: Where it appears to a Court Officer that a claimant is using the Part 8 procedure inappropriately, he may refer the claim to a Judge for the Judge to consider the point.

342.text: The Court may at any stage order the claim to continue as if the claimant had not used the Part 8

343.text: procedure and, if it does so, the Court may give any directions it considers appropriate.

344.text: Application Of Part 8 And Other Rules And Practice Directions

345.text:
8.5   
Part 8 contains Rules applicable to all claims, including those to which Part 8 applies. Those rules and directions should be applied where appropriate.

348.text: (1) provision is made in this Part for the matters which must be stated in the claim form and the defendant is not required to file a defence and therefore:

350.text: (2) the claimant may not obtain default judgment under Part 13.

351.text: (3) the claimant may not obtain judgment by request on an admission and therefore:

352.text:
(a) Rules 15.14 to 15.31 do not apply; and   
(b) the requirement under Rule 7.35 to serve on the defendant a form for admitting the claim does not apply.

354.text: A Rule or Practice Direction may, in relation to a specified type of proceedings:

355.text: (1) require or permit the use of the Part 8 procedure; and (2) disapply or modify any of the rules set out in this Part as they apply to those proceedings.

358.text: Where the claimant uses Part 8 procedure the claim form (Form P8/01) should be used and must state:

359.text: (1) that this Part applies; (2) the question which the claimant wants the Court to decide; or the remedy which the claimant is

360.text: seeking and the legal basis for the claim to that remedy;

361.text: (3) if the claim is being made under an enactment, what that enactment is; (4) if the claimant is claiming in a representative capacity, what that capacity is; and (5) if the defendant is sued in a representative capacity, what that capacity is.

362.text: Issue Of Claim Form Without Naming Defendants

364.text: A Practice Direction may set out circumstances in which a claim form may be issued under this Part without naming a defendant .

366.text: The Practice Direction may set out those cases in which an application for permission must be made by application notice before the claim form is issued.

369.text: (1) need not be served on any other person; and (2) must be accompanied by a copy of the claim form that the applicant proposes to issue.

371.text: Where the Court gives permission it will give directions about the future management of the claim.

375.text: (1) file an acknowledgment of service in Form P8/02 not more than 14 days after service of the claim form; and

376.text: (2) serve the acknowledgment of service on the claimant and any other party.

378.text: The acknowledgment of service must state:

379.text: (1) whether the defendant contests the claim; and (2) if the defendant seeks a different remedy from that set out in the claim form, what that remedy is.

381.text: The following rules of Part 11 (acknowledgment of service ) apply:

382.text: (1) Rule 11.6 (exceptions to the period for filing an acknowledgment of service ); and (2) Rule 11.8 (contents of acknowledgment of service ).

383.text: Consequence Of Not Filing An Acknowledgment Of Service

386.text: (1) the defendant has failed to file an acknowledgment of service ; and

387.text: (2) the time period for doing so has expired;

388.text: the defendant may attend the hearing of the claim but may not take part in the hearing unless the Court gives permission.

389.text: Procedure Where Defendant Objects To Use Of The Part 8 Procedure

391.text: Where the defendant contends that the Part 8 procedure should not be used because:

392.text: (1) there is a substantial dispute of fact; and

393.text: (2) the use of the Part 8 procedure is not required or permitted by a Rule or Practice Direction;

394.text: he must state his reasons when he files his acknowledgment of service . If the statement of reasons includes matters of evidence, it should be verified by a statement of truth.

396.text: When the Court receives the acknowledgment of service and any written evidence it will give directions as to the future management of the case.

399.text: The Court may give directions immediately after a Part 8 claim form is issued, either on the application of a party or on its own initiative.

401.text: Where the Court does not fix a hearing date when the claim form is issued, it will give directions for the disposal of the claim as soon as practicable after the defendant has acknowledged service of the claim form or, as the case may be, after the period for acknowledging service has expired.

403.text: Certain applications may not require a hearing.

405.text: The Court may convene a directions hearing before giving directions.

408.text: The claimant must file any written evidence on which he intends to rely when he files his claim form.

410.text: The claimant’s evidence must be served on the defendant with the claim form (unless the evidence is contained in the claim form itself).

412.text: Evidence will normally be in the form of a witness statement or an affidavit but a claimant may rely on the matters set out in his claim form provided that it has been verified by a statement of truth.

414.text: A defendant who wishes to rely on written evidence must file it when he files his acknowledgment of service .

416.text: If he does so, he must also, at the same time, serve a copy of his evidence on the other parties.

418.text: The claimant may, within 14 days of service of the defendant’s evidence on him, file further written evidence in reply.

420.text: If he does so, he must also, within the same time limit, serve a copy of his evidence on the other parties.

422.text: A party may apply to the court for an extension of time to serve and file evidence or for permission to serve and file additional evidence.

424.text: The parties may, subject to the following provisions, agree in writing on an extension of time for serving and filing evidence.

426.text: An agreement extending time for a defendant to file evidence:

427.text: (1) must be filed by the defendant at the same time as he files his acknowledgement of service ; and (2) must not extend time by more than 14 days after the defendant files his acknowledgement of service .

429.text: An agreement extending time for a claimant to file evidence in reply must not extend time to more than 28 days after service of the defendant’s evidence on the claimant .

432.text: No written evidence may be relied on at the hearing of the claim unless:

433.text: (1) it has been served in accordance with Rules 8.23 to 8.31; or

436.text: The Court may require or permit a party to give oral evidence at the hearing.

438.text: The Court may give directions requiring the attendance for cross-examination of a witness who has given written evidence.

441.text: Where Part 8 procedure is used, Part 21 (counterclaims and other additional claims ) applies except that a party may not make an additional claim (as defined by Rule 21.2) without the Court’s permission.

444.text: All applications made before a claim is commenced should be made under this Part, unless the Court orders otherwise.

446.text: The written evidence in support of such an application must state that the claimant intends to bring proceedings.

450.text: Part 9 Rules About Service Apply Generally

451.text:
9.1   
The Rules in this Section of this Part apply to the service of documents in the DIFC and Dubai, except   
where:

452.text: (1) any other enactment, a Rule in another Part, or a Practice Direction makes a different provision; or (2) the Court orders otherwise.

455.text: A document may be served by any of the following methods:

458.text: Where a document is to be served by electronic means:

459.text: (1) the party who is to be served or his legal representative must previously have expressly indicated in writing to the party serving:

460.text: (a) that he is willing to accept service by electronic means; and (b) the fax number, e-mail address or electronic identification to which it should be sent;

461.text: (2) the following shall be taken as sufficient written indication for the purposes of sub-paragraph (1) above:

462.text: (a) a fax number set out on the writing paper of the legal representative of the party who is to be served; or

463.text: (b) a fax number, e-mail address or electronic identification set out on a statement of case or a response to a claim filed with the Court ;

464.text: (3) the party seeking to serve the document should first seek to clarify with the party who is to be served whether there are any limitations to the recipient’s agreement to accept service by such means including the format in which documents are to be sent and the maximum size of attachments that may be received;

465.text: (4) the address for service given by a party must be within the DIFC or Dubai and any fax number must be at the address for service . Where an e-mail address or electronic identification is given in conjunction with an address for service , the e-mail address or electronic identification will be considered to be at the address for service ;

466.text: (5) the party serving the document need not in addition send a hard copy by mail.

468.text: A company may be served by any method permitted under this Part as an alternative to:

469.text: (1) service by leaving a document at or mailing it to the registered office of a company (Article 20(3) of the Companies Law (Amended and Restated), Law 3 of 2006);

470.text: (2) service upon a Recognised Company by a person appointed to accept service on the behalf of that Company pursuant to Article 116(1) of the Companies Law (Amended and Restated), Law 3 of 2006.

473.text: The party on whose behalf a document is to be served shall serve a document which it has issued or prepared except where:

474.text:
(1) a Rule or Practice Direction provides that the Court must serve the document in question; (2) the Court orders otherwise; or   
(3) the Court has failed to serve and has sent a notice of non-service to the party on whose behalf the document is to be served in accordance with Rule 9.37.

476.text: Where the Court is to serve a document, it is for the Court to decide which of the methods of service specified in Rule 9.2 is to be used.

478.text: Where a party prepares a document which is to be served by the Court , that party must file a copy for the Court , and for each party to be served.

480.text: 9.8 A document to be served may be served personally, except as provided in Rules 9.9 and 9.10.

483.text: (1) is authorised to accept service on behalf of a party; and

484.text: (2) has notified the party serving the document in writing that he is so authorised;

485.text: a document must be served on the legal representative , unless personal service is required by an enactment, Rule, Practice Direction or Court order.

487.text: In civil proceedings brought before the Court to which the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government is a party, documents required to be served before the Court may not be served personally.

489.text: A document is served personally on an individual by leaving it with that individual.

491.text: A document is served personally on a company or other corporation by leaving it with a person holding a senior position within the company or corporation. Each of the following persons is a person holding a senior position:

492.text: (1) in respect of a registered company or corporation, a director, the treasurer, secretary, chief executive, manager or other officer of the company or corporation, and

493.text: (2) in respect of a corporation which is not a registered company, in addition to those persons set out in sub-paragraph (1), the mayor, chairman, president, town clerk or similar officer of the corporation.

495.text: A document is served personally on a partnership where partners are being sued in the name of their firm by leaving it with:

497.text: (2) a person who, at the time of service , has the control or management of the partnership business at its principal place of business.

499.text: 9.14 Except as provided by Section III of this Part, a document must be served within the DIFC or Dubai.

501.text: A party must give an address for service within the DIFC or Dubai. A physical address in Dubai or the UAE will not be required if an email address is given.

504.text: (1) does not give the business address of his legal representative as his address for service ; and (2) resides or carries on business within the DIFC or Dubai; he must give his residence or place of business as his address for service .

507.text: (1) by courier (or an alternative service which provides for delivery on the same or next working day); (2) by leaving it at the place of service ; or (3) by means of electronic communication; must be sent or transmitted to, or left at, the address for service given by the party to be served.

510.text: (1) a legal representative is acting for the party to be served; and (2) the document to be served is not the claim form; the party’s address for service is the business address of his legal representative .

513.text: (1) no legal representative is acting for the party to be served; and (2) the party has not given an address for service , the document must be sent or transmitted to, or left at, the place shown in the following table.

514.text: Nature of party to be served

516.text:
IndividualUsual or last known residence.
Proprietor of a businessUsual or last known residence; or Place of business or last known place of business.
Individual who is suing or being sued in the name of a firmUsual or last known residence; or Principal or last known place of business of the firm.
Corporation incorporated in the DIFC other than a companyPrincipal office of the corporation; or Any place within the DIFC or Dubai where the corporation carries on its activities and which has a real connection with the claim.
Company registered in the DIFCPrincipal office of the company; or Any place of business of the company within the DIFC or Dubai which has a real connection with the claim.
Any other company or corporationAny place within the DIFC or Dubai where the corporation carries on its activities; or Any place of business of the company within the DIFC or Dubai.

518.text: Rules 9.14 to 9.19 do not apply where an order made by the Court under Rule 9.31 (service by an alternative method) specifies where the document in question may be served.

520.text: A party or his legal representative who changes his address for service shall give notice in writing of the change as soon as it has taken place to the Court and every other party.

521.text: Service Of Documents Brought Before The Court To Which The Centre Or Any Of The Centre’S Bodies Or The Government Is A Party

523.text: In proceedings brought before the Court to which the Centre, or any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government is a party, service must be effected in accordance with Part 41.

524.text: Service Of Documents On Children And Patients

526.text: The following table shows the person on whom a document must be served if it is a document which would otherwise be served on a child or a patient—

527.text: Type of document Nature of party Person to be served

528.text:
Claim formChild who is not also a patientOne of the child's parents or guardians; or If there is no parent or guardian, the person with whom the child resides or in whose care the child is.
Claim formPatientThe person authorised to conduct the proceedings in the name of the patient or on his behalf; or If there is no person so authorised, the person with whom the patient resides or in whose care the patient is.

530.text: The Court may make an order permitting a document to be served on the child or patient, or on some person other than the person specified in the table in Rule 9.23.

532.text: An application for an order under Rule 9.24 may be made without notice.

534.text: The Court may order that, although a document has been served on someone other than the person specified in the table in Rule 9.23, the document is to be treated as if it had been properly served.Considered date of service

536.text: A document which is served in accordance with these Rules or any relevant Practice Direction shall be considered to be served on the day shown in the following table

537.text:
Method of serviceConsidered day of service
Courier (or an alternative service whichprovides for delivery on the same or nextworking day)The second business day after the date itwas sent.
Delivering the document to or leaving it ata permitted addressFaxOther electronic methodIf it is delivered, left or transmitted on abusiness day before 4pm., on that day; orIn any other case, on the business dayafter the day on which it is delivered, left ortransmitted.

539.text: If a document is served personally:

540.text: (1) after 4 p.m., on a business day ; or

541.text: (2) at any time on a day which is not a business day ;

542.text: it will be treated as being served on the next business day .

545.text: Where the claim form is to be served in the United Arab Emirates outside the DIFC , it must be accompanied by a certified Arabic translation of the claim form.

547.text: Every translation to be served under Rule 9.29 must be accompanied by a statement by the person making it that it is a correct translation, and the statement must include:

548.text: (1) the name of the person making the translation; (2) his address; and (3) his qualifications for making a translation.

550.text: 9.31 Where it appears to the Court that there is a good reason to authorise service by a method not permitted by these Rules , the Court may make an order permitting service by an alternative method.

552.text: An application for an order permitting service by an alternative method—

553.text: (1) must be supported by evidence stating

554.text: (a) the reason an order for an alternative method of service is sought; and (b) what steps have been taken to serve by other permitted means; and

557.text: An order permitting service by an alternative method must specify:

558.text: (1) the method of service ; and (2) the date when the document will be considered to be served.

559.text: Power Of Court To Dispense With Service

560.text: 9.34 The Court may dispense with service of a document.

562.text: An application for an order to dispense with service may be made without notice.

565.text: Where a Rule, Practice Direction or Court order requires a certificate of service , the certificate must state the details set out in the following table:

566.text:
Method of serviceDetails to be certified
CourierDate of delivery
PersonalDate and time of personal service
Delivery of document to or leaving it at a permitted placeDate and time when the document was delivered to or left at the permitted place
FaxDate and time of transmission
Other electronic meansDate and time of transmission and the means used
Alternative method permitted by the CourtAs required by the Court

567.text: Notification Of Outcome Of Service By Courier By The Court

570.text: (1) a document to be served by the Court is served by courier; and

571.text: (2) such document is returned to the Court ,

572.text: the Court must send notification to the party who requested service stating that the document has been returned.

573.text: Notice Of Non-Service By Court Bailiff

576.text: (1) the Court Bailiff is to serve a document; and

577.text: (2) the Court Bailiff is unable to serve it;

578.text: the Court must send notification to the party who requested service .

579.text: II SPECIAL PROVISIONS ABOUT SERVICE OF THE CLAIM FORM

580.text: General Rules About Service Subject To Special Rules About Service Of Claim Form

582.text: The general rules about service are subject to the special rules about service contained in Rules 9.40 to 9.50.

583.text: Service Of Claim Form By The Court — Defendant’S Address For Service

585.text: Where a claim form is to be served by the Court , the claim form must include the defendant’s address for service .

587.text: For the purposes of Rule 9.40, the defendant’s address for service may be the business address of the defendant’s legal representative if he is authorised to accept service on the defendant’s behalf but not otherwise.

588.text: Certificate Of Service Relating To The Claim Form

590.text: Where a claim form is served by the Court , the Court must send the claimant a notice which will include the date when the claim form is considered to be served under Rule 9.27.

592.text: Where the claim form is served by the claimant :

593.text: (1) he must file a certificate of service not later than 7 days after the date for the filing by the defendant of the acknowledgment of service , unless by that date the acknowledgment of service has been filed; and

594.text: (2) he may not obtain judgment in default under Part 13 unless he has filed the certificate of service .

595.text: Service Of Claim Form By Contractually Agreed Method

598.text: (1) a contract contains a term providing that, in the event of a claim being issued in relation to the contract, the claim form may be served by a method specified in the contract; and

599.text: (2) a claim form containing only a claim in respect of that contract is issued;

600.text: the claim form shall be considered to be served on the defendant if it is served by a method specified in the contract.

602.text: Should parties wish to agree in writing on an alternative address for service, such as a chosen domicile service (CDS) provider, at which to serve their claim form in the event of any future dispute, as well as any further documents in the course of the proceedings, they may do so. The chosen address for service may be located in the DIFC, Dubai or another location, provided that the parties’ intention to opt for such an agreed method for service is evident in the applicable contract, or subsequent written agreement. This in no way limits parties’ prerogative to nominate a lawyer to accept service of court documents on their behalf (see in this regard Rules 9.9, 9.16 and 9.18 above).

603.text: Service Of Claim Form On Agent Of Principal Who Is Outside The Difc

606.text: (1) the defendant is outside the DIFC or Dubai; and

607.text: (2) the conditions specified in Rule 9.47 are satisfied;

608.text: the Court may, on an application only, permit a claim form relating to a contract to be served on a defendant’s agent.

610.text: The Court may not make an order under the preceding Rule unless it is satisfied that:

611.text: (1) the contract to which the claim relates was entered into within the DIFC or Dubai with or through the defendant’s agent; and

612.text: (2) at the time of the application either the agent’s authority has not been terminated or he is still in business relations with his principal.

615.text: (1) must be supported by evidence setting out:

616.text: (a) full details of the contract and that it was entered into within the DIFC or Dubai with or through an agent who is either an individual residing or carrying on business within the DIFC or Dubai, or a registered company or corporation having a registered office or a place of business within the DIFC or Dubai;

617.text: (b) that the principal for whom the agent is acting was, at the time the contract was entered into and is at the time of making the application, neither an individual, registered company or corporation as described in sub-paragraph (a) above; and

618.text: (c) why service out of the DIFC or Dubai cannot be effected; and (2) may be made without notice.

620.text: An order under Rule 9.47 must state a period within which the defendant must respond to the claim.

622.text: The power conferred by this rule is additional to the power conferred by Rule 9.31 (service by an alternative method).

624.text: Where the Court makes an order under this rule, the claimant must send to the defendant copies of:

627.text: III SPECIAL PROVISIONS ABOUT SERVICE OUT OF THE DIFC OR DUBAI

631.text: (1) service out of the DIFC or Dubai; and (2) the procedure for serving out of the DIFC or Dubai.

632.text: Service Out Of The Difc Or Dubai

634.text: Given the international nature of the DIFC , permission to serve process outside the DIFC is not required, but it is the responsibility of the party serving process to ensure he complies with the rules regarding service of the place where he is seeking to effect service .

636.text: Where a claim form is to be served out of the DIFC or Dubai, it may be served by any method permitted by the law of the place in which it is to be served.

638.text: Nothing in these Rules or in any Court order shall authorise or require any person to do anything in the place where the claim form is to be served which is against the law of that place.

640.text: A defendant who wishes to dispute the Court’s jurisdiction may make an application to have service set

641.text: Period For Acknowledging Service Or Admitting The Claim Where The Claim Form Is Served Out Of The Difc Or Dubai

643.text: The period for filing an acknowledgment of service or filing or serving an admission where a claim form has been served out of the DIFC or Dubai is 28 days after the service of the claim form.

644.text: Period For Filing A Defence Where The Claim Form Is Served Out Of The Difc Or Dubai

646.text: The period for filing a defence where a claim form has been served out of the DIFC or Dubai is:

647.text: (1) Where particulars of claim were included in or accompanied the claim form the period for serving and filing a defence is 45 days; or

648.text: (2) Where particulars of claim were not included in and did not accompany the claim form, the period for serving and filing a defence is 28 days from service of the particulars of claim.

651.text: Except where Rule 9.62 applies, every copy of the claim form to be served out of the DIFC or Dubai must be accompanied by a translation of the claim form.

654.text: (1) in the official language of the place in which it is to be served; or

655.text: (2) if there is more than one official language of that place, in any official language which is appropriate to the place where the claim form is to be served.

657.text: Every translation to be served under Rule 9.59 must be accompanied by a statement by the person making it that it is a correct translation, and the statement must include:

658.text: (1) the name of the person making the translation;

660.text: (3) his qualifications for making a translation.

663.text: The claimant is not required to provide a translation of a claim form under Rule 9.59 where the claim form is to be served:

664.text: (1) in a place where English is an official language; or (2) on a State where English is an official language of the State where the claim form is to be served.

665.text: Undertaking To Be Responsible For Expenses Of The Registrar

667.text: Every request for service out of the DIFC and Dubai through the Registrar must contain an undertaking by the person making the request:

668.text: (1) to be responsible for all expenses incurred by the Registrar or foreign judicial authority; and (2) to pay those expenses to the Registrar or foreign judicial authority on being informed of the amount.

669.text: Service Of Documents Other Than The Claim Form

671.text: Where an application notice is to be served out of the DIFC and Dubai under this Section of this Part:

672.text: (1) Rules 9.57 and 9.58 do not apply; and

673.text: (2) where the person on whom the application notice has been served is not a party to proceedings, that person may make an application to the Court under Rule 12.1 as if he were a defendant and Rule 12.2 does not apply.

677.text: (1) a hearing is fixed when the claim is issued;

678.text: (2) the claim form is served on a defendant out of the DIFC and Dubai; and

679.text: (3) that defendant does not appear at the hearing;

680.text: the claimant may take no further steps against that defendant until the claimant files written evidence showing that the claim form has been duly served.

681.text: IV SERVICE OF PROCESS OF OTHER COURTS

684.text: This Section of this Part applies to the service in the DIFC of any court process in connection with civil or commercial proceedings in another court or tribunal .

687.text: Process will be served where the Registrar receives:

689.text: (a) from a consular or other authority; or (b) from the Governor with a recommendation that service should be effected;

690.text: (2) a translation of that request into English;

691.text: (3) two copies of the process to be served; and

692.text: (4) unless the other court or tribunal certifies that the person to be served understands the language of the process, two copies of a translation of it into English.

694.text: 9.68 The process will be served by the Court Bailiff unless the Registrar otherwise directs.

698.text: (1) send the Registrar a copy of the process, and:

699.text:
(a) proof of service ; or   
(b) a statement why the process could not be served; and   
(c) if the Registrar directs, specify the costs incurred in serving or attempting to serve the process.

701.text: The Registrar will send the following documents to the person who requested service :

702.text: (1) a certificate, sealed with the seal of the Court , stating:

703.text: (a) when and how the process was served or the reason why it has not been served; and (b) where appropriate, an amount certified to be the costs of serving or attempting to serve the

706.text: V PROCEDURE FOR ISSUE OF CLAIM FORM WHEN REGISTRY CLOSED

710.text:
(1) The claim form must be signed by the legal representative acting on behalf of the claimant , and   
must not require the permission of the Court for its issue (unless such permission has already been   
given)   
(2) The legal representative causing the claim form to be issued (“the issuing legal representative”)   
must (a) endorse on the claim form the endorsement shown below and sign that endorsement; (b) send a copy of the claim form so endorsed to the Registry by email for issue under this Section; and (c) complete and sign the certificate shown below.

711.text: (3) When the Registry is next open to the public after the issue of a claim form in accordance with this procedure, the issuing legal representative or his agent shall attend and deliver to the Registry the document which was transmitted by email (including the endorsement and the certificate), or if that document has been served, a true and certified copy of it, together with as many copies as the Registry shall require and pay any required Court fees.

712.text: (4) When the proper officer at the Registry has checked and is satisfied that the document delivered under sub-paragraph (3) fully accords with the document received under sub-paragraph (2), and that all proper fees for issue have been paid, he shall allocate a number to the case, and seal , mark as “original” and date the claim form with the date on which it was issued (being, as indicated below, the date when the email is recorded at the Registry as having been received).

713.text: (5) As soon as practicable thereafter, the issuing legal representative shall inform any person served with the unsealed claim form of the case number, and (on request) shall serve any such person with a copy of the claim form sealed and dated under sub-paragraph (4) above and the person may, without paying a fee, inspect and take copies of the documents lodged at the Registry under sub-paragraphs (2) and (3) above.

714.text: Effect Of Issue Following Request By Email

716.text: The issue of a claim form in accordance with this procedure takes place when the email is recorded at the

717.text: Registry as having been received, and the claim form bearing the endorsement shall have the same effect for all purposes as a claim form issued under RDC Part 7 or 8, as the case may be. Unless otherwise ordered the sealed version of the claim form retained by the Registry shall be conclusive proof that the claim form was issued at the time and on the date stated. If the procedure set out in this Section is not complied with, the Court may declare (on its own initiative or on application) that the claim form shall be treated as not having been issued.

720.text: A claim form issued pursuant to a request by email must be endorsed as follows:

721.text: 1. This claim form is issued under RDC Rule 9.71 and may be served notwithstanding that it does not bear the seal of the Court .

722.text: 2. A true copy of this claim form and endorsement has been transmitted to the Registry, DIFC Courts, Level 4, The Gate, Dubai, UAE at the time and date certified below by the undersigned legal representative.

723.text: 3. It is the duty of the undersigned legal representative or his agent to attend at the Registry when it is next open to the public for the claim form to be sealed.

724.text: 4. Any person upon whom this unsealed claim form is served will be notified by the undersigned legal representative of the number of the case and may require the undersigned legal representative to serve a copy of the sealed claim form and may inspect without charge the documents which have been lodged at the Registry by the undersigned legal representative .

725.text: 5. I, the undersigned legal representative , undertake to the Court , to the defendants named in this claim form, and to any other person upon whom this claim form may be served:

728.text: Legal Representative for the claimant” [Note: the endorsement may be signed in the name of the firm rather than an individual legal representative , or by agents in their capacity as agents acting on behalf of their professional clients.]

731.text: A legal representative who causes a claim form to be issued pursuant to a request sent by email must sign a certificate in the following form:

732.text: I, the undersigned legal representative, certify that I have sent a copy of this claim form to the Registry by email on [enter the time and date when the email was sent].

736.text: [Note: the certificate may be signed in the name of the firm rather than an individual legal representative, or by agents in their capacity as agents acting on behalf of their professional clients]

737.text: PART 10 Responding To Particulars Of Claim — General

740.text: When a defendant is served with a claim form that is not accompanied by Particulars of Claim, he may—

741.text: (1) file or serve an admission in accordance with Part 15 admitting the whole of the claim;

742.text: (2) where he admits only part of the claim, serve an admission in accordance with Part 15 admitting that part of the claim and file an acknowledgment of service in accordance with Part 11 in relation to the balance of the claim; or

743.text: (3) where he does not admit any part of the claim, file an acknowledgment of service in accordance with Part 11.

745.text: When particulars of claim are served on a defendant , the defendant may:

746.text: (1) file or serve an admission in accordance with Part 15;

747.text: (2) file a defence in accordance with Part 16;

748.text: (or do both, if he admits only part of the claim); or

749.text: (3) where particulars of claim are served with the claim form, file an acknowledgment of service in accordance with Part 11.

753.text:
11.1   
Where the claimant uses the procedure set out in Part 8 (alternative procedure for claims) this Part applies subject to the modifications set out in Rules 8.13 to 8.15.

755.text: A defendant shall file an acknowledgment of service if:

756.text: (1) the claimant serves a claim form without serving particulars of claim; (2) the defendant is unable to file a defence within the period specified in Rule 16.9; or (3) the defendant wishes to dispute the Court ’s jurisdiction.

758.text: A defendant who wishes to acknowledge service of a claim should do so by using Form P11/01.

759.text: Consequence Of Not Filing An Acknowledgment Of Service

762.text: (1) a defendant fails to file an acknowledgment of service within the period specified in Rule 11.5; and (2) does not within that period file a defence in accordance with Part 16 or serve or file an admission in accordance with Part 15,

763.text: the claimant may obtain default judgmentG if Part 13 allows it.

764.text: The Period For Filing An Acknowledgment Of Service

765.text: 11.5 The general rule is that the period for filing an acknowledgment of service is 14 days after service of the

769.text: The general rule is subject to the following rules:

770.text: (1) Rule 9.56 (which specifies how the period for filing an acknowledgment of service is calculated where the claim form is served out of the DIFC and Dubai); and

771.text: (2) Rule 9.48 (which requires the Court to specify the period for responding to the particulars of claim when it makes an order under that Rule).

772.text: Notice To Claimant That Defendant Has Filed An Acknowledgment Of Service

773.text: 11.7 On filing an acknowledgement of service, the defendant must provide a copy to the claimant.

777.text: (1) be signed by the defendant or his legal representative; and

778.text: (2) include the defendants’ address for service.

780.text: Where the defendant is represented by a legal representative and the legal representative has signed the acknowledgment of service form, the address must be the legal representative’s business address; otherwise the address for service that is given should be as set out in Rules 9.15 and 9.16.

782.text: Where the defendant is a company or other corporation, a person holding a senior position in the company or corporation may sign the acknowledgment of service on the defendant’s behalf, but must state the position he holds.

784.text: Each of the following persons is a person holding a senior position:

785.text: (1) in respect of a registered company or corporation, a director, the treasurer, secretary, chief executive, manager or other officer of the company or corporation, and

786.text: (2) in respect of a corporation which is not a registered company, in addition to those persons set out in (1), the mayor, chairman, president, town clerk or similar officer of the corporation.

788.text: Where a claim is brought against a partnership:

789.text: (1) service must be acknowledged in the name of the partnership on behalf of all persons who were partners at the time when the cause of action accrued; and

790.text: (2) the acknowledgment of service may be signed by any of those partners, or by any person authorised by any of those partners to sign it.

792.text: 11.13 The defendant’s name should be set out in full on the acknowledgment of service.

794.text: Where the defendant’s name has been incorrectly set out in the claim form, it should be correctly set out on the acknowledgment of service followed by the words ‘described as’ and the incorrect name.

796.text: If two or more defendants to a claim acknowledge service of a claim through the same legal representative at the same time, only one acknowledgment of service need be used.

798.text: An acknowledgment of service may be amended or withdrawn only with the permission of the Court. The application must be made in accordance with Part 23 and supported by evidence.

803.text: (1) dispute the Court’s jurisdiction to try the claim; or

804.text: (2) argue that the Court should not exercise its jurisdiction;

805.text: may apply to the Court for an order declaring that it has no such jurisdiction or should not exercise any jurisdiction which it may have.

807.text: A defendant who wishes to make such an application must first file an acknowledgment of service in accordance with Part 11.

809.text: A defendant who files an acknowledgment of service does not, by doing so, lose any right that he may have to dispute the Court’s jurisdiction.

811.text: An application under this Part must:

812.text: (1) be made within 14 days after filing an acknowledgment of service; and

815.text: If the defendant files an acknowledgment of service and does not make an application disputing the Court’s jurisdiction within the period specified in Rule 12.4:

816.text: (1) he is to be treated as having accepted that the Court has jurisdiction to try the claim; and (2) if his acknowledgment of service indicates an intention to dispute jurisdiction, he will be treated as if he has not filed an acknowledgment of service for the purposes of any application for judgment in default under Part 13.

818.text: If the defendant files an acknowledgment of service indicating an intention to dispute the Court’s jurisdiction, the claimant need not serve particulars of claim before the hearing of the application.

820.text: An order containing a declaration that the Court has no jurisdiction or will not exercise its jurisdiction may also make further provision including:

823.text: If on an application under this Part the Court does not make a declaration:

824.text: (1) the acknowledgment of service shall cease to have effect;

825.text: (2) the defendant may file a further acknowledgment of service within 14 days or such other period as the Court may direct; and

826.text: (3) the Court shall give directions as to the filing and service of the defence in a claim under Part 7 or the filing of evidence in a claim under Part 8 in the event that a further acknowledgment of service is filed.

828.text: If the defendant files a further acknowledgment of service in accordance with Rule 12.8(2) he shall be treated as having accepted that the Court has jurisdiction to try the claim.

830.text: If a defendant makes an application under this Part, he must file and serve his written evidence in support with the application notice, but he need not before the hearing of the application file:

831.text: (1) in a Part 7 claim, a defence; or (2) in a Part 8 claim, any other written evidence.

834.text: 13.1 In these Rules, ‘default judgment’ means judgment without trial where a defendant.

835.text: (1) has failed to file an acknowledgment of service; or (2) has failed to file a defence.

836.text: 13.2 For the purposes of this Part, a defence includes any document purporting to be a defence.

837.text: Claims In Which Default Judgment May Not Be Obtained

839.text: A claimant may not obtain a default judgment —

840.text: (1) where he uses the procedure set out in Part 8 (alternative procedure for claims); or

841.text: (2) in any other case where a Rule or Practice Direction provides that the claimant may not obtain default judgment.

844.text: The claimant may obtain judgment in default of an acknowledgment of service only if—

845.text: (1) the defendant has not filed an acknowledgment of service or a defence to the claim (or any part of the claim); and

846.text: (2) the relevant time for doing so has expired.

848.text: Judgment in default of defence may be obtained only—

849.text: (1) where an acknowledgement of service has been filed but a defence has not been filed; (2) in a counterclaim made under Rule 21.7, where a defence has not been filed, and, in either case, the relevant time limit for doing so has expired.

851.text: The claimant may not obtain a default judgment:

852.text: (1) if the defendant has applied: (a) to have the claimant’s statement of case struck out under Rule 4.16; or (b) for immediate judgment under Part 24; and, in either case, that application has not been disposed of;

853.text: (2) if the defendant has satisfied the whole claim (including any claim for costs) on which the claimant is seeking judgment;

854.text: (3) if the claimant is seeking judgment on a claim for money and the defendant has filed or served on the claimant an admission under Rule 15.14 or 15.24 (admission of liability to pay all of the money claimed) together with a request for time to pay;

855.text: (4) unless he has either filed a certificate of service under Rule 9.43 or the Defendant has filed an acknowledgment of service.

857.text: 13.7 A claimant may obtain a default judgment by filing a request in Form P13/01 or Form P13/02.

860.text: (1) in respect of a claim for a specified amount of money or for the delivery of goods where the defendant will be given the alternative of paying a specified sum representing their value, or for fixed costs only, must be in Form P13/01,

861.text: (2) in respect of a claim where an amount of money (including an amount representing the value of goods) is to be decided by the Court, must be in Form P13/02 and

862.text: (3) in every other case, must be in Form P13/02 amended as necessary to describe the judgment requested.

863.text: Nature Of Judgment Where Default Judgment Obtained By Filing A Request

865.text: Where the claim is for a specified sum of money, the claimant may specify in a request filed under Rule 13.7—

866.text: (1) the date by which the whole of the judgment debt is to be paid; or (2) the times and rate at which it is to be paid by instalments.

868.text: Except where Rule 13.12 applies, a default judgment on a claim for a specified amount of money obtained on the filing of a request, will be judgment for the amount of the claim (less any payments made) and costs—

869.text: (1) to be paid by the date or at the rate specified in the request for judgment; or

872.text: Where the claim is for an unspecified amount of money a default judgment obtained on the filing of a request will be for an amount to be decided by the Court and costs.

874.text: Where the claim is for delivery of goods and the claim form gives the defendant the alternative of paying their value, a default judgment obtained on the filing of a request will be judgment requiring the defendant to—

875.text: (1) deliver the goods or (if he does not do so) pay the value of the goods as decided by the Court (less any payments made); and

878.text: The claimant’s right to enter judgment requiring the defendant to deliver goods is subject to Rule 36.50 (judgment in favour of certain part owners relating to the detention of goods).

881.text: A default judgment on a claim for a specified amount of money obtained on the filing of a request may include the amount of interest claimed to the date of judgment if—

882.text:
(1) the claim form includes the details required by Rule 17.18;   
(2) where interest is claimed under (a) Articles 118 or 119 of the Contract Law 2004; (b) Articles 17, 18 or 32 of the Law of Damages and Remedies 2005; (c) Article 121(b) of the Law of Obligations 2005; (d) Article 80 of the DIFC Employment Law 2005; the rate is no higher than the rate of interest fixed by these Rules under Article 39(2)(a) of the Court Law payable on judgment debts at the date when the claim form was issued; and

883.text: (3) the claimant’s request for judgment includes a calculation of the interest claimed for the period from the date up to which interest was stated to be calculated in the claim form to the date of the request for judgment.

885.text: In any case where Rule 13.14 does not apply, judgment will be for an amount of interest to be decided by the Court.

886.text: Procedure For Deciding An Amount Or Value

888.text: Where the claimant obtains a default judgment on the filing of a request and judgment is for—

889.text: (1) an amount of money to be decided by the Court; (2) the value of goods to be decided by the Court; or (3) an amount of interest to be decided by the Court when the Court enters judgment it will give any directions it considers appropriate.

890.text: Claim Against More Than One Defendant

892.text: A claimant may obtain a default judgment on request under this Part against one of two or more defendants, and proceed with his claim against the other defendants.

894.text: Where a claimant applies for a default judgment against one of two or more defendants —

895.text: (1) if the claim can be dealt with separately from the claim against the other defendants

896.text: (a) the Court may enter a default judgment against that defendant; and

897.text: (b) the claimant may continue the proceedings against the other defendants;

898.text: (2) if the claim cannot be dealt with separately from the claim against the other defendants

899.text: (a) the Court will not enter default judgment against that defendant; and

900.text: (b) the Court must deal with the application at the same time as it disposes of the claim against the other defendants.

902.text: A claimant may not enforce against one of two or more defendants any judgment obtained under this Part for delivery of goods unless—

903.text: (1) he has obtained a judgment for delivery (whether or not obtained under this Part) against all the defendants to the claim; or

905.text: Procedure For Obtaining A Default Judgment For Costs Only

907.text: Where a claimant wishes to obtain a default judgment for costs only—

908.text: (1) if the claim is for fixed costs, he may obtain it by filing a request in the relevant practice form;

909.text: (2) if the claim is for any other type of costs, he must make an application in accordance with Part 23.

911.text: Where an application is made under Rule 13.20 for costs only, judgment shall be for an amount to be decided by the Court.

914.text: On a request for default judgment the Court must be satisfied that:

915.text: (1) the claim form has been served on the defendant (a certificate of service on the Court file will be sufficient evidence);

916.text: (2) either the defendant has not filed an acknowledgment of service or has not filed a defence and that in either case the relevant period for doing so has expired; (

917.text: 3) the defendant has not satisfied the claim; and

918.text: (4) the defendant has not returned an admission to the claimant under Rule 15.14 or filed an admission with the Court under Rule 15.24.

920.text: On a request where the defendant was served with the claim outside the jurisdiction and the defendant has not acknowledged service, the evidence must establish that:

921.text: (1) the claim is one that the Court has power to hear and decide, (2) no other court has exclusive jurisdiction to hear and decide the claim, and (3) the claim has been properly served.

923.text: Evidence referred to in Rule 13.23 above must be by affidavit.
