3782.text: A party shall be deemed to admit the authenticity of a document produced to him under Part 28 (production of documents) unless he serves notice that he wishes the document to be proved at trial.

3784.text: A notice to prove a document must be served:

3785.text: (1) by the latest date for serving witness statements; or (2) within 7 days of production of the document, whichever is later.

3788.text: A notarial act or instrument may be received in evidence without further proof as duly authenticated in accordance with the requirements of law unless the contrary is proved.

3791.text: A statement of case may be used as evidence in an interim application provided it is verified by a statement of truth.

3793.text: To verify a statement of case the statement of truth should be set out as follows:

3794.text: “[I believe][the (party on whose behalf the statement of case is being signed) believes] that the facts stated in the statement of case are true”.

3796.text: Attention is drawn to Section VI of this Part which sets out the consequences of verifying a witness statement containing a false statement without an honest belief in its truth.

3799.text: The Court may give directions requiring the parties to use their best endeavours to agree a bundle or bundles of documents for use at any hearing.

3801.text: All documents contained in bundles which have been agreed for use at a hearing shall be admissible at that hearing as evidence of their contents, unless:

3802.text: (1) the Court orders otherwise; or (2) a party gives written notice of objection to the admissibility of particular documents.

3803.text: Use Of Plans, Photographs And Models As Evidence

3805.text: Rules 29.124 to 29.130 apply to evidence (such as a plan, photograph or model) which is not:

3806.text: (1) contained in a witness statement, affidavit or expert’s report; (2) to be given orally at trial; or (3) evidence of which prior notice must be given under Rules 29.102 to 29.105.

3808.text: The evidence referred to in Rule 29.123 includes documents which may be received in evidence without further proof being documents shown to form part of the records of a business or public authority.

3810.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise the evidence shall not be receivable at a trial unless the party intending to put it in evidence has given notice to the other parties in accordance with Rules 29.126 to 29.130.

3812.text: Where the party intends to use the evidence as evidence of any fact then, except where Rule 29.128 applies, he must give notice not later than the latest date for serving witness statements.

3814.text: He must give notice at least 21 days before the hearing at which he proposes to put in the evidence, if:

3815.text:
(1) there are not to be witness statements; or   
(2) he intends to put in the evidence solely in order to disprove an allegation made in a witness statement.

3817.text: Where the evidence forms part of expert evidence, he must give notice when the expert’s report is served on the other party.

3819.text: Where the evidence is being produced to the Court for any reason other than as part of factual or expert evidence, he must give notice at least 21 days before the hearing at which he proposes to put in the evidence.

3821.text: Where a party has given notice that he intends to put in the evidence, he must give every other party an opportunity to inspect it and to agree to its admission without further proof.

3822.text: Evidence Of Finding On Question Of Non-Difc Law

3824.text: Rules 29.133 to 29.135 set out the procedure which must be followed by a party who intends to put in evidence a finding on a question of non-DIFC law.

3826.text: “Non-DIFC law” means any law other than—

3827.text: (1) DIFC law or any other law in force in the DIFC ;

3828.text: (2) the law of any jurisdiction other than that of the DIFC expressly chosen by any DIFC Law;

3829.text: (3) the laws of a jurisdiction as agreed between all the relevant persons concerned in the matter;

3830.text: (4) the laws of any jurisdiction which appear to the Court to be the one most closely related to the facts of and the persons concerned in the matter; and

3831.text: (5) the laws of England and Wales.

3833.text: He must give any other party notice of his intention.

3836.text: (1) if there are to be witness statements, not later than the latest date for serving them; or (2) otherwise, not less than 21 days before the hearing at which he proposes to put the finding in evidence.

3839.text: (1) specify the question on which the finding was made; and (2) enclose a copy of a document where it is reported or recorded.

3840.text: Evidence Of Consent Of Trustee To Act

3842.text: A document purporting to contain the written consent of a person to act as trustee and to bear his signature verified by some other person is evidence of such consent.

3845.text: Proceedings for contempt of court may be brought against a person if he makes, or causes to be made, a false statement in a document verified by a statement of truth without an honest belief in its truth.

3847.text: Proceedings under Rule 29.137 may be brought only with the permission of the Court .

3850.text: Where a party alleges that a statement of truth or a Disclosure Statement is false the party shall refer that allegation to the Court dealing with the claim in which the statement of truth or disclosure statement has been made.

3853.text: (1) exercise any of its powers under these Rules ;

3854.text: (2) initiate steps to consider if there is a contempt of court and, where there is, to punish it; and/or

3855.text: (3) refer the matter to the Attorney General of Dubai.

3857.text: Where a party makes an application to the Court for permission for that party to commence proceedings for contempt of court, it must be supported by written evidence which:

3858.text: (1) identifies the statement said to be false;

3861.text: (b) the basis on which it is contended the maker knew it to be false at the time he made it; and

3862.text: (c) explains why contempt proceedings would be appropriate in the light of the overriding objective in Part 1 of these Rules .

3868.text: This Section of this Part provides:

3869.text: (1) for the circumstances in which a person may be required to attend Court or a tribunal to give evidence or to produce a document; and

3870.text: (2) for a party to obtain evidence before a hearing to be used at the hearing.

3872.text: In this Section, reference to a hearing includes a reference to the trial.

3875.text: A witness summons is a document issued by the Court requiring a witness to:

3876.text: (1) attend the Court or a tribunal to give evidence; or (2) produce documents to the Court or a tribunal .

3878.text: A witness summons must be in Form P30/01.

3879.text: 30.5 There must be a separate witness summons for each witness.

3881.text: A witness summons may require a witness to produce documents to the Court or a tribunal either:

3882.text: (1) on the date fixed for a hearing; or (2) on such date as the Court may direct.

3883.text: PART 30 Witnesses, Depositions And Evidence For Other Courts

3885.text: The only documents that a summons under Rule 30.3(2) can require a person to produce before a hearing are documents which that person could be required to produce at the hearing.

3888.text: A witness summons is issued on the date entered on the summons by the Court .

3890.text: Two copies of the witness summons should be filed with the Court for sealing, one of which will be retained on the Court file.

3892.text: A mistake in the name or address of a person named in a witness summons may be corrected if the summons has not been served.

3894.text: The corrected summons must be re-sealed by the Court and marked ‘Amended and Re-Sealed’.

3896.text: A party must obtain permission from the Court where he wishes to:

3899.text: In Rule 30.12(3) ‘trial’ includes a hearing before a tribunal which is intended to dispose of the matter before it.

3901.text: The Court may set aside or vary a witness summons issued under this Rule 30.8.

3902.text: Witness Summons In Aid Of Tribunal

3904.text: The Court may issue a witness summons in aid of a tribunal .

3906.text: The Court may set aside a witness summons issued under Rule 30.15.

3908.text: Unless the Court otherwise directs, the applicant must give at least 2 days’ notice of the application to the party who issued the witness summons , which will normally be dealt with at a hearing.

3910.text: In Rule 30.15, ‘tribunal’ means any tribunal that does not have power to issue a witness summons in relation to proceedings before it.

3911.text: Time For Serving A Witness Summons

3913.text: The general rule is that a witness summons is binding if it is served at least 7 days before the date on which the witness is required to attend before the Court or tribunal .

3915.text: The Court may direct that a witness summons shall be binding even though it will be served less than 7 days before the date on which the witness is required to attend before the Court or tribunal .

3918.text: (1) served in accordance with this Rule 30.19 or 30.20; and

3919.text: (2) requires the witness to attend before the Court or a tribunal to give evidence;

3920.text: is binding until the conclusion of the hearing at which the attendance of the witness is required.

3921.text: Who Is To Serve A Witness Summons

3923.text: A witness summons is to be served by the Court unless the party on whose behalf it is issued indicates in writing, when he asks the Court to issue the summons, that he wishes to serve it himself.

3925.text: Where the Court is to serve the witness summons , the party on whose behalf it is issued must deposit, in the Court office, the money to be paid or offered to the witness under Rule 30.24.

3926.text: Right Of Witness To Travelling Expenses And Compensation For Loss Of Time

3928.text: At the time of service of a witness summons the witness must be offered or paid:

3929.text: (1) a sum reasonably sufficient to cover his expenses in travelling to and from the Court; and (2) such sum by way of compensation for loss of time as may be specified in the relevant Practice Direction.

3932.text: A party may apply for an order for a person to be examined before the hearing takes place.

3934.text: A person from whom evidence is to be obtained following an order under Rule 30.25 is referred to as a ‘deponent’ and the evidence is referred to as a ‘deposition’.

3936.text: An order under Rule 30.25 shall be for a deponent to be examined on oath before:

3937.text: (1) a Judge ; (2) an examiner of the Court ; or (3) such other person as the Court appoints.

3939.text: The order may require the production of any document which the Court considers is necessary for the purposes of the examination.

3941.text: The order must state the date, time and place of the examination.

3943.text: At the time of service of the order the deponent must be offered or paid:

3944.text: (1) a sum reasonably sufficient to cover his expenses in travelling to and from the place of examination; and

3945.text: (2) such sum by way of compensation for loss of time as may be specified in the relevant Practice Direction.

3947.text: Where the Court makes an order for a deposition to be taken, it may also order the party who obtained the order to serve a witness statement or witness summary in relation to the evidence to be given by the person to be examined.

3950.text: Subject to any directions contained in the order for examination, the examination must be conducted in the same way as if the witness were giving evidence at a trial.

3952.text: If all the parties are present, the examiner may conduct the examination of a person not named in the order for examination if all the parties and the person to be examined consent.

3954.text: The examiner may conduct the examination in private if he considers it appropriate to do so.

3956.text: The examiner must ensure that the evidence given by the witness is recorded in full:

3957.text: (1) In ensuring that the deponent’s evidence is recorded in full, the Court or the examiner may permit it to be recorded on audiotape or videotape, but the deposition must always be recorded in writing by him or by a competent shorthand writer or stenographer.

3958.text: (2) If the deposition is not recorded word for word, it must contain, as nearly as may be, the statement of the deponent; the examiner may record word for word any particular questions and answers which appear to him to have special importance.

3960.text: If a deponent objects to answering any question or where any objection is taken to any question, the examiner must:

3961.text: (1) record in the deposition or a document attached to it:

3962.text:
(a) the question;   
(b) the nature of and grounds for the objection; and (c) any answer given; and

3963.text: (2) give his opinion as to the validity of the objection and must record it in the deposition or a document attached to it.

3965.text: The Court will decide as to the validity of the objection and any question of costs arising from it.

3968.text: (1) have an identifying number or letter marked on them by the examiner; and (2) be preserved by the party or his legal representative who obtained the order for the examination, or as the Court or the examiner may direct.

3970.text: The examiner may put any question to the deponent as to:

3971.text: (1) the meaning of any of his answers; or (2) any matter arising in the course of the examination.

3976.text: The examiner must send a copy of the deposition:

3977.text: (1) to the person who obtained the order for the examination of the witness; and (2) to the Court .

3979.text: The party who obtained the order must send each of the other parties a copy of the deposition which he receives from the examiner.

3981.text: 30.43 If a person served with an order to attend before an examiner:

3983.text: (2) refuses to be sworn for the purpose of the examination or to answer any lawful question or produce any document at the examination;

3984.text: a certificate of his failure or refusal, signed by the examiner, must be filed by the party requiring the deposition. The examiner may include in his certificate any comment as to the conduct of the deponent or of any person attending the examination.

3986.text: On the certificate being filed, the party requiring the deposition may apply to the Court for an order requiring that person to attend or to be sworn or to answer any question or produce any document, as the case may be.

3988.text: An application for an order under Rule 30.44 may be made without notice.

3990.text: The Court will make such order on the application under Rule 30.44 as it thinks fit including an order for the deponent to pay any costs resulting from his failure or refusal.

3992.text: A deponent who wilfully refuses to obey an order made against him under this Section of this Part may be proceeded against for contempt of court.

3993.text: Use Of Deposition At A Hearing

3995.text: A deposition ordered under Rule 30.25 may be given in evidence at a hearing unless the Court orders otherwise.

3997.text: A party intending to put in evidence a deposition at a hearing must serve notice of his intention to do so on every other party.

3999.text: He must serve the notice at least 21 days before the day fixed for the hearing.

4001.text: The Court may require a deponent to attend the hearing and give evidence orally.

4003.text: Where a deposition is given in evidence at trial, it shall be treated as if it were a witness statement for the purposes of Rule 29.61 (availability of witness statements for inspection).

4004.text: Restrictions On Subsequent Use Of Deposition Taken For The Purpose Of Any Hearing Except The Trial

4006.text: Where the Court orders a party to be examined about his or any other assets for the purpose of any hearing except the trial, the deposition may be used only for the purpose of the proceedings in which the order was made.

4008.text: However, it may be used for some other purpose:

4009.text: (1) by the party who was examined; (2) if the party who was examined agrees; or (3) if the Court gives permission.

4011.text: Where a party wishes to take a deposition from a person who is out of the DIFC, they should make an application by application notice in accordance with Part 23, accompanied by supporting evidence. The Registry will proceed to give directions as to the requisite procedure to be followed, depending on the jurisdiction in which the taking of evidence is being sought. Parties are put on notice that the taking of evidence abroad may not be feasible in all jurisdictions.

4012.text: Fees And Expenses Of Examiner Of The Court

4014.text: An examiner of the Court may charge a fee for the examination.

4016.text: He need not send the deposition to the Court unless the fee is paid.

4018.text: The examiner’s fees and expenses must be paid by the party who obtained the order for examination.

4020.text: If the fees and expenses due to an examiner are not paid within a reasonable time, he may report that fact to the Court .

4022.text: The Court may order the party who obtained the order for examination to deposit in the Court office a specified sum in respect of the examiner’s fees and, where it does so, the examiner will not be asked to act until the sum has been deposited.

4024.text: An order under this Rule 30.60 does not affect any decision as to the party who is ultimately to bear the costs of the examination.

4027.text: The Chief Justice shall appoint persons to be examiners of the Court .

4029.text: The persons appointed shall be practitioners with rights of audience before the Court who have been practising for a period of not less than three years.

4030.text: The Chief Justice may revoke an appointment at any time.

4034.text: Where an application is made to the Court for an order for evidence to be obtained in the DIFC and the Court is satisfied:

4035.text: (1) that the application is made in pursuance of a request issued by or on behalf of a court or tribunal (“the requesting court”) exercising jurisdiction in:

4037.text: (b) in any other part of the UAE ;

4038.text: (c) or in a country or territory outside the UAE ; and

4039.text: (2) that the evidence to which the application relates is to be obtained for the purposes of proceedings which either have been instituted before the requesting court or whose institution before that court is contemplated;

4040.text: the Court shall have the powers conferred on it by the following provisions of this Section of this Part.

4042.text: Power Of The Court To Give Effect To Application For Assistance

4044.text: The Court shall have the power, on an application under Rule 30.65, by order to make such provision for obtaining evidence in the DIFC as may appear to the Court to be appropriate for the purpose of giving effect to the request in pursuance of which the application is made; and any such order may require a person specified therein to take such steps as the Court may consider appropriate for that purpose.

4046.text: Without prejudice to the generality of Rule 30.66 but subject to the provisions of this Rule, an order under Rule 30.66 may, in particular, make provision:

4049.text: An order under Rule 30.66 shall not require any particular steps to be taken unless they are steps which can be required to be taken by way of obtaining evidence for the purposes of civil proceedings in the Court (whether or not proceedings of the same description as those to which the application for the order relates); but this Rule shall not preclude the making of an order requiring a person to give testimony (either orally or in writing) otherwise than on oath where this is asked for by the requesting court.

4051.text: An order under Rule 30.66 shall not require a person:

4052.text: (1) to state what documents relevant to the proceedings to which the application for the order relates are or have been in his possession, custody or power; or

4053.text: (2) to produce any documents other than particular documents specified in the order as being documents appearing to the Court making the order to be, or likely to be, in his possession, custody or power.

4055.text: A person who, by virtue of an order under Rule 30.66, is required to attend at any place shall be entitled to the like conduct money and payment for expenses and loss of time as on attendance as a witness in

4058.text: A person shall not be compelled by virtue of an order under Rule 30.66 to give any evidence which he could not be compelled to give:

4059.text:
(1) in civil proceedings in the DIFC ; or   
(2) subject to Rule 30.72, in civil proceedings in the country or territory in which the requesting court exercises jurisdiction .

4061.text: Rule 30.71(2) shall not apply unless the claim of the person in question to be exempt from giving the evidence is either:

4062.text: (1) supported by a statement contained in the request (whether it is so supported unconditionally or subject to conditions that are fulfilled); or

4063.text: (2) conceded by the applicant for the order;

4064.text: and where such a claim made by any person is not supported or conceded as aforesaid he may (subject to the other provisions of this Section of this Part) be required to give the evidence to which the claim relates but that evidence shall not be transmitted to the requesting court if that court, on the matter being referred to it, upholds the claim.

4066.text: Without prejudice to 30.71, a person shall not be compelled by virtue of an order under Rule 30.66 to give any evidence if his doing so would be prejudicial to the security of the DIFC , Dubai or UAE; and a certificate signed by or on behalf of a responsible authority to the effect that it would be so prejudicial for that person to do so shall be conclusive evidence of that fact.

4068.text: In this Section of this Part references to giving evidence include references to answering any question and to producing any document and the reference in Rule 30.72 to the transmission of evidence given by a person shall be construed accordingly.

4071.text: An application for an order under Rule 30.66 for evidence to be obtained:

4073.text: (a) supported by written evidence. The written evidence supporting an application must include or

4076.text: (i) a statement of the issues relevant to the proceedings;

4077.text: (ii) a list of questions or the subject matter of questions to be put to the proposed deponent;

4079.text: (iv) a translation of the documents in (i) and (ii) into English, if necessary; and

4080.text: (b) accompanied by the request as a result of which the application is made, and where appropriate, a translation of the request into English; and

4084.text: The order for the deponent to attend and be examined together with the evidence upon which the order was made must be served on the deponent.

4086.text: Attention is drawn to the provisions of Rule 23.94 (application to vary or discharge an order made without notice).

4089.text: The Court may order an examination to be taken before:

4090.text:
(1) any fit and proper person nominated by the person applying for the order; (2) an examiner of the Court ; or   
(3) any other person whom the Court considers suitable.

4093.text: (1) the examination will be taken as provided by Rules 30.32 to 30.42; and (2) Rules 30.43 to 30.47 apply.

4095.text: The Court may make an order under Rule 30.60 for payment of the fees and expenses of the examination.

4098.text: The examiner must send the deposition of the witness to the Registrar unless the Court orders otherwise.

4101.text: (1) give a certificate sealed with the seal of the Court for use out of the DIFC identifying the following documents:

4102.text: (a) the request; (b) the order of the Court for examination; and (c) the deposition of the witness; and

4103.text: (2) send the certificate and the documents referred to in sub-paragraph (1) to the requesting court.

4107.text: (1) a witness claims to be exempt from giving evidence on the ground specified in Rule 30.79(2); and (2) that claim is not supported or conceded as referred to in Rule 30.80.

4109.text: The examiner may require the witness to give the evidence which he claims to be exempt from giving.

4111.text: Where the examiner does not require the witness to give that evidence, the Court may order the witness to do so.

4113.text: An application for an order under Rule 30.85 may be made by the person who obtained the order under Rule 30.66.

4116.text: (1) it must be contained in a document separate from the remainder of the deposition;

4117.text: (2) the examiner will send to the Registrar :

4118.text: (a) the deposition; and (b) a signed statement setting out the claim to be exempt and the ground on which it was made.

4120.text: On receipt of the statement referred to Rule 30.87(2)(b), the Registrar will:

4121.text: (1) retain the document containing the part of the witness’s evidence to which the claim to be exempt relates; and

4122.text: (2) send the statement and a request to determine that claim to the requesting court or tribunal together with the documents referred to in Rule 30.75.

4125.text: (1) if the claim to be exempt is rejected by the requesting court or tribunal , send the document referred to in Rule 30.87(1) to that court or tribunal ;

4126.text: (2) if the claim is upheld, send the document to the witness; and

4127.text: (3) in either case, notify the witness and person who obtained the order under Rule 30.66 of the requesting court or tribunal’s decision.

4132.text:
31.1   
A reference to an ‘expert’ in this Section of this Part is a reference to an expert who has been instructed to give or prepare evidence for the purpose of court proceedings.

4134.text: Parties are referred to the English “Protocol for the Instruction of Experts to give evidence in civil claims” which is reproduced at Schedule A to this Part as amended from time to time. Not all parts of the Protocol are applicable to litigation before the DIFC Courts but the Protocol provides valuable practical guidance

4135.text: both to those who instruct experts and experts themselves as to what they are expected to do in civil and commercial proceedings before the Court . The Court will expect legal practitioners and experts to have read the Protocol.

4136.text: Experts — Overriding Duty To The Court

4137.text: 31.3 It is the duty of an expert to help the Court on the matters within his expertise.

4138.text:
31.4   
This duty overrides any obligation to the person from whom he has received instructions or by whom he is paid.

4140.text: Expert evidence should be the independent product of the expert uninfluenced by the pressures of litigation.

4142.text: An expert should assist the Court by providing objective, unbiased opinion on matters within his expertise, and should not assume the role of an advocate.

4143.text: 31.7 An expert should consider all material facts, including those which might detract from his opinion.

4145.text: An expert should make it clear:

4146.text: (1) when a question or issue falls outside his expertise; and (2) when he is not able to reach a definite opinion, for example because he has insufficient information.

4148.text: If an expert’s opinion is not properly researched because he considers that insufficient data is available, this must be stated in his report with an indication that the opinion is no more than a provisional one.

4150.text: In a case where an expert witness who has prepared a report is unable to confirm that the report contains the truth, the whole truth and nothing but the truth without some qualification, that qualification must be stated in the report.

4152.text: If, after producing a report, an expert changes his view on any material matter, such change of view should

4153.text: be communicated to all the parties without delay, and when appropriate to the Court (through the legal representatives of the party instructing the expert or directly to the Court in the case of a Court-appointed expert).

4154.text: Court’S Power To Restrict Expert Evidence

4155.text: 31.12 Expert evidence shall be restricted to that which is reasonably required to resolve the proceedings.

4156.text: 31.13 No party may call an expert or put in evidence an expert’s report without the Court’s permission.

4158.text: Any application for permission to call an expert witness or serve an expert’s report should normally be made at the Case Management Conference.

4160.text: When a party applies for permission under Rule 31.13 he must identify:

4161.text: (1) the field in which he wishes to rely on expert evidence; and (2) where practicable the expert in that field on whose evidence he wishes to rely.

4163.text: If permission is granted under Rule 31.13 shall be in relation only to the expert named or the field identified under Rule 31.15.

4165.text: In appropriate cases the Court will direct that the reports of expert witnesses be exchanged sequentially rather than simultaneously. This is an issue that the Court will normally wish to consider at the Case Management Conference.

4167.text: The Court may limit the amount of the expert’s fees and expenses that the party who wishes to rely on the expert may recover from any other party.

4170.text: Where an order requires an act to be done by an expert, or otherwise affects an expert, the party instructing that expert must serve a copy of the order on the expert instructed by him.

4171.text: General Requirement For Expert Evidence To Be Given In A Written Report

4173.text: Expert evidence is to be given in a written report unless the Court directs otherwise.

4174.text: Documents Referred To In Experts’ Reports

4176.text: Unless they have already been provided, copies of any photographs, plans, analyses, measurements, survey reports or other similar documents relied on by an expert witness as well as copies of any unpublished sources must be provided to all parties at the same time as his report.

4178.text: Rules 28.7 to 28.9 provide that (subject to Rule 31.53) a party may inspect a document mentioned in an expert’s report. An expert’s report will frequently list all or many of the relevant previous papers (published or unpublished) or books written by the expert or to which the expert has contributed. Requiring inspection of this material may often be unrealistic, and the collating and copying burden could be considerable.

4180.text: A party wishing to inspect a document in an expert report should (failing agreement) make an application to the Court . The Court will not permit inspection unless it is satisfied that it is necessary for the just disposal of the case and that the document is not reasonably available to the party making the application from an alternative source.

4184.text: (1) an expert instructed by another party; or (2) a Court-appointed expert appointed under Rule 31.29; written questions about his report.

4187.text: (1) may be put once only; (2) must be put within 28 days of service of the expert’s report; and (3) must be for the purpose only of clarification of the report; unless:

4188.text: (a) the Court gives permission; or (b) the party who has instructed the expert agrees.

4190.text: In cases where questions that are oppressive in number or content are put, or questions are put for any purpose other than clarification of the report, the Court will disallow the questions and make an appropriate order for costs against the party putting them.

4192.text: An expert’s answers to questions put in accordance with Rule 31.24 shall be treated as part of the expert’s report.

4195.text: (1) a party has put a written question to an expert instructed by another party in accordance with Rule 31.24; and

4196.text: (2) the expert does not answer that question;

4197.text: the Court may make one or both of the following orders in relation to the party who instructed the expert:

4198.text: (a) that the party may not rely on the evidence of that expert; or

4199.text: (b) that the party may not recover the fees and expenses of that expert from any other party.

4202.text: The Court may appoint one or more independent experts to report to it on specific issues designated by the Court .

4204.text: Not less than 21 days before making any such appointment, the Court will notify each party in writing of the name of the proposed expert, of the matter in respect of which the opinion of the expert will be sought and of the qualifications of the expert to give that opinion.

4206.text: The Court shall establish the terms of reference for any Court-appointed expert report. A copy of the final terms of reference shall be sent by the Court to the parties.

4208.text: The Court-appointed expert shall, before accepting appointment, submit to the Court and to the parties a

4209.text: statement of his independence from the parties and the Court . Within 7 days of receipt of the statement the parties shall inform the Court whether they have any objections to the Court-appointed expert’s independence.

4211.text: Any such objection must be made in writing and filed with the Court and will be taken into account by the Court in deciding whether or not to make the appointment.

4213.text: The Court-appointed expert may request a party to provide any relevant and material information or to provide access to any relevant documents, goods, samples, property or site for inspection, examination or experiment.

4215.text: If a party objects to a request made under Rule 31.34 the Court shall, at the request of the Court appointed-expert or the party determine the validity of the objection and may direct the party to comply with the request.

4217.text: The Court may give directions about any inspection, examination or experiments under Rule 31.34 which the Court appointed-expert wishes to carry out.

4219.text: The Court appointed-expert shall record in his report any non-compliance by a party with a request under Rule 31.34 or direction by the Court and shall describe its effects on the determination of the specific issue.

4221.text: A party shall have the right to receive any information and to attend any inspection provided in compliance with a request made under Rule 31.34.

4223.text: A party may examine any document that the Court-appointed expert has examined and any correspondence between the Court and the Court-appointed expert.

4225.text: The Court-appointed expert shall report in writing to the Court . The Court-appointed expert shall describe in the report the method, evidence and information used in arriving at the conclusions.

4227.text: The Court shall send a copy of such expert report to the parties.

4229.text: Within 28 days of receipt of the Court-appointed expert’s report, any party may respond to the report in a submission by the party or through an expert report. The party must serve the submission or expert report on the Court , the Court-appointed expert and the other parties.

4231.text: Any party may use a Court-appointed expert’s report as evidence at the trial.

4233.text: The Court may direct that the Court-appointed expert attend any hearing. The Court may question the Court-appointed expert, and he may be questioned by the parties.

4235.text: The Court may give any expert report made by a Court-appointed expert and its conclusions such weight as it considers appropriate.

4237.text: The Court may order that any party shall be responsible for part or all of the fees and expenses of a Courtappointed expert, including:

4238.text: (1) directing any party to pay an amount into Court ; and

4239.text: (2) making payment to the expert on account to be revised on final judgment .

4241.text: Unless the Court otherwise directs, the parties are jointly and severally liable for the payment of the Courtappointed expert’s fees and expenses.

4244.text: (1) This Rule does not affect the provisions set out in the rest of this Part.

4245.text: (2) If it considers it appropriate in the circumstances of the case, the Court may give directions that expert witnesses engaged by opposing parties be sworn and present their evidence concurrently as a panel.

4246.text: (3) Where the expert witnesses testify as a panel, unless the Court orders otherwise:

4247.text: (a) each expert may, with the permission of the Court, give a short opening statement not exceeding 10 minutes (or such longer period as may be allowed by the Court) to introduce his report,

4248.text: (b) each expert will thereafter be subject to questioning by the Court and Counsel, and the Court may direct that the expert witnesses may be questioned in any sequence that the Court thinks fit before

4249.text: or after they have testified as a panel.

4250.text: (c) The expert witnesses may also be directed by the Court and Counsel to comment on the views of the other expert witnesses on the panel, and may also, with the permission of the Court, make a short concluding statement not exceeding 10 minutes (or such longer period as may be allowed by the Court).

4251.text: (4) The Court may give any other directions as to the giving of concurrent expert evidence as it thinks fit.

4252.text: Power Of Court To Direct A Party To Provide Information

4254.text: Where a party has access to information which is not reasonably available to the other party, the Court may direct the party who has access to the information to:

4255.text: (1) prepare and file a document recording the information; and

4256.text: (2) serve a copy of that document on the other party.

4258.text: The document served must include sufficient details of all the facts, tests, experiments and assumptions which underlie any part of the information to enable the party on whom it is served to make, or to obtain, a proper interpretation of the information and an assessment of its significance.

4260.text: 31.51 An expert’s report must comply with the requirements set out in Rules 31.52 to 31.56.

4262.text: An expert’s report should be addressed to the Court and not to the party from whom the expert has received his instructions.

4264.text: The expert’s report must state the substance of all material instructions, whether written or oral, on the basis of which the report was written.

4266.text: The instructions referred to in Rule 31.53 shall not be privileged against production but the Court will not, in relation to those instructions:

4267.text: (1) order production of any specific document; or (2) permit any questioning in Court , other than by the party who instructed the expert;

4268.text: unless it is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to consider the statement of instructions given under Rule 31.53 to be inaccurate or incomplete (or unless the party who gave the instructions consents to it). If the Court is so satisfied, it will allow the cross-examination where it appears to be in the interests of justice to do so.

4271.text: (1) give details of the expert’s qualifications;

4272.text: (2) give details of any literature or other material which the expert has relied on in making the report;

4273.text: (3) contain a statement setting out the substance of all facts and instructions given to the expert which are material to the opinions expressed in the report or upon which those opinions are based;

4274.text: (4) state clearly if a stated assumption is, in the opinion of the expert witness, unreasonable or unlikely;

4275.text: (5) make clear which of the facts stated in the report are within the expert’s own direct knowledge;

4276.text: (6) say who carried out any examination, measurement, test or experiment which the expert has used for the report, give the qualifications of that person, and say whether or not the test or experiment has been carried out under the expert’s supervision;

4277.text: (7) where there is a range of opinion on the matters dealt with in the report:

4278.text: (a) summarise the range of opinion; and

4279.text: (b) give reasons for his own opinion;

4280.text: (8) contain a summary of the conclusions reached;

4281.text: (9) if the expert is not able to give his opinion without qualification, state the qualification; and

4282.text: (10) contain a statement that the expert understands his duty to the Court , and has complied and will continue to comply with that duty.

4284.text: An expert’s report must be verified by a statement of truth as well as containing the statements required in Rules 31.55(9) and 31.56(10) above. The form of the statement of truth is as follows:

4285.text: “I confirm that insofar as the facts stated in my report are within my own knowledge I have made clear which they are and I believe them to be true, and that the opinions I have expressed represent my true and complete professional opinion.”

4286.text: (Attention is drawn to Section VI of Part 29 which sets out the consequences of verifying a document containing a false statement without an honest belief in its truth).

4287.text: Use By One Party Of Expert’S Report Produced By Another

4289.text: Where a party has produced an expert’s report, any party may use that expert’s report as evidence at the trial.

4292.text: The Court will normally direct a meeting or meetings of expert witnesses (including any Court-appointed expert) before trial. Sometimes it may be useful for there to be further meetings during the trial itself. The Court may, at any stage, direct a discussion between experts for the purpose of requiring the experts to:

4293.text: (1) identify and discuss the expert issues in the proceedings; and

4294.text: (2) decide, with the benefit of that discussion, on which expert issues they share or can come to share the same expert opinion and on which expert issues there remains a difference of expert opinion between them (and what that difference is).

4296.text: The Court may specify the issues which the experts must discuss.

4298.text: Subject to any directions of the Court , the procedure to be adopted at a meeting of experts is a matter for the experts themselves, not the parties or their legal representatives .

4300.text: Neither the parties nor their legal representatives should seek to restrict the freedom of experts to identify and acknowledge the expert issues on which they agree at, or following further consideration after, meetings of experts.

4302.text: The content of the discussion between the experts shall not be referred to at the trial unless the parties agree.

4304.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise, at or following any meeting, the experts should prepare a joint memorandum for the Court recording:

4305.text:
(1) the fact that they have met and discussed the expert issues; (2) the issues on which they agree;   
(3) the issues on which they disagree; and

4306.text: (4) a brief summary of the reasons for their disagreement.

4308.text: Where experts reach agreement on an issue during their discussions, the agreement shall not bind the parties unless the parties expressly agree to be bound by the agreement.

4309.text: Consequence Of Failure To Produce Expert’S Report

4311.text: A party who fails to produce an expert’s report may not use the report at the trial or call the expert to give evidence orally unless the Court gives permission.

4312.text: Expert’S Right To Ask Court For Directions

4314.text: An expert (including any Court-appointed expert) may file a written request for directions to assist him in carrying out his function as an expert.

4316.text: An expert must, unless the Court orders otherwise, provide a copy of any proposed request for directions under Rule 31.66:

4317.text: (1) (if he is not a Court-appointed expert) to the party instructing him, at least 7 days before he files the request; and

4318.text: (2) to all other parties, at least 4 days before he files it.

4320.text: The Court , when it gives directions, may also direct that a party be served with a copy of the directions.

4323.text: At trial the evidence of expert witnesses is usually taken as a block, after the evidence of witnesses of fact has been given.

4326.text: This Section of this Part applies where the Court appoints one or more persons (an ‘Assessor ‘) under Article 18 of the Court Law .

4328.text: The Assessor shall assist the Court in dealing with a matter in which the Assessor has skill and experience.

4331.text: Not less than 21 days before making any such appointment, the Court will notify each party in writing of the name of the proposed Assessor , of the matter in respect of which the assistance of the Assessor will be sought and of the qualifications of the Assessor to give that assistance.

4333.text: Where any person has been proposed for appointment as an Assessor , objection to him, either personally or in respect of his qualification, may be taken by any party.

4335.text: Any such objection must be made in writing and filed with the Court within 7 days of receipt of the notification referred to in Rule 31.72 and will be taken into account by the Court in deciding whether or not to make the appointment.

4337.text: Prior to commencing duties an Assessor shall take an oath or affirmation of office in accordance with these Rules .

4339.text: An Assessor who has or may have a conflict concerning the subject matter of the proceedings must disclose the matters giving rise to that conflict to the Court and the parties to the proceeding as soon as he becomes aware of the conflict or potential conflict.

4341.text: For the purposes of this Section of this Part, an Assessor has a conflict of interest in relation to proceedings if the Assessor has any interest, pecuniary or otherwise, that could conflict with the proper performance of the Assessor’s functions in relation to the proceedings.

4342.text: Part To Be Taken In Proceedings By Assessor

4344.text: An Assessor shall take such part in the proceedings as the Court may direct and in particular the Court may:

4345.text: (1) direct the Assessor to prepare a report for the Court on any matter at issue in the proceedings; and

4346.text: (2) direct the Assessor to attend the whole or any part of the trial to advise the Court on any such

4349.text: If the Assessor prepares a report for the Court before the trial has begun:

4352.text: The Assessor will not give oral evidence or be open to cross-examination or questioning.

4354.text: The range of topics on which advice might be sought from the Assessor should be canvassed with the parties’ legal representatives by, at the latest, the stage of final submissions.

4356.text: Ordinarily, the questions asked of the Assessor by the Court should not stray outside the range previously discussed with the parties’ legal representatives .

4358.text: The questions ultimately put by the Court , together with the answers given by the Assessor , must be disclosed to the parties’ legal representatives before any draft judgment is handed down.

4360.text: The parties’ legal representatives will thereafter be given the opportunity to make submissions to the Court , as to whether the advice given by the Assessor should be followed. Ordinarily, any such submissions should be in writing; but if there is good reason for doing so, an application may be made for an oral hearing. The Court will consider any such submissions before finalising any judgment .

4362.text: Generally speaking, the interests of proportionality and finality will make it unnecessary to repeat the procedure after the Court and the Assessor have had the opportunity of considering the parties’ submissions and any suggested further or revised questions. Accordingly, unless the Court in its discretion thinks it appropriate to disclose them to the parties’ legal representatives before the judgment is finalised, any further or revised answers will simply be recorded in the judgment , together with the Court’s decision as to whether or not to accept the Assessor’s advice and its reasons for doing so.

4365.text: The Registrar shall pay to any Assessor appointed under this Section of this Part a fee as set out in Schedule B to this Part for carrying out his duties and shall reimburse him for any expenses reasonably incurred.

4367.text: The remuneration to be paid to the Assessor for his services shall be determined by the Court and shall form part of the costs of the proceedings.

4369.text: The Court may order any party to deposit in the Registry a specified sum in respect of the Assessor’s fees and, where it does so, the Assessor will not be asked to act until the sum has been deposited.

4375.text: (1) offers to settle; and (2) the consequences where an offer to settle is made in accordance with Rule 32.4.

4377.text: Nothing in this Part prevents a party making an offer to settle in whatever way he chooses, but if that offer is not made in accordance with Rule 32.4, it will not have the consequences specified in Rules 32.28 to 32.41, and 32.49 to 32.54.

4378.text: Form And Content Of A Part 32 Offer

4379.text: 32.3 An offer to settle which is made in accordance with Rule 32.4 is called a Part 32 offer.

4380.text: Schedule A to Part 31 Protocol for the Instruction of Experts to give evidence in civil claims

4385.text: Rule 32.4(3) does not apply if the offer is made less than 21 days before the start of the trial.

4386.text: 32.6 An offeror may make a Part 32 offer solely in relation to liability.

4393.text: (1) may be made at any time, including before the commencement of proceedings; and (2) may be made in appeal proceedings.

4395.text: A Part 32 offer which offers to pay or offers to accept a sum of money will be treated as inclusive of all

4397.text: (1) the date on which the period stated under Rule 32.4(3) expires; or

4398.text: (2) if Rule 32.5 applies, a date 21 days after the date the offer was made.

4400.text: A Part 32 offer shall have the consequences set out in this Part only in relation to the costs of the proceedings in respect of which it is made, and not in relation to the costs of any appeal from the final decision in those proceedings.

4402.text: Before expiry of the relevant period , a Part 32 offer may be withdrawn or its terms changed to be less advantageous to the offeree , only if the Court gives permission.

4404.text: The permission of the court must be sought:

4405.text: (1) by making an application under Part 23, which must be dealt with by a Judge other than the Judge (if any) allocated in advance to conduct the trial, unless the parties agree that such Judge may hear the application;

4406.text: (2) at a trial or other hearing, provided that it is not to the trial Judge or to the Judge (if any) allocated in advance to conduct the trial, unless the parties agree that such Judge may hear the application.

4408.text: After expiry of the relevant period and provided that the offeree has not previously served notice of acceptance, the offeror may withdraw the offer or change its terms to be less advantageous to the offeree without the permission of the Court .

4410.text: The offeror does so by serving written notice of the withdrawal or change of terms on the offeree .

4413.text: A Part 32 offer by a defendant to pay a sum of money in settlement of a claim must be an offer to pay a single sum of money.

4415.text: An offer that includes an offer to pay all or part of the sum, if accepted, at a date later than 14 days following the date of acceptance will not be treated as a Part 32 offer unless the offeree accepts the offer.

4416.text: Time When A Part 32 Offer Is Made

4418.text: A Part 32 offer is made when it is served on the offeree .

4420.text: A change in the terms of a Part 32 offer will be effective when notice of the change is served on the offeree

4423.text: The offeree may, within 7 days of a Part 32 offer being made, request the offeror to clarify the offer.

4425.text: If the offeror does not give the clarification requested under 32.19 within 7 days of receiving the request, the offeree may, unless the trial has started, apply for an order that he does so (a clarification order).

4427.text: An application for a clarification order should be made in accordance with Part 23.

4429.text: The application notice should state the respects in which the terms of the Part 32 offer are said to need clarification.

4431.text: If the Court makes a clarification order, it must specify the date when the Part 32 offer is to be treated as having been made.

4434.text: A Part 32 offer is accepted by serving written notice of the acceptance on the offeror and filing the notice with the Court .

4436.text: Subject to Rule 32.26, a Part 32 offer may be accepted at any time (whether or not the offeree has subsequently made a different offer) unless the offeror serves notice of withdrawal on the offeree .

4438.text: The Court’s permission is required to accept a Part 32 offer where:

4442.text: Where the Court’s permission is required to accept a Part 32 offer, the permission of the Court must be sought:

4443.text: (1) by making an application under Part 23, which must be dealt with by a Judge other than the Judge (if any) allocated in advance to conduct the trial, unless the parties agree that such Judge may hear the application;

4444.text: (2) at a trial or other hearing, provided that it is not to the trial Judge or to the Judge (if any) allocated in advance to conduct the trial, unless the parties agree that such Judge may hear the application.

4445.text: Costs Consequences Of Acceptance Of A Part 32 Offer

4447.text: Subject to Rules 32.29 and 32.31(1), where a Part 32 offer is accepted within the relevant period , the claimant will be entitled to his costs of the proceedings up to the date on which notice of acceptance was served on the offeror .

4450.text: (1) a defendant’s Part 32 offer relates to only part of the claim; and

4451.text: (2) at the time of serving notice of acceptance within the relevant period the claimant abandons the balance of the claim;

4452.text: the claimant will be entitled to his costs of the proceedings up to the date of serving notice of acceptance unless the Court orders otherwise.

4454.text: Costs under Rules 32.28 and 32.29 will be assessed on the standard basis if the amount of costs is not agreed.

4457.text: (1) a Part 32 offer that was made less than 21 days before the start of trial is accepted; or

4458.text: (2) a Part 32 offer is accepted after expiry of the relevant period ;

4459.text: if the parties do not agree the liability for costs, the Court will make an order as to costs.

4461.text: Where Rule 32.31(2) applies, unless the Court orders otherwise—

4462.text: (1) the claimant will be entitled to his costs of the proceedings up to the date on which the relevant period expired; and

4463.text: (2) the offeree will be liable for the offeror’s costs for the period from the date of expiry of the relevant period to the date of acceptance.

4465.text: The claimant’s costs include any costs incurred in dealing with the defendant’s counterclaim if the Part 32 offer states that it takes into account the counterclaim .

4466.text: The Effect Of Acceptance Of A Part 32 Offer

4468.text: If a Part 32 offer is accepted, the claim will be stayed .

4470.text: In the case of acceptance of a Part 32 offer which relates to the whole claim the stay will be upon the terms of the offer.

4472.text: If a Part 32 offer which relates to only part of the claim is accepted:

4473.text: (1) the claim will be stayed as to that part upon the terms of the offer; and

4474.text: (2) subject to Rule 32.29, unless the parties have agreed costs, the liability for costs shall be decided by the Court .

4476.text: If the approval of the Court is required before a settlement can be binding, any stay which would otherwise arise on the acceptance of a Part 32 offer will take effect only when that approval has been given.

4478.text: Any stay will not affect the power of the Court:

4479.text: (1) to enforce the terms of a Part 32 offer;

4480.text: (2) to deal with any question of costs (including interest on costs) relating to the proceedings.

4482.text: Unless the parties agree otherwise in writing, where a Part 32 offer by a defendant that is or that includes an offer to pay a single sum of money is accepted, that sum must be paid to the offeree within 14 days of

4485.text: If the accepted sum is not paid within 14 days or such other period as has been agreed the offeree may enter judgment for the unpaid sum.

4488.text: (1) a Part 32 offer (or part of a Part 32 offer) which is not an offer to Rule 32.39 applies is accepted; and

4489.text: (2) a party alleges that the other party has not honoured the terms of the offer;

4490.text: that party may apply to enforce the terms of the offer without the need for a new claim.

4491.text: Acceptance Of A Part 32 Offer Made By One Or More, But Not All, Defendants

4493.text: Rules 32.43 to 32.45 apply where the claimant wishes to accept a Part 32 offer made by one or more, but not all, of a number of defendants .

4495.text: If the defendants are sued jointly or in the alternative, the claimant may accept the offer if:

4496.text: (1) he discontinues his claim against those defendants who have not made the offer; and (2) those defendants give written consent to the acceptance of the offer.

4498.text: If the claimant alleges that the defendants have a several liability to him the claimant may:

4499.text: (1) accept the offer; and (2) continue with his claims against the other defendants if he is entitled to do so.

4501.text: In all other cases the claimant must apply to the Court for an order permitting him to accept the Part 32 offer.

4502.text: Restriction On Disclosure Of A Part 32 Offer

4504.text: A Part 32 offer will be treated as ‘without prejudice except as to costs’.

4506.text: The fact that a Part 32 offer has been made must not be communicated to the trial Judge or to the Judge (if any) allocated in advance to conduct the trial until the case has been decided.

4509.text: (1) where the defence of tender before claim has been raised; (2) where the proceedings have been stayed following acceptance of a Part 32 offer; or (3) where the offeror and the offeree agree in writing that it should not apply.

4512.text: Rules 32.50 to 32.54 apply where upon judgment being entered:

4513.text: (1) a claimant fails to obtain a judgment more advantageous than a defendant’s Part 32 offer; or (2) judgment against the defendant is at least as advantageous to the claimant as the proposals contained in a claimant’s Part 32 offer.

4515.text: Subject to Rule 32.54, where Rule 32.49(1) applies, the Court will, unless it considers it unjust to do so, order that the defendant is entitled to:

4516.text: (1) his costs from the date on which the relevant period expired; and

4519.text: Subject to Rule 32.54, where Rule 32.49(2) applies, the Court will, unless it considers it unjust to do so, order that the claimant is entitled to:

4520.text: (1) interest on the whole or part of any sum of money (excluding interest) awarded at a rate not exceeding $10 \%$ above Base Rate for some or all of the period starting with the date on which the relevant period expired;

4521.text: (2) his costs on the indemnity basis from the date on which the relevant period expired; and

4522.text: (3) interest on those costs at a rate not exceeding $10 \%$ above Base Rate .

4524.text: In considering whether it would be unjust to make the orders referred to in Rules 32.50 and 32.51, the Court will take into account all the circumstances of the case including:

4527.text: Where the Court awards interest under this rule and also awards interest on the same sum and for the same period under any other power, the total rate of interest may not exceed $10 \%$ above Base Rate .

4529.text: Rules 32.50 and 32.51 do not apply to a Part 32 offer:

4531.text: Formalities Of Part 32 Offers And Other Notices Under This Part

4533.text: A Part 32 offer may be made using Form P32/01.

4536.text: (1) Part 32 offer; (2) notice of acceptance; (3) notice of withdrawal; or (4) notice of change of terms;

4537.text: is to be served on a party who is legally represented, the document to be served must be served on the legal representative .

4540.text: Money Paid Into Court Under A Court Order

4542.text: A party who makes a payment into Court under a Court order must:

4543.text: (1) serve notice of the payment on every other party; and (2) in relation to each such notice, file a certificate of service .

4544.text: Money Paid Into Court Where Defendant Wishes To Rely On A Defence Of Tender Before Claim

4546.text: Where a defendant wishes to rely on a defence of tender before claim he must make a payment into Court of the amount he says was tendered.

4548.text: If the defendant does not make a payment in accordance with Rule 33.2, the defence of tender before claim will not be available to him until he does so.

4549.text: Payment Into Court Under An Order

4551.text: A party paying money into Court under an order or in support of a defence of tender before claim must:

4552.text: (1) send to the Registry: (a) the payment; (b) a sealed copy of the order or a copy of the defence; and (c) Form P33/01;

4553.text: (2) serve notice of payment on the other parties; and

4555.text: (a) a copy of the notice of payment; and (b) a certificate of service confirming service of the notice on each party served.

4556.text: Application Relating To Funds In Court

4558.text: Any application relating to money or securities which have been paid into Court , other than an application for the payment out of the money or securities (for example, an application for money to be invested, or for payment of interest to any person):

4559.text: (1) must be made in accordance with Part 23; and (2) may be made without notice, but the Court may direct notice to be served on any person.

4560.text: Payment Out Of Money Paid Into Court

4562.text: Money paid into Court under a Court order or in support of a defence of tender before claim may not be paid out without the Court’s permission except where:

4563.text: (1) a Part 32 offer is accepted without needing the permission of the Court ; and (2) the defendant agrees that a sum paid into Court by him should be used to satisfy the offer (in whole or in part).

4565.text: Permission may be obtained by making an application in accordance with Part 23. The application notice must state the grounds on which the order for payment out is sought. Evidence of any facts on which the applicant relies may also be necessary.

4567.text: Where the Court gives permission under Rule 33.6, it will include a direction for the payment out of any money in Court , including any interest accrued.

4569.text: Where permission is not required to take money out of Court , the requesting party should file a request for payment in Form P33/02 with Registry , accompanied by a statement that the defendant agrees that the money should be used to satisfy the Part 32 offer in Form P33/03.

4571.text: The request for payment should contain the following details:

4572.text: (1) where the party receiving the payment is legally represented: (a) the name, business address and reference of the legal representative ; and (b) the name of the bank and the sort code number, the title of the account and the account number where the payment is to be transmitted;

4573.text: (2) where the party is acting in person:

4574.text: (a) his name and address; and (b) his bank account details as in Rule 33.10(1)(b).

4576.text: Where Rule 33.9 applies, interest accruing up to the date of acceptance will be paid to the defendant .

4578.text: Subject Rule 33.13, if a party does not wish the payment to be transmitted into his bank account or if he does not have a bank account, he may send a written request to the Registrar for the payment to be made to him by cheque.

4580.text: Where a party seeking payment out of Court has provided the necessary information, the payment where a party is legally represented must be made to the legal representative .

4584.text: 34.1 The Rules in this Part set out the procedure by which a claimant may discontinue all or part of a claim.

4587.text: (1) claims more than one remedy; and (2) subsequently abandons his claim to one or more of the remedies but continues with his claim for the other remedies; is not treated as discontinuing all or part of a claim for the purposes of this Part.

4590.text: A claimant may discontinue all or part of a claim at any time.

4593.text: (1) a claimant must obtain the permission of the Court if he wishes to discontinue all or part of a claim in relation to which:

4594.text: (a) the Court has granted an interim injunction ; or (b) any party has given an undertaking to the Court ;

4595.text: (2) where the claimant has received an interim payment in relation to a claim, he may discontinue that claim only if:

4596.text: (a) the defendant who made the interim payment consents in writing; or (b) the Court gives permission;

4597.text: (3) where there is more than one claimant , a claimant may not discontinue unless:

4598.text: (a) every other claimant consents in writing; or

4601.text: Where there is more than one defendant , the claimant may discontinue all or part of a claim against all or any of the defendants .

4604.text: To discontinue a claim or part of a claim, a claimant must:

4605.text: (1) file a notice of discontinuance; and (2) serve a copy of it on every other party to the proceedings.

4607.text: The claimant must state in the notice of discontinuance which he files that he has served a notice of discontinuance on every other party to the proceedings.

4609.text: Where the claimant needs the consent of some other party, a copy of the necessary consent must be attached to the notice of discontinuance.

4611.text: Where there is more than one defendant , the notice of discontinuance must specify against which defendants the claim is discontinued.

4612.text: Right To Apply To Have Notice Of Discontinuance Set Aside

4614.text: Where the claimant discontinues under Rule 34.3, the defendant may apply to have the notice of discontinuance set aside .

4616.text: The defendant may not make an application under Rule 34.10 more than 28 days after the date when the notice of discontinuance was served on him.

4617.text: When Discontinuance Takes Effect Where Permission Of The Court Is Not Needed

4619.text: Discontinuance against any defendant takes effect on the date when notice of discontinuance is served on him under Rule 34.6.

4621.text: Subject to Rules 34.10 and 34.11, the proceedings are brought to an end as against him on that date.

4623.text: This does not affect proceedings to deal with any question of costs.

4626.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise, a claimant who discontinues a claim is liable for the defendant’s costs incurred up to and on the date on which notice of the discontinuance was served on him or his legal representative. If proceedings are only partly discontinued:

4627.text:
(1) the claimant is liable for costs relating only to the part of the proceedings which he is discontinuing; and   
(2) unless the Court orders otherwise, the costs which the claimant is liable to pay must not be assessed until the conclusion of the rest of the proceedings.

4630.text: A claimant who discontinues a claim needs the permission of the Court to make another claim against the

4632.text:
(1) he discontinued the claim after the defendant filed a defence; and   
(2) the other claim arises out of facts which are the same or substantially the same as those relating to the discontinued claim.

4633.text: Stay Of Remainder Of Partly Discontinued Proceedings Where Costs Not Paid

4638.text: Where this Rule applies, the Court may stay the remainder of the proceedings until the claimant pays the whole of the costs which he is liable to pay under Rule 34.15.

4639.text: PART 35 Miscellaneous Provisions Relating To Hearings

4643.text: 35.1 In this Part, reference to a hearing includes a reference to the trial.

4644.text: General Rule — Hearings To Be In Public

4645.text: 35.2 The general rule is that a hearing is to be in public.

4647.text: The requirement for a hearing to be in public does not require the Court to make special arrangements for accommodating members of the public.

4649.text: A hearing, or any part of it, may be in private if:

4650.text: (1) publicity would defeat the object of the hearing;

4651.text: (2) it involves matters relating to national security;

4652.text: (3) it involves confidential information (including information relating to personal financial matters) and publicity would damage that confidentiality;

4653.text: (4) a private hearing is necessary to protect the interests of any child or patient, including the approval of a compromise or settlement on behalf of a child or patient or an application for the payment of money out of Court to such a person;

4654.text: (5) it is a hearing of an application made without notice and it would be unjust to any respondent for there to be a public hearing;

4655.text: (6) it involves uncontentious matters arising in the administration of trusts or in the administration of a deceased person’s estate; or

4656.text: (7) the Court considers this to be necessary, in the interests of justice.

4658.text: The Court may order that the identity of any party or witness must not be disclosed if it considers nondisclosure necessary in order to protect the interests of that party or witness.

4660.text: The decision as to whether to hold a hearing in public or in private must be made by the Judge conducting the hearing having regard to any representations which may have been made to him.

4662.text: The hearings set out below shall in the first instance be listed by the Court as hearings in private under Rule 35.4(3), namely:

4663.text: (1) an application to suspend a warrant of execution or a warrant of possession or to stay execution where the Court is being invited to consider the ability of a party to make payments to another party;

4664.text: (2) a determination under Rule 15.41 or a redetermination under Rule 15.44 or an application to vary or suspend the payment of a judgment debt by instalments;

4665.text: (3) an application for a charging order (including an application to enforce a charging order), third party debt order, attachment of earnings order, administration order, or the appointment of a receiver;

4666.text: (4) an order to attend Court for questioning;

4667.text: (5) an application for security for costs under Article 46(1) of the Court Law 2005; and

4668.text: (6) an application by a trustee or personal representative for directions as to bringing or defending legal proceedings.

4670.text: Nothing in this Part prevents a Judge ordering that a hearing taking place in public shall continue in private, or vice-versa.

4672.text: If the Court or Judge’s room in which the proceedings are taking place has a sign on the door indicating that the proceedings are private, members of the public who are not parties to the proceedings will not be admitted unless the Court permits.

4674.text: Where there is no such sign on the door of the Court or Judge’s room, members of the public will be admitted where practicable. The Judge may, if he thinks it appropriate, adjourn the proceedings to a larger room or Court .

4676.text: When a hearing takes place in public, members of the public may obtain a transcript of any judgment given or a copy of any order made, subject to payment of the appropriate fee.

4678.text: When a judgment is given or an order is made in private, if any member of the public who is not a party to the proceedings seeks a transcript of the judgment or a copy of the order, he must seek the permission of the Judge who gave the judgment or made the order.

4680.text: A judgment or order given or made in private, when drawn up, must have clearly marked in the title:

4681.text: ‘Before [title and name of Judge] sitting in Private’

4684.text: The Court may proceed with a trial in the absence of a party but:

4685.text: (1) if no party attends the trial, it may strike out the whole of the proceedings;

4686.text: (2) if the claimant does not attend, it may strike out his claim and any defence to counterclaim . The defendant may prove any counterclaim at trial and obtain judgment on his counterclaim and for costs;

4688.text: (3) if a defendant does not attend, it may strike out his defence or counterclaim (or both). The claimant may prove his claim at trial and obtain judgment on his claim and for costs.

4690.text: Where the Court strikes out proceedings, or any part of them, under Rule 35.14, it may subsequently restore the proceedings, or that part.

4692.text: Where a party does not attend and the Court gives judgment or makes an order against him, the party who failed to attend may apply in accordance with Part 23 for the judgment or order to be set aside .

4694.text: An application under Rule 35.15 or Rule 35.16 must be supported by evidence, giving reasons for the failure to attend Court and stating when the applicant found out about the order against him.

4696.text: Where an application is made under Rule 35.15 or Rule 35.16 by a party who failed to attend the trial, the Court will only grant the application if the applicant:

4697.text: (1) acted promptly when he found out that the Court had exercised its power to strike out or to enter judgment or make an order against him;

4698.text: (2) had a good reason for not attending the trial; and

4699.text: (3) has a reasonable prospect of success at the trial.

4702.text: When the Court sets a timetable for a trial in accordance with Rule 26.75 (fixing or confirming the trial date and giving directions) it will do so in consultation with the parties.

4704.text: Where the trial is fixed for a Sunday, or for a Monday where the Judge is hearing other matters on the Sunday:

4705.text: All documents to be submitted for the trial must be received by the DIFC Courts Registry by no later than 10am on the Wednesday prior to the trial. Skeleton arguments are to be filed with the DIFC Courts by no later than 2pm on the Thursday prior to the trial.

4709.text: It is the claimant’s responsibility to prepare and lodge the agreed trial bundles.

4711.text: If another party wishes to put before the Court a bundle that the claimant regards as unnecessary, he must prepare and lodge it himself.

4713.text: The number, content and organisation of the trial bundles must be approved by the legal representatives with the conduct of the trial.

4715.text: Consideration must always be given to what documents are and are not relevant and necessary. Where the Court is of the opinion that costs have been wasted by the copying of unnecessary documents it will have no hesitation in making a special order for costs against the person responsible.

4717.text: Any party preparing a trial bundle should, as a matter of course, provide all other parties who are to take part in the trial with a copy, at the cost of the receiving party. Further copies should be supplied on request, again at the cost of the receiving party.

4719.text: If oral evidence is to be given at trial, the claimant must provide a clean unmarked set of all relevant trial bundles for use in the witness box. The claimant is responsible for ensuring that these bundles are kept up to date throughout the trial.

4721.text: Failure to comply with the requirements for lodging bundles for the trial may result in the trial not commencing on the date fixed, at the expense of the party in default. An order for immediate payment of costs may be made.

4724.text: The preparation of bundles requires a high level of co-operation between legal representatives for all parties. It is the duty of all legal representatives to cooperate to this high level.

4726.text: When agreeing bundles for trial, the parties should also establish through their legal representatives , and record in correspondence, whether the agreement of bundles:

4727.text: (1) extends no further than agreement of the composition and preparation of the bundles; or (2) includes agreement that the documents in the bundles are authentic (see further Rules 29.115 and 29.116); or (3) includes agreement that the documents may be treated as evidence of the facts stated in them.

4729.text: The Court will expect parties normally to agree that the documents or at any rate the great majority of them may be treated as evidence of the facts stated in them. A party not willing so to agree should, when the trial bundles are lodged, write a letter to the Court (with a copy to all other parties) stating that it is not willing so to agree, and explaining why that is so.

4731.text: The number content and organisation of the trial bundles should be agreed in accordance with the following procedure:

4732.text: (1) the claimant must submit proposals to all other parties at least 6 weeks before the date fixed for trial; and (2) the other parties must submit details of additions they require and any suggestions for revision of the claimant’s proposals to the claimant at least 4 weeks before the date fixed for trial.

4734.text: Where it is not possible to agree the contents of the bundle, a summary of the points on which the parties are unable to agree should be included.

4736.text: Preparation of the trial bundles must be completed not later than 21 days before the date fixed for trial unless the Court orders otherwise.

4738.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise, a full set of the trial bundles must be lodged with the Listing Office at least 14 days before the date fixed for trial.

4741.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise, the trial bundle should include a copy of:

4742.text: (1) the claim form and all statements of case;

4743.text: (2) a case summary and/or chronology where appropriate;

4744.text: (3) requests for further information and responses to the requests;

4745.text: (4) all witness statements to be relied on as evidence;

4747.text: (6) any notices of intention to rely on hearsay evidence under Rule 29.104;

4748.text: (7) any notices of intention to rely on evidence (such as a plan, photograph etc.) under Rule 29.126 which is not:

4749.text: (a) contained in a witness statement, affidavit or experts report;

4752.text: (8) any experts’ reports and responses to them;

4753.text: (9) any order giving directions as to the conduct of the trial;

4754.text: (10) any other documents required by a Court order;

4755.text: (11) any other documents required by any other Rule or Practice Direction; and

4758.text: The originals of the documents contained in the trial bundle, together with copies of any other Court orders should be available at the trial.

4761.text: Bundles should be prepared as follows:

4762.text: (1) No more than one copy of any one document should be included, unless there is good reason for doing otherwise;

4763.text: (2) Contemporaneous documents, and correspondence, should be included in chronological order;

4764.text: (3) Where a contract or similar document is central to the case it may be included in a separate place provided that a page is inserted in the chronological run of documents to indicate:

4765.text: (a) the place the contract or similar document would have appeared had it appeared chronologically; and

4766.text: (b) where it may be found instead;
