4767.text: (4) Documents in manuscript, or not fully legible, should be transcribed; the transcription should be marked and placed adjacent to the document transcribed;

4768.text: (5) Documents that are not in English should be translated; the translation should be marked and placed adjacent to the document transcribed; the translation should be agreed, or, if it cannot be agreed, each party’s proposed translation should be included;

4769.text: (6) If a document has to be read across rather than down the page, it should be so placed in the bundle as to ensure that the top of the text is nearest the spine;

4770.text: (7) No bundle should contain more than 300 pages;

4771.text: (8) Bundles should not be overfilled, and should allow sufficient room for later insertions. Subject to this, the size of file used should not be a size that is larger than necessary for the present and anticipated contents;

4772.text: (9) Bundles should be paginated, in the bottom right hand corner and in a form that can clearly be distinguished from any existing pagination on the document;

4773.text: (10) Bundles should be indexed, save that a chronological bundle of contemporaneous documents need not be indexed if an index is unlikely to be useful;

4774.text: (11) Bundles should be numbered and named on the outside and on the inside front cover, the label to include the short title of the case, and a description of the bundle (including its number, where relevant).

4776.text: Documents within bundles should be marked as follows:

4777.text: (1) When copy documents from exhibits have been included in the bundle(s), then unless clearly unnecessary, the copy of the affidavit or witness statement to which the documents were exhibited should be marked in the right hand margin (in manuscript if need be) to show where the document referred to may be found in the bundle(s).

4778.text: (2) Unless clearly unnecessary, where copy documents in a bundle are taken from the disclosure of more than one party, the documents should be marked in the top right hand corner (in manuscript if need be) to show from which party’s disclosure the copy document has been taken;

4779.text: (3) Where there is a reference in a statement of case or witness statement to a document which is contained in the trial bundles, a note should be made in the margin (if necessary in manuscript) identifying the place where that document is to be found. Unless otherwise agreed this is the responsibility of the party tendering the statement of case or witness statement.

4781.text: For the trial a handy-sized core bundle should normally be provided containing the really important documents in the case. The documents in this bundle should be paginated, but each page should also bear

4782.text: its bundle and page number reference in the main bundles. It is particularly important to allow sufficient room for later insertions (see Rule 35.37(8) above).

4784.text: Large documents, such as plans, should be placed in an easily accessible file.

4785.text: Representation At Trial Of Companies Or Other Corporations

4787.text: At any hearing, a written statement containing the following information should be provided for the Court:

4788.text: (1) the name and address of each of the parties’ legal representatives for trial; (2) his qualification or entitlement to act as a legal representatives for trial; and (3) the party for whom he so acts.

4790.text: A company or other corporation may be represented at trial by an employee who is not otherwise authorised to appear before the Court if:

4791.text: (1) the employee has been authorised by the company or corporation to appear at trial on its behalf; and

4794.text: Where a party is a company or other corporation and is to be represented at a hearing by an employee, the written statement should contain the following additional information:

4795.text: (1) The full name of the company or corporation as stated in its certificate of registration;

4796.text: (2) The registered number of the company or corporation;

4797.text: (3) The position or office in the company or corporation held by the representative; and

4798.text: (4) The date on which and manner in which the representative was authorised to act for the company or corporation, e.g. _ 20____: written authority from managing director; or 20____: Board resolution dated 20_ .

4800.text: Although Rule 35.42 allows a company or other corporation with the permission of the Court to be represented at trial by an employee, the complexity of most cases in the Court will make that unsuitable. Accordingly, permission will be given only in unusual circumstances.

4802.text: Permission under Rule 35.42 should be obtained in advance of the hearing from the Judge who is to hear the case. If it is for any reason impracticable or inconvenient to do so, the permission should be obtained from any Judge by whom the case could be heard.

4804.text: The permission may be obtained informally and without notice to the other parties. The Judge who gives the permission should record in writing that he has done so and supply a copy to the company or corporation in question and to any other party who asks for one.

4806.text: Permission will not normally be granted under Rule 35.42 in contempt proceedings.

4809.text: The use of information technology at trial is encouraged where it is likely substantially to save time and cost or to increase accuracy.

4811.text: If any party considers that it might be advantageous to make use of information technology in preparation for, or at, trial, the matter should be raised at the case management conference. This is particularly important if it is considered that document handling systems would assist disclosure and inspection of documents or the use of documents at trial.

4813.text: Where information technology is to be used for the purposes of presenting the case at trial, the same system must be used by all parties and must be made available to the Court .

4814.text: Reading Lists, Authorities And Trial Timetable

4816.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise, a single reading list approved by all the parties’ legal representatives for trial must be lodged with the Registry not later than 1 p.m., two days (i.e. two clear days) before the date fixed for trial together with an estimate of the time required for reading.

4818.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise, a single reading list approved by all the parties’ representatives for trial must be lodged with the Registry not later than 1pm, four clear days before the date fixed for trial together with an estimate of the time required for reading.

4820.text: If any party objects to the Judge reading any document in advance of the trial, the objection and its grounds should be clearly stated in a letter accompanying the trial bundles and in the skeleton argument of that party.

4822.text: Parties should consider in particular whether they have any objection to the Judge’s reading the witness statements before the trial.

4824.text: In the absence of objection, the Judge will be free to read the witness statements and documents in advance.

4826.text: A composite bundle of the authorities referred to in the skeleton arguments should be lodged with the Registry as soon as possible after skeleton arguments have been exchanged.

4828.text: Unless otherwise agreed, the preparation of the bundle of authorities is the responsibility of the claimant , who should provide copies to all other parties. The parties’ legal representatives should liaise in relation to the production of bundles of authorities to ensure that the same authority does not appear in more than one bundle.

4830.text: When lodging the reading list, the claimant should also lodge a trial timetable.

4832.text: A trial timetable may have been fixed by the Judge at the pre-trial review. If it has not, a trial timetable should be prepared by the legal representatives for the claimant after consultation with the legal representatives for all other parties.

4834.text: If there are differences of view between the legal representatives for the claimant and legal representatives for other parties, these should be shown.

4836.text: The trial timetable will provide for oral submissions, witness evidence and expert evidence over the course of the trial. On the first day of the trial the Judge may fix the trial timetable, subject to any further order.

4839.text: Written skeleton arguments should be prepared by each party. Guidelines on the preparation of skeleton arguments are set out in Schedule A to Part 23.

4841.text: Unless otherwise ordered, the skeleton arguments should be served on all other parties and lodged with the Court as follows:

4842.text: (1) by the claimant , not later than 1 p.m., two days (i.e. two clear days) before the start of the trial; (2) by each of the defendants , not later than 1 p.m., one day (i.e. one clear day) before the start of the trial.

4844.text: Unless otherwise ordered, the skeleton arguments should be served on all other parties and lodged with the Court as follows:

4845.text:
(1) by the claimant, not later than 1pm, four clear days before the start of the trial;   
(2) by each of the defendants, not later than 1pm, three days before the start of the trial.

4847.text: In heavier cases, it will often be appropriate for skeleton arguments to be served and lodged at earlier times than indicated at Rule 35.62. The timetable should be discussed between the legal representatives and may be the subject of a direction in the pre-trial timetable or at any pre-trial review.

4849.text: The claimant should provide a chronology with his skeleton argument. Indices (i.e. documents that collate key references on particular points, or a substantive list of the contents of a particular bundle or bundles) and dramatis personae, should also be provided where these are likely to be useful. Guidelines on the preparation of chronologies and indices are set out in Schedule A to Part 23.

4850.text: Trial Sitting Days And Hearing Trials In Public

4852.text: Trial sitting days will not normally include Fridays, Saturdays and UAE Public Holidays.

4854.text: Where it is necessary in order to accommodate hearing evidence from certain witnesses or types of witness, the Court may agree to sit outside normal hours.

4857.text: Oral opening statements should as far as possible be uncontroversial and in any event no longer than the circumstances require. Even in a very heavy case, oral opening statements may be very short.

4859.text: At the conclusion of the opening statement for the claimant , the legal representatives for each of the other parties will usually each be invited to make a short opening statement.

4860.text: Applications In The Course Of Trial

4862.text: It will not normally be necessary for an application notice to be issued for an application which is to be made during the course of the trial, but all other parties should be given adequate notice of the intention to apply.

4864.text: Unless the Judge directs otherwise, the parties should prepare skeleton arguments for the hearing of the application.

4867.text: All parties will be expected to make oral closing submissions, whether or not closing submissions have been made in writing. It is a matter for the legal representatives to consider how in all the circumstances these oral submissions should be presented.

4869.text: Unless the trial Judge directs otherwise, the claimant will make his oral closing submissions first, followed by the defendant (s) in the order in which they appear on the claim form with the claimant having a right of reply.

4872.text: In a more substantial trial, the Court will normally also require closing submissions in writing after oral closing submissions. However, the trial judge may decide:

4876.text: In the case of written submissions the Court will normally allow an appropriate period of time after the conclusion of the evidence to allow the preparation of these submissions.

4878.text: Even in a less substantial trial, the Court will normally require a skeleton argument on matters of law.

4880.text: 35.76 Where judgment is to be reserved the Judge may, at the conclusion of the hearing, invite the views of the parties’ legal representatives as to the arrangements to be made for the handing down of the judgment .

4882.text: The Court may provide a copy of the draft judgment to the parties’ legal representatives by 4 p.m. on the second working day before handing down, or at such other time as the Court may direct.

4884.text: If the Judge intends to hand down a written judgment , a copy of the draft text marked

4885.text: “Unapproved judgment. No permission is granted to copy or use in Court”

4887.text: “Confidential to the parties and their legal representatives ”

4888.text: may, at the Court’s discretion, be supplied to the advocates one clear day before the judgment is to be delivered.

4890.text: A copy of the draft judgment may be shown, in confidence, to the parties provided that:

4891.text: (1) neither the judgment nor its substance is disclosed to any other person or used in the public domain; and

4892.text: (2) no action is taken (other than internally) in response to the judgment , before the judgment is handed down.

4894.text: Any breach of the obligation of confidentiality prescribed by Rule 35.79 may be treated as contempt of Court .

4896.text: The case may be listed for judgment , and the judgment handed down at the appropriate time.

4898.text: Legal representatives should inform the Court not later than noon on the day before judgment is to be handed down of any typographical or other errors of a similar nature which the Judge might wish to correct.

4900.text: Unless the parties or their legal representatives are told otherwise when the draft judgment is circulated, the parties must, in respect of any draft agreed order:

4901.text: (1) e-mail a copy to the Registry (together with any proposed corrections or amendments to the draft judgment ); and

4902.text: (2) file four copies in the Registry , by noon on the working day before handing down.

4904.text: A copy of a draft order must bear the case reference, the date of handing down and the name of the Judge.

4906.text: Judgment is not delivered until it is formally pronounced in an open hearing by the Court or is issued by the Registry to the parties or their legal representatives .

4908.text: Where any consequential orders are agreed, the parties’ legal representatives need not attend on the handing down.

4910.text: Where a legal representative does attend, the Court may, if it considers such attendance was unnecessary, disallow the costs of the attendance.

4912.text: In a matter heard by more than one Judge , the judgment may be handed down by a single member of the Court .

4914.text: Copies of the approved judgment will be made available to the parties, to law reporters and to any other person wanting a copy.

4916.text: The Judge may direct that the written judgment stand as the definitive record and that no transcript need

4917.text: be made. Any editorial corrections made at the time of handing down will be incorporated in an approved official text as soon as possible.

4920.text: The rules governing the award and assessment of costs are contained in Parts 38 to 40.

4922.text: The immediate assessment procedure provided for in Part 38 also applies to trials lasting one day or less.

4925.text: Documents impounded by order of the Court must not be released from the custody of the Court except in compliance:

4926.text: (1) with a Court order; or (2) with a written request made by the Attorney General of Dubai.

4928.text: A document released from the custody of the Court under Rule 35.92(2) must be released into the custody of the person who requested it.

4930.text: Documents impounded by order of the Court , while in the custody of the Court , may not be inspected except by a person authorised to do so by a Court order.

4931.text: Settlement Or Discontinuance After The Trial Date Is Fixed

4934.text: (1) an offer to settle a claim is accepted;

4935.text: (2) or a settlement is reached; or

4936.text: (3) a claim is discontinued, which disposes of the whole of a claim for which a date or ‘window’ has been fixed for the trial;

4937.text: the parties must ensure that the Registry is notified immediately.

4939.text: If an order is drawn up giving effect to the settlement or discontinuance of a claim, a copy of the sealed order should be filed with the Registry .

4942.text: At any hearing the proceedings will be tape recorded unless the Judge directs otherwise.

4944.text: No party or member of the public may use unofficial recording equipment in any Court or Judge’s room without the permission of the Court . To do so without permission constitutes a contempt of Court .

4946.text: (1) Any party or person may require a transcript or transcripts of the recording of any hearing to be supplied to him, upon payment of the charges authorised by any scheme in force for the making of the recording or the transcript.

4947.text: (2) Video and audio recordings of a hearing will normally only be provided for the purpose of preparing a transcript of the proceedings or to provide a record for the private use of the party or person by whom it is requested. Such recordings shall not be used for any other purpose without express authorisation of the Court and shall not, without express permission of the Court, be published or broadcast in any way whatsoever.

4949.text: Where the person requiring the transcript or transcripts is not a party to the proceedings and the hearing or any part of it was held in private under Rule 35.4, Rule 35.99 does not apply unless the Court so orders.

4952.text: Exhibits which are handed in and proved during the course of the trial should be recorded on an exhibit list and kept in the custody of the Court until the conclusion of the trial, unless the Judge directs otherwise. At the conclusion of the trial it is the parties’ responsibility to obtain the return of those exhibits which they handed in and to preserve them for the period in which any appeal may take place.

4957.text: 36.1 This Section sets out Rules about judgments and orders which apply except where any other of these Rules or a Practice Direction makes a different provision in relation to the judgment or order in question.

4960.text: Every judgment or order must state the name and judicial title of the person who made it, unless it is:

4961.text: (1) default judgment entered under Rule 13.7 (entry of default judgment where judgment is entered by a Court officer ) or a default costs certificate obtained under Rule 40.17; or

4962.text: (2) judgment entered under Rule 15.17, 15.23 and 15.29 (entry of judgment on admission where judgment is entered by a Court Officer ).

4965.text: (1) bear the time of day at which and date on which it is given or made; and

4968.text: Court orders may be issued and sealed by the Registry electronically.

4970.text: Where a party applies for permission to appeal against a judgment or order at the hearing at which the judgment or order was made, the judgment or order shall state:

4971.text: (1) whether or not the judgment or order is final; and (2) whether the Court gives permission to appeal.

4972.text: Examples Of Forms Of Trial Judgment

4974.text: Form P36/01 is the general form of judgment .

4976.text: A trial judgment should have the following matters set out in a preamble:

4977.text: (1) any order made during the course of the trial concerning the use of evidence; (2) any matters that were agreed between the parties prior to or during the course of the trial in respect of:

4979.text: (3) the findings of the Judge in respect of each head of damage in a personal injury case.

4980.text: Statement As To Service Of A Claim Form

4982.text: Where a party to proceedings which have gone to trial requires a statement to be included in the judgment as to where, and by what means the claim form issued in those proceedings was served, application should be made to the trial Judge when judgment is given.

4984.text: If the Judge so orders, the statement will be included in a preamble to the judgment as entered.

4985.text: Adjustment Of Final Judgment Figure In Respect Of An Interim Payment

4987.text: In a final judgment where an interim payment has previously been made which is less than the total amount awarded by the Judge , the judgment or order should set out in a preamble:

4988.text: (1) the total amount awarded by the Judge ; and

4989.text: (2) the amount and date of the interim payment(s).

4991.text: The total amount awarded by the Judge should then be reduced by the total amount of any interim payments, and the judgment or order should then provide for entry of judgment and payment of the balance.

4993.text: In a final judgment where an interim payment has previously been made which is more than the total amount awarded by the Judge , the judgment or order should set out in a preamble:

4994.text: (1) the total amount awarded by the Judge ; and

4995.text: (2) the amount and date of the interim payment(s).

4997.text: An order should then be made for repayment, reimbursement, variation or discharge under Rule 25.87 and for interest on an overpayment under Rule 25.90.

5000.text: Where judgment is ordered to be entered in a currency other than US Dollars, the order should be in the following form:

5001.text: It is ordered that the defendant pay the claimant (state the sum in the currency other than US Dollars) or the US Dollar equivalent at the time of payment.

5004.text: Attention is drawn to Parts 38 to 40, in particular, to the Court’s power to make a summary assessment of costs and the provisions relating to interest in detailed assessment proceedings.

5006.text: Attention is also drawn to Rule 38.56 which provides that if an order makes no mention of costs, none are payable in respect of the proceedings to which it relates.

5009.text: Where a judgment is to be paid by instalments, the judgment should set out:

5011.text: Drawing Up And Filing Of Judgments And Orders

5013.text: Except for orders made by the Court of its own initiative and unless the Court otherwise orders, every judgment or order will be drawn up by the parties.

5015.text: Where a judgment or an order is to be drawn up by a party:

5016.text: (1) he must file it no later than 48 hours after the date on which the Court ordered or permitted him to draw it up so that it can be sealed by the Court ; and

5017.text: (2) if he fails to file it within that period, any other party may draw it up and file it.

5019.text: Where any judgment , order or direction is to be served outside the DIFC , unless the Court otherwise directs:

5020.text: (1) in proceedings on notice the parties must agree and lodge with the Court an Arabic translation of any judgment , order or direction within 48 hours of the same being given, made or handed down as the case may be, in default of which the Court may accept one party’s translation or suspend or revoke the judgment , order or direction on such terms as it thinks fit;

5021.text: (2) in without notice proceedings the party seeking relief must provide an Arabic translation of the order sought and made; or

5022.text: (3) in the event of any inconsistency between the Arabic and English versions of any judgment , order or direction, the English version shall prevail.

5025.text: (1) a judgment or an order drawn up by a party must be checked by the Court before it is sealed; or

5026.text: (2) before a judgment or an order is drawn up by the Court , the parties must file an agreed statement of its terms.

5028.text: If the Court directs that a judgment or order which is being drawn up by a party must be checked by the Court before it is sealed, the party responsible must file the draft within 48 hours of the date the order was made with a request that the draft be checked before it is sealed.

5030.text: If the Court directs the parties to file an agreed statement of terms of an order which the Court is to draw up, the parties must do so no later than 48 hours from the date the order was made, unless the Court directs otherwise.

5032.text: If the Court requires the terms of an order which is being drawn up by the Court to be agreed by the parties the Court may direct that a copy of the draft order is to be sent to all the parties:

5033.text: (1) for their agreement to be endorsed on it and returned to the Court before the order is sealed; or (2) with notice of an appointment to attend before the Court to agree the terms of the order.

5036.text: Where a judgment or an order has been drawn up by a party and is to be served by the Court:

5037.text: (1) the party who drew it up must file a copy to be retained at Court and sufficient copies for service on him and on the other parties; and

5038.text: (2) once it has been sealed, the Court must serve a copy of it on each party to the proceedings.

5040.text: Unless the Court directs otherwise, any order made otherwise than at trial must be served on:

5041.text: (1) the applicant and the respondent; and

5042.text: (2) any other person on whom the Court orders it to be served.

5043.text: Power To Require Judgment Or Order To Be Served On A Party As Well As His Legal Representative

5045.text: Where the party on whom a judgment or order is to be served is acting by a legal representative , the Court may order the judgment or order to be served on the party as well as on his legal representative .

5048.text: An application for an agreed order must include a draft of the proposed order signed on behalf of all the parties to whom it relates.

5050.text: Where proceedings are to be stayed on agreed terms:

5051.text: (1) The draft order should include the following:

5052.text: “(1) The Defendant having agreed to the terms set forth in the schedule hereto, IT IS ORDERED THAT all further proceedings in this claim be stayed, except for the purpose of carrying such terms into effect.

5053.text: (2) Liberty to apply in order to carry such terms into effect.”; and

5054.text: (2) Settlement terms should be set out in a schedule attached to the draft order.

5055.text: When Judgment Or Order Takes Effect

5057.text: A judgment or order takes immediate effect from the time on the day when it is given or made, or such later time or date as the Court may specify.

5058.text: Time From Which Interest Begins To Run

5060.text: Where interest is payable on a judgment pursuant to Article 39 of the Court Law 2004, the interest shall begin to run from the date that judgment is given unless:

5061.text: (1) a Rule in another Part or a Practice Direction makes different provision; or

5064.text: The Court may order that interest shall begin to run from a date before the date that judgment is given.

5065.text: Who May Apply To Set Aside Or Vary A Judgment Or Order

5067.text: A person who is not a party but who is directly affected by a judgment or order may apply to have the judgment or order set aside or varied.

5068.text: Time For Complying With A Judgment Or Order

5070.text: A party must comply with a judgment or order for the payment of an amount of money (including costs) within 14 days of the date of the judgment or order, unless:

5071.text: (1) the judgment or order specifies a different date for compliance (including specifying payment by instalments);

5072.text: (2) any of these Rules specifies a different date for compliance; or

5073.text: (3) the Court has stayed the proceedings or judgment .

5074.text: Orders Requiring An Act To Be Done

5076.text: An order which requires an act to be done (other than a judgment or order for the payment of an amount of money) must specify the time within which the act should be done.

5078.text: The consequences of failure to do an act within the time specified may be set out in the order. In this case the wording of the following example suitably adapted must be used:

5079.text: “Unless the [claimant/defendant] serves his list of documents by $4 . 0 0 \ \mathsf { p . m }$ . on Wednesday, 31 January 2007 his [claim/defence] will be struck out and judgment entered for the [defendant/claimant].”

5080.text: Non-Compliance With A Judgment Or Order

5082.text: An order which restrains a party from doing an act or requires an act to be done should, if disobedience is to be dealt with by an application to bring contempt of Court proceedings, have a penal notice endorsed on it as follows:

5083.text: “If you [name] disobey this order you may be held to be in contempt of Court and may be imprisoned, fined or have your assets seized.”

5085.text: The provisions of Rule 36.35 also apply to an order which contains an undertaking by a party to do or not do an act, subject to Rule 36.39.

5087.text: The Court has the power to decline to:

5089.text: (2) deal with disobedience in respect of an undertaking by contempt of Court proceedings, unless the party giving the undertaking has made a signed statement to the effect that he understands the terms of his undertaking and the consequences of failure to comply with it.

5091.text: The statement may be endorsed on the order containing the undertaking or may be filed in a separate document such as a letter.

5092.text: Correction Of Errors In Judgments And Orders

5094.text: The Court may at any time correct an accidental slip or omission in a judgment or order.

5096.text: An application for a correction must be made by application notice and the standard application fee should be paid. The application notice should be precise in describing the nature of the error, should set out the exact wording of the correction sought and, where appropriate should be accompanied by a draft order. An application may be dealt with without a hearing:

5098.text: (2) with the consent of the parties; or

5099.text: (3) where the Court does not consider that a hearing would be appropriate.

5101.text: The Judge 43may deal with the application without notice if the slip or omission is obvious or may direct notice of the application to be given to the other party or parties.

5103.text: If the application is opposed it should, if practicable, be listed for hearing before the Judge who gave the judgment or made the order.

5105.text: The Court has an inherent power to vary its own orders to make the meaning and intention of the Court clear.

5107.text: Where the Court allows an application to correct an order and the reason for the error was a clerical mistake on the part of the Registry , the application fee will be refunded.

5108.text: Cases Where Court Gives Judgment Both On Claim And Counterclaim

5110.text: Rule 36.48 applies where the Court gives judgment for specified amounts both for the claimant on his claim and against the claimant on a counterclaim .

5112.text: If there is a balance in favour of one of the parties, it may order the party whose judgment is for the lesser amount to pay the balance.

5114.text: In a case to which Rule 36.48 applies, the Court may make a separate order as to costs against each party.

5115.text: Judgment In Favour Of Certain Part Owners Relating To The Detention Of Goods

5117.text: In Rule 36.51 ‘part owner’ means one of two or more persons who have an interest in the same goods.

5120.text: (1) a part owner makes a claim relating to the detention of the goods; and

5121.text: (2) the claim is not based on a right to possession;

5122.text: any judgment or order given or made in respect of the claim is to be for the payment of damages only, unless the claimant had the written authority of every other part owner of the goods to make the claim on his behalf as well as for himself.

5125.text: The Court may make binding declarations whether or not any other remedy is claimed.

5126.text: III PREPARATION OF DOCUMENTS UNDER AN ORDER

5128.text: Where a judgment or order directs any document to be prepared, executed or signed, the order will state:

5129.text: (1) the person who is to prepare the document; and (2) if the document is to be approved, the person who is to approve it.

5131.text: If the parties are unable to agree on the form of the document, any party may apply in accordance with Part 23 for the form of the document to be settled.

5133.text: In such case the Judge may:

5136.text: (a) the Registrar ; or (b) an Assessor in accordance with Part 31 subject to such modifications as he thinks fit.

5140.text: Where the Court orders any account to be taken or any inquiry to be made, it may, by the same or a subsequent order, give directions as to the manner in which the account is to be taken and verified or the inquiry is to be conducted.

5142.text: In particular, the Court may direct that in taking an account, the relevant books of account shall be evidence of their contents but that any party may take such objections to the contents as he may think fit.

5144.text: Any party may apply to the Court in accordance with Part 23 for directions as to the taking of an account or the conduct of an inquiry or for the variation of directions already made.

5146.text: Every direction for the taking of an account or the making of an inquiry shall be numbered in the order so that, as far as possible, each distinct account and inquiry is given its own separate number.

5149.text: Subject to any order to the contrary:

5150.text: (1) the accounting party must make out his account and verify it by an affidavit to which the account is exhibited; and

5151.text: (2) the accounting party must file the account with the Court and at the same time notify the other parties that he has done so and of the filing of any affidavit verifying or supporting the account.

5154.text: Any party who wishes to contend:

5155.text: (1) that an accounting party has received more than the amount shown by the account to have been received;

5156.text: (2) that the accounting party should be treated as having received more than he has actually received;

5157.text: (3) that any item in the account is erroneous in respect of amount; or

5158.text: (4) that in any other respect the account is inaccurate;

5159.text: must, unless the Court directs otherwise, give written notice to the accounting party of his objections.

5161.text: The written notice referred to in Rule 36.61 must, so far as the objecting party is able to do so:

5162.text: (1) state the amount by which it is contended that the account understates the amount received by the accounting party;

5163.text: (2) state the amount which it is contended that the accounting party should be treated as having

5164.text: received in addition to the amount he actually received; $< / { \mathsf { p } } >$

5165.text: (3) specify the respects in which it is contended that the account is inaccurate; and

5166.text: (4) in each case, give the grounds on which the contention is made.

5168.text: The contents of the written notice must be verified by an affidavit to which the notice is an exhibit.

5171.text: In taking any account all just allowances shall be made without any express direction to that effect.

5174.text: The Court may at any stage in the taking of an account or in the course of an inquiry direct a hearing in order to resolve an issue that has arisen and for that purpose may order that points of claim and points of defence be served and give any necessary directions.

5177.text: If it appears to the Court that there is undue delay in the taking of any account or the progress of any inquiry the Court may require the accounting party or the party with the conduct of the inquiry, as the case may be, to explain the delay and may then make such order for the management of the proceedings (including a stay ) and for costs as the circumstances may require.

5180.text: Where some of the persons entitled to share in a fund are known but there is, or is likely to be, difficulty or delay in ascertaining other persons so entitled, the Court may direct, or allow, immediate payment of their shares to the known persons without reserving any part of those shares to meet the subsequent costs of ascertaining the other persons.

5181.text: Accounts And Inquiries To Be Conducted Before The Registrar

5183.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise, an account or inquiry will be taken or made by the Registrar .

5187.text: (1) direct any necessary advertisement; and (2) fix the time within which the advertisement should require a reply.

5190.text: Where the Court orders an account of debts or other liabilities to be taken, it may direct any party, within a specified time, to:

5191.text: (1) examine the claims of persons claiming to be owed money out of the estate or fund in question;

5192.text: (2) determine, so far as he is able, which of them are valid; and

5194.text: (a) stating his findings and his reasons for them; and

5195.text: (b) listing any other debts which are or may be owed out of the estate or fund.

5197.text: Where the Court orders an inquiry for next of kin or other unascertained claimants to an estate or fund, it may direct any party, within a specified time, to:

5198.text: (1) examine the claims that are made; (2) determine, so far as he is able, which of them are valid; and (3) file written evidence stating his findings and his reasons for them.

5200.text: If the personal representatives or trustees concerned are not the parties directed by the Court to examine claims, the Court may direct them to join with the party directed to examine claims in producing the written evidence required by this rule.

5201.text: Consideration Of Claims By The Court

5203.text: For the purpose of considering a claim the Court may:

5204.text: (1) direct it to be investigated in any manner; (2) direct the person making the claim to give further details of it; and

5206.text: (a) file written evidence; or (b) attend Court to give evidence;

5211.text: (1) the Court has allowed or disallowed any claim or part of a claim; and

5212.text: (2) the person making the claim was not present when the decision was made;

5213.text: the Court will serve on that person a notice informing him of its decision.

5216.text: Where an account of the debts of a deceased person is directed by any judgment , unless the deceased’s estate is insolvent or the Court orders otherwise, interest shall be allowed:

5217.text:
(1) on any debt which carries interest, at the rate it carries; and   
(2) on any other debt, from the date of the judgment , at the rate payable on judgment debts at that date.

5219.text: Where interest on a debt is allowed under Rule 36.75(2), it shall be paid out of any assets of the estate which remain after payment of:

5220.text: (1) any costs of the proceedings directed to be paid out of the estate; (2) all the debts which have been established; and (3) the interest on such of those debts as by law carry interest.

5222.text: For the purpose of Rules 36.75 and 36.76:

5223.text: (1) ‘debt’ includes funeral, testamentary or administration expenses; and (2) in relation to any expenses incurred after the judgment , Rule 36.75(2) applies as if, instead of the date of the judgment , it referred to the date when the expenses became payable.

5226.text: Legal Representative Acting For A Party

5228.text: Where the address for service of a party is the business address of his legal representative , the legal representative will be considered to be acting for that party until the provisions of this Part have been complied with.

5230.text: A legal representative appointed to represent a party only as an advocate at a hearing will not be considered to be acting for that party within the meaning of this Part.

5231.text: Change Of Legal Representative — Duty To Give Notice

5234.text: (1) a party for whom a legal representative is acting wants to change his legal representative ; (2) a party, after having conducted the claim in person, appoints a legal representative to act on his behalf (except where the legal representative is appointed only to act as an advocate for a hearing); or (3) a party, after having conducted the claim by a legal representative , intends to act in person.

5236.text: Where Rules 37.3 to 37.10 apply, the party or his legal representative (where one is acting) must:

5237.text:
(1) file notice of the change; and   
(2) serve notice of the change on every other party and, where Rule 37.3(1) or 37.3(3) applies, on the former legal representative .

5239.text: The notice must state the party’s new address for service .

5241.text: The notice filed at Court must state that notice has been served as required by paragraph 37.4(2).

5243.text: Where a party has changed his legal representative or intends to act in person, the former legal representative will be considered to be the party’s legal representative unless and until:

5244.text: (1) notice is filed and served in accordance with paragraph 37.4; or

5245.text: (2) the Court makes an order under Rules 37.11 to 37.13 and the order is served as required by Rule 37.13.

5247.text: In addition, where a party or legal representative changes his address for service , a notice of that change should be filed and served on every party.

5249.text: A party who, having conducted a claim by a legal representative , intends to act in person must give in his notice an address for service that is within the jurisdiction .

5251.text: Form P37/01 should be used to give notice of any change.

5252.text: Order That A Legal Representative Has Ceased To Act

5254.text: A legal representative may apply for an order declaring that he has ceased to be the legal representative acting for a party.

5256.text: Where an application is made under Rule 37.11:

5259.text: Where the Court makes an order that a legal representative has ceased to act:

5260.text: (1) a copy of the order must be served on every party to the proceedings;

5261.text: (2) if it is served by a party or the legal representative , the party or the legal representative (as the case may be) must file a certificate of service in Form P37/01; and

5262.text: (3) the order takes effect when it is served.

5263.text: Removal Of Legal Representative Who Has Ceased To Act On Application Of Another Party

5266.text: (1) a legal representative who has acted for a party:

5267.text: (a) has died; (b) has become bankrupt; (c) has ceased to practice; or (d) cannot be found; and

5268.text: (2) the party has not given notice of a change of legal representative or notice of intention to act in person as required by Rule 37.4;

5269.text: any other party may apply for an order declaring that the legal representative has ceased to be the lega representative acting for the other party in the case.

5271.text: Where an application is made under Rule 37.14:

5274.text: Where the Court makes an order made under Rule 37.14:

5275.text: (1) a copy of the order must be served on every other party to the proceedings; (2) where it is served by a party, that party must file a certificate of service Form P37/01; and (3) the order takes effect when it is served.

5276.text: New Address For Service Where Order Made Under Rules 37.11 Or 37.14

5278.text: Where the Court has made an order under Rule 37.11 that a legal representative has ceased to act or under Rule 37.14 declaring that a legal representative has ceased to be the legal representative for a party, the party for whom the legal representative was acting must give a new address for service to comply with Rule 9.15.

5283.text: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires:

5286.text: The costs to which Parts 38 to 40 apply include the following costs where those costs may be assessed by the Court:

5290.text: ‘Immediate assessment’ means the procedure by which the Court , when making an order about costs, orders payment of a sum of money instead of fixed costs or ‘detailed assessment’.

5293.text:
‘Detailed assessment’ means the procedure by which the amount of costs is decided by the Court in accordance with Part 40.   
Legal representative’s duty to notify client

5294.text: Legal Representative’S Duty To Notify Client

5297.text: (1) the Court makes a costs order against a legally represented party; and

5298.text: (2) the party is not present when the order is made;

5299.text: the party’s legal representative must notify his client in writing of the costs order no later than 7 days after the legal representative receives notice of the order.

5300.text: Court’s discretion and circumstances to be taken into account when exercising its discretion as to costs

5301.text: Court’S Discretion And Circumstances To Be Taken Into Account When Exercising Its Discretion As To Costs

5303.text: Subject to Rules 38.15 to 38.16 the Court has discretion as to:

5304.text:
(1) whether costs are payable by one party to another; (2) the amount of those costs; and   
(3) when they are to be paid.

5306.text: If the Court decides to make an order about costs:

5307.text: (1) the general rule is that the unsuccessful party will be ordered to pay the costs of the successful party; but

5308.text: (2) the Court may make a different order.

5310.text: In deciding what order (if any) to make about costs, the Court must have regard to all the circumstances, including:

5311.text: (1) the conduct of all the parties;

5312.text: (2) whether a party has succeeded on part of his case, even if he has not been wholly successful; and (3) any payment into Court or admissible offer to settle made by a party which is drawn to the Court’s attention and which is not a Part 32 offer.

5314.text: The conduct of the parties includes:

5315.text: (1) conduct before, as well as during, the proceedings; (2) whether it was reasonable for a party to raise, pursue or contest a particular allegation or issue; (3) the manner in which a party has pursued or defended his case or a particular allegation or issue; and (4) whether a claimant who has succeeded in his claim, in whole or in part, exaggerated his claim.

5317.text: The orders which the Court may make include an order that a party must pay:

5320.text: Where the Court would otherwise consider making an order under Rule 38.10(6), it must instead, if practicable, make an order under paragraph 38.10(1) or 38.10(3).

5322.text: There are certain costs orders which the Court will commonly make in proceedings before trial. The following table sets out the general effect of these orders. The table is not an exhaustive list of the orders which the Court may make.

5323.text:
TermEffect
The party in whose favour the order is made is entitled to the costs in
Costs Costs in any eventrespect of the part of the proceedings to which the order relates, whatever other costs orders are made in the proceedings.
Costs in the caseCosts in the applicationThe party in whose favour the Court makes an order for costs at the endof the proceedings is entitled to his costs of the part of the proceedingsto which the order relates.
Costs reservedThe decision about costs is deferred to a later occasion, but if no laterorder is made the costs will be costs in the case.
Claimants Defendant 'scosts in case/applicationIf the party in whose favour the costs order is made is awarded costs atthe end of the proceedings, that party is entitled to his costs of the partof the proceedings to which the order relates. If any other party isawarded costs at the end of the proceedings, the party in whose favourthe final costs order is made is not liable to pay the costs of any otherparty in respect of the part of the proceedings to which the order relates.Where, for example, a judgment or order is set aside , the party in whosefavour the costs order is made is entitled to the costs which have beenincurred as a consequence. This includes the costs of—
Costs thrown away

5324.text: (a) preparing for and attending any hearing at which the judgment or order which has been set aside was made;

5325.text: (b) preparing for and attending any hearing to set aside the judgment or order in question;

5326.text: (c) preparing for and attending any hearing at which the Court orders the proceedings or the part in question to be adjourned;

5327.text: (d) any steps taken to enforce a judgment or order which has subsequently been set aside .

5329.text: Where, for example, the Court makes this order on an application to amend a statement of case , the party in whose favour the costs order is made is entitled to the costs of preparing for and attending the application and the costs of any consequential amendment to his own statement of case .

5331.text: The party in whose favour the costs order is made is entitled not only to his costs in respect of the proceedings in which the Court makes the order but also to his costs of the proceedings in any lower Court .

5333.text: Each party to pay his own costs

5334.text: Each party is to bear his own costs of the part of the proceedings to which the order relates whatever costs order the Court makes at the end of the proceedings.

5336.text: Where the Court has ordered a party to pay costs, it may order an amount to be paid on account before the costs are assessed.

5338.text: Where a party entitled to costs is also liable to pay costs the Court may assess the costs which that party is liable to pay and either:

5339.text:
(1) set off the amount assessed against the amount the party is entitled to be paid and direct him to pay any balance; or   
(2) delay the issue of a certificate for the costs to which the party is entitled until he has paid the amount which he is liable to pay.

5340.text: Costs In The Small Claims Tribunal (‘Sct’)

5342.text: The SCT may not order a party to a small claim to pay a sum to another party in respect of that other party’s costs, fees and expenses, including those relating to an appeal, except:

5343.text: (1) such part of any Court or Tribunal fees paid by that other party as the SCT may consider appropriate; (2) such further costs as the SCT may assess by the summary procedure and order to be paid by a party who has behaved unreasonably.

5345.text: A party’s rejection of an offer in settlement will not of itself constitute unreasonable behaviour under Rule 38.15(1) but the Court may take it into consideration when it is applying the unreasonableness test.

5348.text: Where the Court is to assess the amount of costs (whether by immediate or detailed assessment) it will assess those costs:

5351.text: but the Court will not in either case allow costs which have been unreasonably incurred or are unreasonable in amount.

5353.text: Where the amount of costs is to be assessed on the standard basis, the Court will:

5354.text:
(1) only allow costs which are proportionate to the matters in issue; and   
(2) resolve any doubt which it may have as to whether costs were reasonably incurred or reasonable and proportionate in amount in favour of the paying party .

5356.text: Where the amount of costs is to be assessed on the indemnity basis, the Court will resolve any doubt which it may have as to whether costs were reasonably incurred or were reasonable in amount in favour of the receiving party .

5359.text: (1) the Court makes an order about costs without indicating the basis on which the costs are to be assessed; or

5360.text: (2) the Court makes an order for costs to be assessed on a basis other than the standard basis or the indemnity basis;

5361.text: the costs will be assessed on the standard basis.

5362.text: Factors To Be Taken Into Account In Deciding The Amount Of Costs

5364.text: The Court is to have regard to all the circumstances in deciding whether costs were:

5365.text: (1) if it is assessing costs on the standard basis: (a) proportionately and reasonably incurred; and (b) were proportionate and reasonable in amount; or

5366.text: (2) if it is assessing costs on the indemnity basis: (a) unreasonably incurred; or (b) unreasonable in amount.

5368.text: In particular, the Court must give effect to any orders which have already been made.

5370.text: The Court must also have regard to:

5371.text: (1) the conduct of all the parties, including in particular: (a) conduct before, as well as during, the proceedings; and (b) the efforts made, if any, before and during the proceedings in order to try to resolve the dispute;

5372.text: (2) the amount or value of any money or property involved;

5373.text: (3) the importance of the matter to all the parties;

5374.text: (4) the particular complexity of the matter or the difficulty or novelty of the questions raised;

5375.text: (5) the skill, effort, specialised knowledge and responsibility involved;

5376.text: (6) the time spent on the case; and

5377.text: (7) the place where and the circumstances in which work or any part of it was done.

5379.text: In applying the test of proportionality the Court will have regard to Rule 1.6(3). The relationship between the total of the costs incurred and the financial value of the claim may not be a reliable guide. A fixed percentage cannot be applied in all cases to the value of the claim in order to ascertain whether or not the costs are proportionate.

5381.text: In any proceedings there will be costs which will inevitably be incurred and which are necessary for the successful conduct of the case. Legal representatives are not required to conduct litigation at rates which are uneconomic. Thus in a modest claim the proportion of costs is likely to be higher than in a large claim, and may even equal or possibly exceed the amount in dispute.

5383.text: Where a trial takes place, the time taken by the Court in dealing with a particular issue may not be an accurate guide to the amount of time properly spent by the legal or other representatives in preparation for the trial of that issue.

5386.text: A party may recover the fixed costs specified in Part 39 in accordance with that Part.

5389.text: Where the Court orders a party to pay costs to another party (other than fixed costs ) it may either:

5390.text: (1) make an immediate assessment of the costs; or

5391.text: (2) order detailed assessment of the costs;

5392.text: unless any Rule, Practice Direction or other enactment provides otherwise.

5394.text: Whenever the Court makes an order about costs which does not provide for fixed costs to be paid, the Court should consider whether to make an immediate assessment of costs.

5396.text: The general rule is that the Court should make an immediate assessment of the costs:

5397.text: (1) at the conclusion of any hearing, which has lasted no more than one day, in which case the order will deal with the costs of the application or matter to which the hearing related. If this hearing disposes of the claim, the order may deal with the costs of the whole claim;

5398.text: (2) in hearings in the Court of Appeal to which FRDC 44.165 / ARDC 44.140 applies;

5399.text: unless there is good reason not to do so e.g. where the paying party shows substantial grounds for disputing the sum claimed for costs that cannot be dealt with on the material available or there is insufficient time to carry out an immediate assessment.

5401.text: If an immediate assessment of costs is appropriate but the Court awarding costs is unable to do so on the day, the Court will give directions as to a further hearing before the same Judge.

5403.text: Where the parties agree an order without the need for any party to attend, the parties must agree a figure for costs to be inserted in the agreed order or agree that there should be no order for costs.

5405.text: It is the duty of the parties and their legal representatives to assist the Judge in making an immediate assessment of costs in any case to which Rule 38.30 applies, in accordance with Rules 38.34 to 38.36.

5407.text: Each party who intends to claim costs must prepare a written statement of the costs he intends to claim showing separately in the form of a schedule:

5410.text: The statement of costs should follow as closely as possible Form P38/01 and must be signed by the party or his legal representative .

5412.text: The statement of costs must be filed at Court and copies of it must be served on any party against whom an order for payment of those costs is intended to be sought. The statement of costs should be filed and the copies of it should be served as soon as possible and in any event not less than 24 hours before the date fixed for the hearing.

5414.text: The failure by a party, without reasonable excuse, to comply with Rules 38.33 to 38.36 will be taken into account by the Court in deciding what order to make about the costs of the claim, hearing or application, and about the costs of any further hearing or detailed assessment hearing that may be necessary as a result of that failure.

5416.text: The Court will not make an immediate assessment of the costs of a receiving party who is a child or patient unless the legal representative acting for the child or patient has waived the right to further costs.

5418.text: The Court may make an immediate assessment of costs payable by a child or patient.

5419.text: Time For Complying With An Order For Costs

5421.text: A party must comply with an order for the payment of costs within 14 days of:

5423.text: Cases Where Costs Orders Will Be Considered To Have Been Made

5425.text: Where a right to costs arises under:

5426.text: (1) Rule 4.31 (defendant’s right to costs where claim struck out for nonpayment of fees);

5427.text: (2) Rules 32.28 and 32.29 (claimant’s entitlement to costs where a Part 32 offer is accepted); or

5428.text: (3) Rules 34.15 and 34.16 (defendant’s right to costs where claimant discontinues);

5429.text: a costs order will have been made on the standard basis.

5431.text: Interest on the costs deemed to have been ordered under Rule 38.41 shall begin to run from the date on which the event which gave rise to the entitlement to costs occurred.

5434.text: Rules 38.44 to 38.53 set out a procedure which may be followed where:

5435.text: (1) the parties to a dispute have reached an agreement on all issues (including which party is to pay the costs) which is made or confirmed in writing; but

5436.text: (2) they have failed to agree the amount of those costs; and

5439.text: Either party to the agreement may start proceedings under Rules 38.44 to 38.53 by issuing a claim form in accordance with Part 8.

5444.text: In proceedings to which Rules 38.44 to 38.53 apply the Court:

5446.text: (a) make an order for costs to be determined by detailed assessment; or

5448.text: (2) must dismiss the claim if it is opposed.

5451.text: (1) a claim will be treated as opposed if the defendant files an acknowledgment of service stating that he intends to contest the making of an order for costs or to seek a different remedy; and

5452.text: (2) a claim will not be treated as opposed if the defendant files an acknowledgment of service stating that he disputes the amount of the claim for costs.

5454.text: An order dismissing the claim will be made as soon as an acknowledgment of service opposing the claim is filed. The dismissal of a claim under Rule 38.46(2) does not prevent the claimant from issuing another claim form under Part 7 or Part 8 based on the agreement or alleged agreement to which the proceedings under this rule related.

5456.text: The evidence to be filed and served with the claim form under Rule 8.23 must include copies of the documents on which the claimant relies to prove the defendant’s agreement to pay costs.

5458.text: The Registrar has jurisdiction to hear and decide any issue which may arise in a claim issued under Rules 38.44 to 38.53 irrespective of the amount of the costs claimed or of the value of the claim to which the agreement to pay costs relates.

5460.text: When the time for filing the defendant’s acknowledgement of service has expired, the claimant may by letter request the Court to make an order in the terms of his claim, unless the defendant has filed an acknowledgement of service stating that he intends to contest the claim or to seek a different order.

5462.text: An order for costs made under Rule 38.50 will be treated as an order for the amount of costs to be decided by a detailed assessment.

5464.text: In cases in which an additional liability is claimed, regard should be had to the time when and the extent to which the claim has been settled and to the fact that the claim has been settled without the need to commence proceedings.

5466.text: Nothing in Rules 38.44 to 38.53 prevents a person from issuing a claim form under Part 7 or Part 8 to sue on an agreement made in settlement of a dispute where that agreement makes provision for costs, nor from claiming in that case an order for costs or a specified sum in respect of costs.

5469.text: Where the Court makes an order which does not mention costs:

5470.text: (1) subject to Rules 38.56 and 38.57, the general rule is that no party is entitled to costs in relation to that order; but

5471.text: (2) this does not affect any entitlement of a party to recover costs out of a fund held by him as trustee or personal representative, or pursuant to any lease, mortgage or other security.

5476.text: Any party affected by an order for costs under Rule 38.56 may apply at any time to vary the order.

5478.text: The Court hearing an appeal may, unless it dismisses the appeal, make orders about the costs of the proceedings giving rise to the appeal as well as the costs of the appeal.

5479.text: Court’S Powers In Relation To Misconduct

5481.text: The Court may, in connection with an immediate or detailed assessment, make an order under Rule 38.60 where:

5482.text: (1) a party or his legal representative fails to comply with a Rule, Practice Direction or Court order; or (2) it appears to the Court that the conduct of a party or his legal representative , before or during the proceedings which gave rise to the assessment proceedings, was unreasonable or improper.

5484.text: Where Rule 38.59 applies, the Court may:

5485.text: (1) disallow all or part of the costs which are being assessed; or

5486.text: (2) order the party at fault or his legal representative to pay costs which he has caused any other party to incur.

5489.text: (1) the Court makes an order under Rule 38.60 against a legally represented party; and (2) the party is not present when the order is made;

5490.text: the party’s legal representative must notify his client in writing of the order no later than 7 days after the legal representative receives notice of the order and either in the order under Rule 38.60 or in a subsequent order, require the legal representative to produce to the Court evidence that he took reasonable steps to do so.

5492.text: Before making an order under Rule 38.60 the Court must give the party or legal representative in question a reasonable opportunity to attend a hearing to give reasons why it should not make such an order.

5494.text: Conduct before or during the proceedings which gave rise to the assessment which is unreasonable or improper includes steps which are calculated to prevent or inhibit the Court from furthering the Overriding Objective.

5495.text: Production Of Documents And Inspection Of Property Before Commencement Or Against A Non-Party

5497.text: Rule 38.65 applies where a person applies:

5500.text: which give the Court powers exercisable before commencement of proceedings; or

5503.text: which give the Court power to make an order against a non-party for disclosure of documents, inspection of property etc.

5505.text: The general rule is that the Court will award the person against whom the order is sought his costs:

5506.text: (1) of the application; and (2) of complying with any order made on the application.

5508.text: The Court may however make a different order, having regard to all the circumstances, including the extent to which it was reasonable for the person against whom the order was sought to oppose the application.

5509.text: Costs Orders In Favour Of Or Against Non-Parties

5511.text: Where the Court is considering whether to exercise its power to make a costs order in favour of or against a person who is not a party to proceedings:

5512.text: (1) that person must be added as a party to the proceedings for the purposes of costs only; and (2) he must be given a reasonable opportunity to attend a hearing at which the Court will consider the matter further.

5515.text: (1) where the Court is considering whether to make a wasted costs order; and (2) in proceedings to which Rule 38.65 applies.

5516.text: Amount Of Costs Where Costs Are Payable Pursuant To A Contract

5518.text: Where the Court assesses (whether by the immediate or detailed procedure) costs which are payable by the paying party to the receiving party under the terms of a contract, the costs payable under those terms are, unless the contract expressly provides otherwise, to be presumed to be costs which:

5519.text: (1) have been reasonably incurred; and (2) are reasonable in amount, and the Court will assess them accordingly.

5521.text: Rule 38.69 does not apply where the contract is between a legal representative and his client.

5522.text: Limitations On Court’S Power To Award Costs In Favour Of Trustee Or Personal

5526.text: (1) a person is or has been a party to any proceedings in the capacity of trustee or personal representative (“person representing his estate”); and

5529.text: The general rule is that he is entitled to be paid the costs of those proceedings, insofar as they are not recovered from or paid by any other person, out of the relevant trust fund or estate.

5531.text: Where he is entitled to be paid any of those costs out of the fund or estate, those costs will be assessed on the indemnity basis.

5532.text: Costs Where Money Is Payable By Or To A Child Or Patient

5534.text: Rule 38.75 applies to any proceedings where a party is a child or patient and:

5535.text: (1) money is ordered or agreed to be paid to, or for the benefit of, that party; or

5536.text: (2) money is ordered to be paid by him or on his behalf.

5539.text: (1) the Court must order a detailed assessment of the costs payable by, or out of money belonging to, any party who is a child or patient; and

5540.text: (2) on an assessment under (1), the Court must also assess any costs payable to that party in the proceedings, unless the Court has issued a default costs certificate in relation to those costs under Part 40.

5541.text: (3) The Court need not order detailed assessment of costs:

5542.text: (a) where there is no need to do so to protect the interests of the child or patient or his estate;

5543.text: (b) where another party has agreed to pay a specified sum in respect of the costs of the child or patient and the legal representative acting for the child or patient has waived the right to claim further costs;

5544.text: (c) where the Court has decided the costs payable to the child or patient by way of immediate assessment and the legal representative acting for the child or patient has waived the right to claim

5546.text: (d) where an insurer or other person is liable to discharge the costs which the child or patient would otherwise be liable to pay to his legal representative and the Court is satisfied that the insurer or other person is financially able to discharge those costs.

5548.text:
(a) a claimant is a child or patient; and   
(b) a detailed assessment has taken place under this Rule the only amount payable by the child or patient is the amount which the Court certifies as payable.

5549.text: Costs Where The Court Has Made A Group Litigation Order

5551.text: Rules 38.77 to 38.82 apply where the Court has made a Group Litigation Order (‘GLO’).

5554.text: (1) ‘individual costs’ means costs incurred in relation to an individual claim on the group register;

5556.text: (a) costs incurred in relation to the GLO issues;

5557.text: (b) individual costs incurred in a claim while it is proceeding as a test claim; and

5558.text: (c) costs incurred by the lead legal representative in administering the group litigation;

5559.text: (3) ‘group litigant’ means a claimant or defendant , as the case may be, whose claim is entered on the group register.

5561.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise, any order for common costs against group litigants imposes on each group litigant several liability for an equal proportion of those common costs.

5563.text: The general rule is that where a group litigant is the paying party , he will, in addition to any costs he is liable to pay to the receiving party , be liable for:

5564.text: (1) the individual costs of his claim; and (2) an equal proportion, together with all the other group litigants, of the common costs.

5566.text: Where the Court makes an order about costs in relation to any application or hearing which involved:

5567.text: (1) one or more GLO issues; and

5568.text: (2) issues relevant only to individual claims;

5569.text: the Court will direct the proportion of the costs that is to relate to common costs and the proportion that is to relate to individual costs.

5571.text: Where common costs have been incurred before a claim is entered on the group register, the Court may order the group litigant to be liable for a proportion of those costs.

5573.text: Where a claim is removed from the group register, the Court may make an order for costs in that claim which includes a proportion of the common costs incurred up to the date on which the claim is removed from the group register.

5574.text: Personal Liability Of Legal Representative For Costs — Wasted Costs Orders

5576.text: The Court shall have power to disallow or, (as the case may be) order the legal or other representative concerned to meet, the whole of any wasted costs or such part of them as may be determined.

5578.text: The Court must give the legal representative a reasonable opportunity to attend a hearing to give reasons why it should not make such an order.

5580.text: When the Court makes a wasted costs order, it must:

5581.text: (1) specify the amount to be disallowed or paid; or

5582.text: (2) direct the Registrar to decide the amount of costs to be disallowed or paid.

5584.text: The Court may direct that notice must be given to the legal representative’s client, in such manner as the Court may direct:

5585.text: (1) of any hearing under Rule 38.84; or

5586.text: (2) of any order made against his legal representative .

5588.text: The Court may refer the question of wasted costs to the Registrar instead of making a wasted costs order.

5589.text: Costs Order In Favour Of A Party Represented Pro Bono

5591.text: Where a party (“the claiming party”) is represented by a law firm acting pro bono, the law firm may apply for an order that the other party (“the paying party”) make a payment in respect of the costs of legal services provided to the pro bono litigant at no charge (“a pro bono costs order”). The Court shall consider the application and assess the costs as if the claiming party were obliged to pay the fees of the law firm at the normal commercial rates charged by the law firm.

5593.text: Where the Court makes a pro bono costs order, such costs shall be assessed summarily by a Judge or the Registrar, unless a detailed assessment is necessary.

5595.text: Any costs payable pursuant to a pro bono costs order shall be paid to an account (“the Accou established by the Chief Justice by Practice Direction, for the following purposes:

5596.text: (1) Scholarships providing financial support to Emirati students studying law in the Emirate of Dubai and in need of such financial support;

5597.text: (2) The provision of a Pro Bono Clinic within the DIFC Courts;

5599.text: (4) Such other purposes as the Chief Justice may direct or order from time to time.

5601.text: The Account shall be administered by the signatories of the Account who are the Chief Justice, the Deputy Chief Justice, the Registrar and the Deputy Registrar in accordance with the purposes of the Account.

5603.text: Any individual, group or organisation may apply to the Account by way of letter stating the extent of support that is required and the reasons for requiring such support to the designated Pro Bono Programme Leader at the DIFC Courts Registry.

5605.text: All applications will be determined by the signatories of the Account. The Chief Justice will have the final determination on such applications. When making a decision on the allocation of funds, the signatories will have an absolute discretion and the signatories shall not accept any duty or liability to any individual, group or organisation seeking a distribution.

5607.text: The allocation of funds will be listed in available records, on the Court website and in its Annual Report.

5609.text: Where a pro bono costs order is sought, to assist the Court in making an immediate assessment of the amount payable to the Account, the pro bono litigant must prepare, file and serve in accordance with Rules 38.34 to 38.36 a written statement of the sum which the pro bono law firm would have claimed for legal representation had it not been provided free of charge.

5611.text: If a detailed assessment of costs is required then it shall be undertaken in accordance with Part 40 in the usual manner and the costs of the detailed assessment proceedings shall also be awarded to the Account.

5613.text: In proceedings where a party is represented pro bono the Court, wherever possible, shall reserve the issue of costs until the conclusion of the trial or the final hearing. At the conclusion of the trial or the final hearing, a single costs order may be made which reflects the overall justice of the case.

5616.text: A pro bono litigant may be entitled to a costs-free trial, where the pro bono litigant will not be obliged to meet the legal costs of the opposing party or parties even if the pro bono litigant is unsuccessful. The pro bono litigant will only be entitled to a costs-free trial once an application has been approved by the DIFC Courts Pro Bono Panel (the ‘Panel’).

5618.text: Applications for a costs-free trial should, save in exceptional circumstances, be submitted before the trial commences. Only those applications qualifying for pro bono assistance will be forwarded to the Panel for its consideration by the designated Programme Leader (chosen by the Chief Justice and responsible for all aspects of the operation and administration of the Pro Bono Programme).

5621.text: (1) should be supported by the pro bono lawyer of the applicant. The pro bono lawyer must certify that in their opinion there is a reasonable prospect of the applicant’s case succeeding; and

5622.text: (2) will be forwarded to the opposing party’s or parties’ lawyers for an opportunity to respond to the application and to provide any reasons why the applicant should not be permitted a costs-free trial.

5624.text: In determining the application, the Panel shall consider whether the applicant meets the threshold of:

5627.text: (3) such other criteria as the Panel may consider relevant from time to time.

5628.text: The Panel has absolute discretion as to whether an applicant meets the threshold for a costs-free trial. The Panel may decide that the applicant shall be entitled to costs protection in respect of any stage of proceedings, including any appeal.

5630.text: The designated Programme Leader shall ensure that a decision of the Panel that an applicant is entitled to a costs-free trial be delivered in a Court Order.

5632.text: A party may only appeal against an Order that an applicant is entitled to a costs-free trial:

5633.text: (1) to a judge of the Court; and

5634.text: (2), either prior to or during the course of the trial, save where, in exceptional circumstances, new information has come to light after the trial which affects or may affect the pro bono litigant’s eligibility for a costs-free trial.

5636.text: A pro bono litigant may at any time apply to the designated Programme Leader to have the Court fees suspended until the end of the case. The designated Programme Leader has absolute discretion in determining whether Court fees are to be suspended, and to what extent.

5638.text: Where the Court makes an order under Rule 38.88, the order must specify that the payment by the paying party must be made to the Account.

5640.text: The administrative costs and other expenses related to the office and to the management and investment of the fund will be borne by the Account.

39.1   
This Part sets out the amounts which, unless the Court orders otherwise, are to be allowed in respect of legal representatives’ charges in the cases to which this Part applies.

5650.text: (d) immediate judgment is given under Part 24;

5655.text: Any appropriate Court fee will be allowed in addition to the costs set out in this Part.

5657.text: The claim form may include a claim for fixed commencement costs.

5693.text: PART 40 Procedure For Detailed Assessment Of Costs

5695.text: I GENERAL RULES ABOUT DETAILED ASSESSMENT

5696.text: Time When Detailed Assessment May Be Carried Out

5698.text: The general rule is that the costs of any proceedings or any part of the proceedings are not to be assessed by the detailed procedure until the conclusion of the proceedings. However the Court may order them to be assessed immediately.

5699.text: No Stay Of Detailed Assessment Where There Is An Appeal

5701.text: Detailed assessment is not stayed pending an appeal unless the Court so orders.

5704.text: The Registrar or a judicial officer of the Court appointed for the purpose by the Chief Justice under Article 14 of the Court Law (referred to as “an authorised Court Officer ” in this Part) has all the powers of the Court when making a detailed assessment, except:

5705.text: (1) power to make a wasted costs order;

5706.text: (2) power to make an order under:

5707.text: (a) Rule 38.60 (powers in relation to misconduct); and

5708.text: (b) Rule 40.12 (sanction for delay in commencing detailed assessment proceedings).

5709.text: II COSTS PAYABLE BY ONE PARTY TO ANOTHER — COMMENCEMENT OF DETAILED ASSESSMENT PROCEEDINGS

5712.text: This section of Part 40 applies where the Registrar is to make a detailed assessment of costs which are payable by one party to another.

5715.text: Detailed assessment proceedings are commenced by the receiving party serving on the paying party :

5716.text: (1) notice of commencement in Form P40/01; and

5717.text: (2) a copy of the bill of costs.

5719.text: The receiving party must also serve a copy of the notice of commencement and the bill on any other relevant person including:

5720.text: (1) any person who has taken part in the proceedings which gave rise to the assessment and who is directly liable under an order for costs made against him;

5721.text: (2) any person who has given to the receiving party notice in writing that he has a financial interest in the outcome of the assessment and wishes to be a party accordingly; and/or

5722.text: (3) any other person whom the Court orders to be treated as such.

5724.text: Where a party is unsure whether a person is or is not a relevant person, that party may apply to the appropriate office for directions.

5726.text: The Court will generally not make an order that the person in respect of whom the application is made will be treated as a relevant person, unless within a specified time he applies to the Court to be joined as a party to the assessment proceedings in accordance with Part 20.
