5728.text: A person on whom a copy of the notice of commencement is served under Rule 40.6 is a party to the

5729.text: detailed assessment proceedings (in addition to the paying party and the receiving party).

5730.text: Period For Commencing Detailed Assessment Proceedings

5732.text: The following table shows the period for commencing detailed assessment proceedings.

5733.text:
Source of right to detailed assessmentTime by which detailed assessment proceedings must be commenced
Judgment, direction, order, award or other determination3 months after the date of the judgment etc. Where detailed assessment is stayed pending an appeal, 3 months after the date of the order lifting the stay
Discontinuance under Part 343 months after the date of service of notice of discontinuance under Rule 34.6; or 3 months after the date of the dismissal of application to set the notice of discontinuance aside under Rule 34.10
Acceptance of an offer to settle3 months after the date when the right to costs arose

5734.text: Sanction For Delay In Commencing Detailed Assessment Proceedings

5736.text: Where the receiving party fails to commence detailed assessment proceedings within the period specified:

5738.text: (2) by any direction of the Court ;

5739.text: the paying party may apply for an order requiring the receiving party to commence detailed assessment proceedings within such time as the Court may specify.

5741.text: On an application under Rule 40.11, the Court may direct that, unless the receiving party commences detailed assessment proceedings within the time specified by the Court , all or part of the costs to which the receiving party would otherwise be entitled will be disallowed.

5744.text: (1) the paying party has not made an application in accordance with Rule 40.11; and

5745.text: (2) the receiving party commences the proceedings later than the period specified in Rule 40.10;

5746.text: the Court may disallow all or part of the interest otherwise payable to the receiving party under Article 39 of the Court Law, No. 10 of 2004 but must not impose any other sanction except in accordance with Rule

5747.text: Points Of Dispute And Consequence Of Not Serving

5749.text: The paying party and any other party to the detailed assessment proceedings may dispute any item in the bill of costs by serving points of dispute on:

5751.text: (2) every other party to the detailed assessment proceedings.

5753.text: The period for serving points of dispute is 21 days after the date of service of the notice of commencement.

5755.text: If a party serves points of dispute after the period set out in Rule 40.15, he may not be heard further in the detailed assessment proceedings unless the Court gives permission.

5757.text: The receiving party may file a request for a default costs certificate if:

5758.text: (1) the period set out in Rule 40.15 for serving points of dispute has expired; and

5759.text: (2) he has not been served with any points of dispute.

5761.text: If any party (including the paying party ) serves points of dispute before the issue of a default costs certificate the Court may not issue the default costs certificate.

5764.text: If the paying party and the receiving party agree the amount of costs, either party may apply for a costs certificate (either interim or final) in the amount agreed.

5765.text: III COSTS PAYABLE BY ONE PARTY TO ANOTHER — DEFAULT PROVISIONS

5768.text: Where the receiving party is permitted by Rule 40.17 to obtain a default costs certificate, he does so by filing a request Form P40/02.

5771.text: A default costs certificate will include an order to pay the costs to which it relates.

5773.text: Where a receiving party obtains a default costs certificate, the costs payable to him for the commencement of detailed assessment proceedings shall be $\$ 105160$ .

5776.text: The Court must set aside a default costs certificate if the receiving party was not entitled to it.

5778.text: In any other case, the Court may set aside or vary a default costs certificate if it appears to the Court that there is some good reason why the detailed assessment proceedings should continue.

5781.text: (1) the receiving party has purported to serve the notice of commencement on the paying party ;

5782.text: (2) a default costs certificate has been issued; and

5783.text: (3) the receiving party subsequently discovers that the notice of commencement did not reach the paying party at least 21 days before the default costs certificate was issued;

5785.text: (a) file a request for the default costs certificate to be set aside ; or (b) apply to the Court for directions.

5787.text: Where Rule 40.25 applies, the receiving party may take no further step in:

5789.text: (2) the enforcement of the default costs certificate;

5790.text: until the certificate has been set aside or the Court has given directions.

5791.text: IV COSTS PAYABLE BY ONE PARTY TO ANOTHER — PROCEDUREWHERE POINTS OF DISPUTE ARE SERVED

5794.text: Where any party to the detailed assessment proceedings serves points of dispute, the receiving party may serve a reply on the other parties to the assessment proceedings.

5796.text: He may do so within 21 days after service on him of the points of dispute to which his reply relates.

5799.text: Where points of dispute are served in accordance with this Part, the receiving party must file a request for a detailed assessment hearing.

5801.text: He must file the request within 3 months of the expiry of the period for commencing detailed assessment proceedings as specified:

5803.text: (2) by any direction of the Court .

5805.text: Where the receiving party fails to file a request in accordance with Rule 40.30, the paying party may apply for an order requiring the receiving party to file the request within such time as the Court may specify.

5807.text: On an application under Rule 40.31, the Court may direct that, unless the receiving party requests a detailed assessment hearing within the time specified by the Court , all or part of the Costs to which the receiving party would otherwise be entitled will be disallowed.

5810.text: (1) the paying party has not made an application in accordance with Rule 40.31; and

5811.text: (2) the receiving party files a request for a detailed assessment hearing later than the period specified in Rule 40.30;

5812.text: the Court may disallow all or part of the interest otherwise payable to the receiving party under Article 39 of the Court Law, No. 10 of 2004 but must not impose any other sanction except in accordance with Rule 38.60.

5817.text: (3) any party who has served points of dispute under 40.14;

5818.text: may be heard at the detailed assessment hearing unless the Court gives permission.

5820.text: Only items specified in the points of dispute may be raised at the hearing, unless the Court gives permission.

5821.text: V INTERIM COSTS CERTIFICATE AND FINAL COSTS CERTIFICATE

5822.text: Power To Issue An Interim Certificate

5824.text: The Court may at any time after the receiving party has filed a request for a detailed assessment hearing:

5825.text: (1) issue an interim costs certificate for such sum as it considers appropriate;

5826.text: (2) amend or cancel an interim certificate.

5828.text: An interim certificate will include an order to pay the costs to which it relates, unless the Court orders otherwise.

5830.text: The Court may order the costs certified in an interim certificate to be paid into Court .

5833.text: In Rules 40.40 and 40.41, a completed bill means a bill calculated to show the amount due following the detailed assessment of the costs.

5835.text: The period for filing the completed bill is 14 days after the end of the detailed assessment hearing.

5837.text: When a completed bill is filed, the Court will issue a final costs certificate and serve it on the parties to the

5840.text: Rule 40.41 is subject to any order made by the Court that a certificate is not to be issued until other costs have been paid.

5842.text: A final costs certificate will include an order to pay the costs to which it relates, unless the Court orders otherwise.

5843.text: VI COSTS OF DETAILED ASSESSMENT PROCEEDINGS

5844.text: Liability For Costs Of Detailed Assessment Proceedings

5846.text: The receiving party is entitled to his costs of the detailed assessment proceedings except where:

5847.text: (1) the provisions of any enactment, any of these Rules or any relevant Practice Direction provide otherwise; or

5848.text: (2) the Court makes some other order in relation to all or part of the costs of the detailed assessment proceedings.

5850.text: In deciding whether to make some other order, the Court must have regard to all the circumstances, including:

5851.text: (1) the conduct of all the parties;

5852.text: (2) the amount, if any, by which the bill of costs has been reduced; and

5853.text: (3) whether it was reasonable for a party to claim the costs of a particular item or to dispute that item.

5854.text: Offers To Settle Without Prejudice Save As To Costs Of The Detailed Assessment Proceedings

5857.text: (1) a party (whether the paying party or the receiving party) makes a written offer to settle the costs of the proceedings which gave rise to the assessment proceedings; and

5858.text: (2) the offer is expressed to be without prejudice save as to the costs of the detailed assessment proceedings, the Court will take the offer into account in deciding who should pay the costs of those proceedings.

5860.text: The fact of the offer must not be communicated to the Court until the question of costs of the detailed assessment proceedings falls to be decided.

5861.text: VII APPEALS FROM AUTHORISED COURT OFFICERS IN DETAILED ASSESSMENT PROCEEDINGS

5864.text: An appeal against a decision of an authorised Court Officer in detailed assessment proceedings is to a Judge of the Court of First Instance.

5867.text: An appeal against a decision of an authorised Court Officer in detailed assessment proceedings should be made in accordance with the provisions of Part 44 of the Rules of Court.

5868.text: PART 41 Proceedings By Or Against The Centre, Its Bodies And The Government

5872.text: This Part contains rules for proceedings by or against the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government and civil proceedings to which the Centre or any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government is a party.

5873.text: Application Of The Rules Of The Dubai International Financial Centre Court

5875.text: These Rules apply to proceedings by or against the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government and other proceedings to which Centre, any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government is a party unless this Part, a Practice Direction or any other enactment provides otherwise.

5876.text: I ACTIONS INVOLVIN THE CENTRE, ANY OF THE CENTRE’S BODIES OR THE GOVERNMENT — GENERAL

5877.text: Action On Behalf Of The Centre, Any Of The Centre’S Bodies Or The Government

5879.text: Where by reason of a Rule, Practice Direction or Court order the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government is permitted or required:

5881.text: that function shall be performed by an appropriate officer acting on behalf of the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government .

5883.text: The Court may if necessary nominate an appropriate officer.

5884.text: Counterclaims, Other Additional Claims And Set-Off

5885.text: 41.5 In a claim by the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government for taxes, duties or penalties, the defendant cannot make a counterclaim or other additional claim or raise a defence of set-off.

5887.text: In any other claim by the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government , the defendant cannot make a counterclaim or other additional claim or raise a defence of set-off which is based on a claim for repayment of taxes, duties or penalties.

5889.text: In proceedings by in the name of the Attorney-General, no counterclaim or other additional can be made or defence of set-off raised without the permission of the Court .

5891.text: In proceedings by or against the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government in the name of one Centre Body or Government department, no counterclaim or other additional claim can be made or defence of set-off raised without the permission of the Court unless the subject-matter relates to that Centre Body or Government department.

5892.text: Enforcement Against The Centre, Any Of The Centre’S Bodies Or The Government

5894.text: Parts 45 to 51 do not apply to any order against the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government

5896.text: In Rule 41.9, ‘order against the Centre, any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government’ means any judgment or order against the Centre , any Centre Body, a Government department, or an officer of the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government as such, made:

5897.text:
(1) in civil proceedings by or against the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government ; (2) in proceedings under Part 42   
(3) in connection with an arbitration to which the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government is a party; or   
(4) in other civil proceedings to which the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government is a party.

5898.text: Money Due From The Centre, Any Of The Centre’S Bodies Or The Government

5901.text: (1) a third party debt order under Part 47;

5902.text: (2) an order for the appointment of a receiver under Part 49; or

5903.text: (3) an order for the appointment of a sequestrator under Rule 48.36;

5904.text: may be made or have effect in respect of any money due from the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government .

5906.text: In Rule 41.11, ‘money due from the Centre, any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government’ includes money accruing due, and money alleged to be due or accruing due.

5908.text: Where any money is payable by the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government to some person who, under any order of the Court , is liable to pay any money to any other person, and that other person would otherwise be entitled under these Rules to obtain an order for the attachment thereof as a debt due or accruing due, or an order for the appointment of a sequestrator or receiver to receive the money on his behalf, the Court may direct payment thereof into the Court , make an order restraining the first-mentioned person from receiving that money and directing payment thereof to that other person, or to the

5909.text: sequestrator or receiver, provided that no such order shall be made in respect of:

5910.text: (1) any wages or salary payable to any officer of the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government as such; or

5911.text: (2) any money which is subject to the provisions of any enactment prohibiting or restricting assignment or charging or taking in execution.

5913.text: An application for an order under Rule 41.13:

5914.text: (1) restraining a person from receiving money payable to him by the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government ; and

5915.text: (2) directing payment of the money to the applicant or another person;

5918.text: The application must be supported by written evidence setting out the facts on which it is based, and in particular identifying the debt from the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government .

5920.text: Notice of the application, with a copy of the written evidence, must be served:

5921.text: (1) on the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government ; and

5922.text: (2) on the person to be restrained;

5923.text: at least 7 days before the hearing.

5925.text: Rules 47.35 to 47.40 apply to an application under Rule 41.13 as they apply to an application under Rule 47.3 for a third party debt order, except that the Court will not have the power to order enforcement to issue against the Centre , any of the Centre’s Bodies or the Government .

5926.text: II SERVICE ON THE CENTRE AND ITS BODIES

5928.text: The persons to be served on behalf the Centre and its bodies, their addresses for service and manner(s) in which service must be effected are set out in Schedule A to this Part.

5931.text: Notice of intention to commence proceedings must be served on the Government at least 15 days before

5933.text: The persons to be served on behalf of the Government and its departments, their addresses for service and manner(s) in which service must be effected are set out in Schedule B to this Part.

5939.text: Scope Of This Part And Interpretation

5940.text: 42.1 This Part contains rules about judicial review.

5943.text: (1) a ‘claim for judicial review’ means a claim to review the lawfulness of a decision, action or failure to act in relation to the exercise of a public function;

5944.text: (2) ‘the judicial review procedure’ means the Part 8 procedure as modified by this Part; and

5945.text: (3) ‘interested party’ means any person (other than the claimant and defendant ) who is directly affected by the claim.

5946.text: When This Part Must Be Used

5948.text: The judicial review procedure must be used in a claim for judicial review where the claimant is seeking:

5949.text: (1) a mandatory order; (2) a prohibiting order; or

5952.text: When This Part May Be Used

5954.text: The judicial review procedure may be used in a claim for judicial review where the claimant is seeking:

5957.text: A claim for judicial review may include a claim for damages , restitution or the recovery of a sum due, but may not seek such a remedy alone.

5959.text: 42.6 The Court’s permission to proceed is required in a claim for judicial review .

5960.text: Time Limit For Filing Claim Form

5962.text: The claim form must be filed:

5963.text: (1) promptly; and (2) in any event not later than 3 months after the grounds to make the claim first arose.

5965.text: The time limit in Rule 42.7 may not be extended by agreement between the parties.

5967.text: Rule 42.7 does not apply when any other enactment specifies a shorter time limit for making the claim for judicial review .

5970.text: In addition to the matters set out in Rule 8.8 (contents of the claim form) the claimant must also state:

5971.text: (1) the name and address of any person he considers to be an interested party ; (2) that he is requesting permission to proceed with a claim for judicial review ; and

5972.text: (3) any remedy (including any interim remedy) he is claiming.

5974.text: Where the claim for judicial review relates to proceedings in a tribunal , any other parties to those proceedings must be named in the claim form as interested parties under Rule 42.10 (and therefore served with the claim form under Rule 42.17(2)).

5976.text: The claim form must include or be accompanied by:

5979.text: In addition, the claim form must be accompanied by:

5982.text: Where it is not possible to file all the above documents, the claimant must indicate which documents have not been filed and the reasons why they are not currently available.

5984.text: The claimant must file two copies of a paginated and indexed bundle containing all the documents referred to in Rules 42.12 and 42.13.

5986.text: Attention is drawn to Rules 8.23 and 8.29.

5989.text: The claim form must be served on:

5990.text: (1) the defendant ; and (2) unless the Court otherwise directs, any person the claimant considers to be an interested party ; within 7 days after the date of issue.

5992.text: Except as required by Rules 42.30 or 42.34, the Court will not serve documents and service must be effected by the parties.

5994.text: 42.19 Any person served with the claim form who wishes to take part in the judicial review must file an acknowledgment of service in Form P42/01 in accordance with Rules 42.20 to 42.23.

5996.text: Any acknowledgment of service must be:

5997.text: (1) filed not more than 21 days after service of the claim form; and

6000.text: (b) subject to any direction under 42.17(2), any other person named in the claim form;

6001.text: as soon as practicable and, in any event, not later than 7 days after it is filed.

6003.text: The time limits under Rule 42.20 may not be extended by agreement between the parties.

6006.text: (1) must: (a) where the person filing it intends to contest the claim, set out a summary of his grounds for doing so; and (b) state the name and address of any person the person filing it considers to be an interested party ;

6008.text: (2) may include or be accompanied by an application for directions.

6012.text: Attention is drawn to Rule 8.14 and to Rule 11.8.

6013.text: Failure To File Acknowledgment Of Service

6015.text: Where a person served with the claim form has failed to file an acknowledgment of service in accordance with Rule 42.19, he:

6016.text: (1) may not take part in a hearing to decide whether permission should be given unless the Court allows him to do so; but

6017.text: (2) provided he complies with Rule 42.41 or any other direction of the Court regarding the filing and service of:

6018.text: (a) detailed grounds for contesting the claim or supporting it on additional grounds; and

6020.text: may take part in the hearing of the judicial review.

6022.text: Where that person takes part in the hearing of the judicial review, the Court may take his failure to file an acknowledgment of service into account when deciding what order to make about costs.

6027.text: Where permission to proceed is given, the Court may also give directions.

6029.text: Directions under Rule 42.28 may include a stay of proceedings to which the claim relates and directions about serving the claim form and any evidence on other persons.

6030.text: Service Of Order Giving Or Refusing Permission

6033.text: (1) the order giving or refusing permission; and (2) any directions;

6037.text: An order refusing permission or giving it subject to conditions or on certain grounds only must set out or be accompanied by the Court’s reasons for coming to that decision.

6039.text: 42.32 The Court will normally consider the question of permission without a hearing.

6041.text: Rules 42.34 and 42.37 apply where the Court , without a hearing:

6042.text: (1) refuses permission to proceed; or (2) gives permission to proceed: (a) subject to conditions; or (b) on certain grounds only.

6044.text: The Court will serve its reasons for making the decision when it serves the order giving or refusing permission in accordance with Rule 42.30.

6046.text: The claimant may not appeal, but may request the decision to be reconsidered at a hearing.

6048.text: A request under Rule 42.35 must be filed within 7 days after service of the reasons under Rule 42.34.

6050.text: The claimant , defendant and any other person who has filed an acknowledgment of service will be given at least 2 days’ notice of the hearing date.

6053.text: Neither the defendant nor any other interested party need attend a hearing on the question of permission unless the Court directs otherwise.

6055.text: Where the defendant or any party does attend a hearing, the Court will not normally make an order for costs against the claimant .

6056.text: Defendant Etc. May Not Apply To Set Aside

6058.text: Neither the defendant nor any other person served with the claim form may apply to set aside an order giving permission to proceed.

6061.text: A defendant and any other person served with the claim form who wishes to contest the claim or support it on additional grounds must file and serve:

6062.text: (1) detailed grounds for contesting the claim or supporting it on additional grounds; and

6064.text: within 35 days after service of the order giving permission.

6066.text: Where the party filing the detailed grounds intends to rely on documents not already filed, he must file a paginated bundle of those documents when he files the detailed grounds.

6068.text: The following rules do not apply:

6069.text: (1) Rules 8.25 and 8.26 (defendant to file and serve written evidence at the same time as acknowledgment of service ); and

6070.text: (2) Rules 8.27 and 8.28 (claimant to file and serve any reply within 14 days).

6071.text: Where Claimant Seeks To Rely On Additional Grounds

6073.text: The Court’s permission is required if a claimant seeks to rely on grounds other than those for which he has been given permission to proceed.

6075.text: Where the claimant intends to apply to rely on additional grounds at the hearing of the claim for judicial review , he must give notice to the Court and to any other person served with the claim form no later than 7 clear days before the hearing (or the warned date where appropriate).

6080.text: No written evidence may be relied on unless:

6081.text: (1) it has been served in accordance with any:

6082.text: (a) Rule under this Part; or (b) direction of the Court ; or

6084.text: Court’S Powers To Hear Any Person

6086.text: Any person may apply for permission:

6087.text: (1) to file evidence; or (2) make representations at the hearing of the judicial review.

6089.text: An application under Rule 42.48 should be made promptly.

6091.text: Where all the parties consent, the Court may deal with an application under Rule 42.48 without a hearing.

6093.text: Where the Court gives permission for a person to file evidence or make representations at the hearing of

6094.text: the claim for judicial review , it may do so on conditions and may give case management directions.

6096.text: An application for permission should be made by letter to the Registry , identifying the claim, explaining who the applicant is and indicating why and in what form the applicant wants to participate in the hearing.

6098.text: If the applicant is seeking a prospective order as to costs, the letter should say what kind of order and on what grounds.

6100.text: Applications to intervene must be made at the earliest reasonable opportunity, since it will usually be essential not to delay the hearing.

6103.text: The claimant must file and serve a skeleton argument not less than 21 working days before the date of the hearing of the judicial review.

6105.text: The defendant and any other party wishing to make representations at the hearing of the judicial review must file and serve a skeleton argument not less than 14 working days before the date of the hearing of the judicial review (or the warned date).

6108.text: (1) a time estimate for the complete hearing, including delivery of judgment ;

6110.text: (3) a list of the legal points to be taken (together with any relevant authorities with page references to the passages relied on);

6111.text: (4) a chronology of events (with page references to the bundle of documents (see Rule 42.58);

6112.text: (5) a list of essential documents for the advance reading of the Court (with page references to the passages relied on) (if different from that filed with the claim form) and a time estimate for that reading; and

6113.text: (6) a list of persons referred to.

6114.text: Bundle Of Documents To Be Filed

6116.text: The claimant must file a paginated and indexed bundle of all relevant documents required for the hearing of the judicial review application when he files his skeleton argument.

6118.text: The bundle must also include those documents required by the defendant and any other party who is to make representations at the hearing.

6121.text: If the parties agree about the final order to be made in a claim for judicial review , the claimant must file at the Court a document (with 2 copies) signed by all the parties setting out the terms of the proposed agreed order together with a short statement of the matters relied on as justifying the proposed agreed order and copies of any authorities or statutory provisions relied on.

6123.text: The Court will consider the documents referred to in Rule 42.60 and will make the order if satisfied that the order should be made.

6125.text: If the Court is not satisfied that the order should be made, a hearing date will be set.

6127.text: Where the agreement relates to an order for costs only, the parties need only file a document signed by all the parties setting out the terms of the proposed order.

6128.text: Judicial Review May Be Decided Without A Hearing

6130.text: The Court may decide the claim for judicial review without a hearing where all the parties agree.

6131.text: Court’S Powers In Respect Of Quashing Orders

6133.text: Rule 42.66 applies where the Court makes a quashing order in respect of the decision to which the claim relates.

6136.text: (1) remit the matter to the decision-maker; and (2) direct it to reconsider the matter and reach a decision in accordance with the judgment of the Court .

6138.text: Where the Court considers that there is no purpose to be served in remitting the matter to the decisionmaker it may, subject to any statutory provision, take the decision itself.

6142.text: (1) order a claim to continue as if it had not been started under this Part; and (2) where it does so, give directions about the future management of the claim.

6146.text: 43.1 This Part contains rules about arbitration claims.

6147.text: 43.1A This part applies to Arbitration Division claims (“DIFC-ARB Claims”).

6152.text: (1) ‘the Arbitration Law’ means the Arbitration Law, DIFC Law No.1 of 2008; and (2) ‘Arbitration Claim Form’ means a claim form in Form P43/01;

6156.text: (a) any application to the Court under the Arbitration Law, including any application:

6157.text: (i) for an order under Article 12 of the Arbitration Law disapplying that Article;

6158.text: (ii) to dismiss or stay an action which is the subject of an arbitration agreement under Article 13 of the Arbitration Law;

6159.text: (iii) for an order under Article 14 of the Arbitration Law for an Order requiring disclosure of information relating to arbitral proceedings;

6160.text: (iv) to appoint or to facilitate the appointment of an Arbitral tribunal, to challenge the appointment of an Arbitrator or to terminate the mandate of an Arbitrator under Articles 17 to 20 of the Arbitration Law;

6161.text: (v) for an Order relieving an Arbitrator of any liability incurred by reason of his resignation under Article 21 of the Arbitration Law;

6162.text: (vi) to determine whether an Arbitral Tribunal has jurisdiction in respect of a dispute under Article 23 of the Arbitration Law;

6163.text: (vii) to enforce an interim measure made by an Arbitral Tribunal under Article 24 of the Arbitration Law;

6164.text: (viii) for an order from the Court issuing an interim measure under Article 24 of the Arbitration Law;

6165.text: (ix) to the Court for assistance in taking evidence for the purposes of an arbitration under Article 34 of the Arbitration Law;

6166.text: $( \mathsf { x } )$ to the Court of First Instance to determine the amount of fees and expenses properly payable to the Arbitral Tribunal under Article 39 of the Arbitration Law;

6167.text: (xi) to set aside an arbitral award under Article 41 of the Arbitration Law;

6168.text: (xii) for recognition or enforcement of an arbitral award under Article 42 of the Arbitration Law;

6170.text: (i) arbitration proceedings (whether started or not); or

6174.text: Except where Rule 43.4 or Rule 43.5 applies an arbitration claim must be started by the issue of an arbitration claim form in accordance with the Part 8 procedure.

6176.text: An application under Article 13 of the Arbitration Law to stay legal proceedings must be made by Application Notice in the proceedings.

6178.text: An application under Article 34 of the Arbitration Law to secure the attendance of a witness should be made in accordance with RDC Part 30.

6184.text: (b) any questions on which the Arbitral Tribunal seeks the decision of the Court;

6185.text: (2) give details of any arbitration award challenged by the Arbitral Tribunal, identifying which part or parts of the award are challenged and specifying the grounds for the challenge;

6186.text: (3) show that any statutory requirements have been met;

6187.text: (4) specify under which Article of the Arbitration Law the claim is made;

6188.text: (5) identify against which (if any) defendants a Costs Order is sought; and

6190.text: (a) the persons on whom the Arbitration Claim Form will be served, stating their role in the arbitration and whether they are defendants; or

6191.text: (b) that the claim is made without notice and the grounds relied on.

6193.text: Reference in the Arbitration Claim Form to a witness statement or affidavit filed in support of the claim is not sufficient to comply with the requirements of Rule 43.6.

6196.text: An Arbitration Claim Form must be served by the claimant.

6199.text:
(1) the Court orders otherwise; or   
(2) the Arbitration Claim Form seeks enforcement or recognition of an award under Part 4 of the   
Arbitration Law

6200.text: an Arbitration Claim Form must be served on the defendant within 30 days from the date of issue and Rule 7.20 is modified accordingly

6202.text: The rules governing service of the Claim Form are set out in RDC Part 9.

6204.text: The Court may exercise its powers under Rule 9.31 to permit service of an Arbitration Claim Form at the address of a party’s legal representative or other representative acting for him in the arbitration.

6207.text:
The claimant must file a Certificate of Service within 7 days of service of serving the Arbitration Claim Form.   
Rule 9.36 specifies what a Certificate of Service must show.

6209.text: Where the Arbitration Claim Form is served outside Dubai, the Arbitration Claim Form should be served in accordance with Section III of Part 9 of the RDC.

6212.text: Where an arbitration claim is made under Article 19(3) (challenging an Arbitrator), Article 20(1) (terminating an Arbitrator’s mandate) or Article 39(4) of the Arbitration Law (determination of fees and expenses payable to the arbitral tribunal), each Arbitrator must be a defendant.

6214.text: Where notice must be given to an Arbitrator or any other person it may be given by sending him a copy of

6218.text: Save where Rule 43.17 applies, where a party makes an Arbitration Claim, each of the other parties to the arbitration must be made a defendant to the Arbitration Claim.

6220.text: Where a party makes an Arbitration Claim for recognition or enforcement of an award under Part 4 of the Arbitration Law:

6221.text: (1) only the party against whom such an order is sought need be made a defendant to the Arbitration Claim; and

6222.text: (2) the Arbitration Claim may be made without notice.

6225.text: A defendant must file an Acknowledgment of Service of the Arbitration Claim Form in every case in Form P43/02.

6227.text: Where an Arbitration Claim Form is served within the DIFC or Dubai, the period for filing an Acknowledgment of Service is 14 days after the service of the Claim Form in accordance with RDC Rule 11.5.

6229.text: Where an Arbitration Claim Form is served outside Dubai, the period for filing an Acknowledgment of Service is 28 days after the service of the Claim Form in accordance with RDC Rule 9.56.

6232.text: Where an Arbitrator is sent a copy of an Arbitration Claim Form (including an Arbitration Claim Form sent under Rule 43.15), that arbitrator may:

6233.text: (1) apply to be made defendant; or (2) apply to make representations to the Court under Rule 43.22.

6235.text: An application under Rule 43.21(1) to be made a defendant:

6236.text: (1) must be served on the Arbitral Tribunal; but

6237.text: (2) need not be served on any other party.

6239.text: An Arbitrator may make representations by filing written evidence or in writing to the Court.

6241.text: Any Arbitrator filing written evidence or making representations to the Court may not be called to give oral evidence without the permission of the Court.

6242.text: Supply Of Documents From Court Records

6244.text: An Arbitration Claim Form may only be inspected with the permission of the Court.

6249.text: The claimant should apply for a hearing date as soon as possible after issuing an Arbitration Claim Form.

6251.text: When applying for a hearing date, the Arbitral Tribunal should provide to the Court an estimate for the length of the hearing.

6253.text: The directions set out in Rules 43.30 to 43.37 apply unless the Court orders otherwise.

6255.text: Where a claimant in an arbitration claim seeks to rely on written evidence, a copy of that evidence must be filed and served with the Arbitration Claim Form.

6257.text: A defendant who wishes to rely on evidence before the Court must file and serve his written evidence within 21 days after the date by which he was required to acknowledge service.

6259.text: A claimant who wishes to rely on evidence in reply to written evidence filed under Rule 43.31 must file and serve his written evidence within 7 days after service of the defendant’s evidence.

6261.text: Following exchange of written evidence, the parties should confirm or amend by agreement the estimate for the length of the hearing provided pursuant to RDC 43.28.

6263.text: Agreed indexed and paginated bundles of all the evidence and other documents to be used at the hearing must be prepared by the claimant.

6265.text: Not later than 5 days before the hearing date the claimant must file a complete set of the documents to be used.

6267.text: Not later than 2 days before the hearing date the claimant must file and serve:

6268.text: (1) a chronology of the relevant events cross-referenced to the bundle of documents;

6269.text: (2) (where necessary) a list of the persons involved; and (3) a skeleton argument which lists succinctly:

6270.text: (a) the issues which arise for decision; (b) the grounds of relief (or opposing relief) to be relied upon; (c) the submissions of fact to be made with the references to the evidence; and (d) the submissions of law with references to the relevant authorities.

6272.text: Not later than the day before the hearing date the defendant must file and serve a skeleton argument which lists succinctly:

6273.text: (1) the issues which arise for decision; (2) the grounds of relief (or opposing relief) to be relied upon; (3) the submissions of fact to be made with the references to the evidence; and (4) the submissions of law with references to the relevant authorities.

6278.text: The Court may order that an arbitration claim be heard either in public or in private.

6280.text: Rules 35.2 to 35.4 do not apply, save insofar as provided by Rule 43.41 below.

6282.text: (1) Subject to sub-rule (2) below, arbitral proceedings under the DIFC Arbitration Law 2008 in the DIFC Courts are to be heard otherwise than in open court.

6283.text: (2) The court may order those proceedings to be heard in open court —

6284.text: (a) on the application of any party; or

6285.text: (b) if, in any particular case, the court is satisfied that those proceedings ought to be heard in open court.

6286.text: (3) An order of the court under subsection (2) is not subject to appeal.

6288.text: (1) This Rule applies to arbitral proceedings under the DIFC Arbitration Law 2008 in the DIFC Courts heard otherwise than in open court (“closed court proceedings”).

6289.text: (2) The Court in which closed court proceedings are being heard must, on the application of any party, make a direction as to what information, if any, relating to the proceedings may be published.

6290.text: (3) The court must not make a direction permitting information to be published unless—

6291.text: (a) all parties agree that the information may be published; or

6292.text: (b) the court is satisfied that the information, if published, would not reveal any matter (including the identity of any party) that any party reasonably wishes to remain confidential.

6294.text: (a) the court gives a judgment in respect of closed court proceedings; and

6295.text: (b) the court considers that judgment to be of major legal interest, the court may direct that reports of the judgment may be published in law reports and professional publications.

6296.text: (5) If the court directs under sub-rule (4) that reports of a judgment may be published, but any party reasonably wishes to conceal any matter in those reports (including the fact that the party was such a party), the court must, on the application of the party—

6297.text: (a) make a direction as to the action to be taken to conceal that matter in those reports; and

6298.text: (b) if the court considers that a report published in accordance with the direction made under paragraph (5)(a) would still be likely to reveal that matter, direct that the report may not be published until after the end of a period, not exceeding 10 years, that the court may direct.

6299.text: (6) A direction of the court under this section is not subject to appeal.

6301.text: Having regard to the overriding objective the Court may decide particular issues without a hearing.

6302.text: Stay Or Dismissal Of Legal Proceedings

6304.text: An application notice seeking a stay or dismissal of legal proceedings under Article 13 of the Arbitration Law must be served on all parties to those proceedings in accordance with RDC Part 9.

6306.text: Where a question arises as to whether:

6307.text:
(1) an arbitration agreement has been concluded; or   
(2) the dispute which is the subject-matter of the proceedings falls within the terms of such an agreement;

6308.text: the Court may decide that question or give directions to enable it to be decided and may order the proceedings to be stayed pending its decision.

6311.text: An application to challenge an Arbitrator under Article 19(3) of the Arbitration Law must be made within 30 days after receipt of notice of the ruling by the Arbitral Tribunal rejecting the party’s challenge.

6312.text: Applications To Determine The Jurisdiction Of The Arbitral Tribunal

6314.text: An arbitration claim for the determination of a preliminary question as to the substantive jurisdiction of the Arbitral Tribunal under Article 23(3) of the Arbitration Law must be made within 30 days after receipt of notice of the ruling by the Arbitral Tribunal as a preliminary question that it has jurisdiction.

6317.text: An application to the Court to enforce an interim measure made by an Arbitral Tribunal or for an order from the Court issuing an interim measure under Article 24 of the Arbitration Law must be made in an Arbitration Claim Form.

6319.text: An application to enforce an interim measure made by an Arbitral Tribunal will not be granted unless the applicant files written evidence showing that the application is made with the written permission of the Arbitral Tribunal.

6321.text: An application for an order from the Court issuing an interim measure will be determined in accordance with the Rules set out in Part 25 of the RDC.

6324.text: An Arbitral Tribunal or a party to arbitral proceedings being conducted in the DIFC who wishes to rely on Article 34 of the Arbitration Law to secure the attendance of a witness must apply for a witness summons in accordance with RDC Part 30.

6326.text: A witness summons will not be issued on the application of a party to arbitral proceedings unless the applicant files written evidence showing that the application is made with the approval of the tribunal.

6328.text: Any other application made under Article 34 of the Arbitration Law for the assistance of the Court in taking evidence must be made in an Arbitration Claim Form.

6329.text: Applications To Set Aside An Award

6331.text: An application under Article 41 of the Arbitration Law to set aside an arbitral award must be made:

6332.text: (1) within 3 months from the date on which the party making the application received the award; or

6333.text: (2) if a request had been made under Article 40 of the Arbitration Law, within 3 months from the date on which that request was disposed of by the Arbitral Tribunal; or

6334.text: (3) within such longer period as the parties to the arbitration agree in writing.

6336.text: Where a party applies to set aside an arbitral award, the arbitration claim form must state:

6337.text: (1) the grounds under Article 41(2) of the Arbitration Law on which the party alleges that the award should be set aside; and

6338.text: (2) whether the Arbitral Tribunal requests that the setting aside proceedings be suspended under Article 41(4) of the Arbitration Law in order to give the Arbitral Tribunal an opportunity to resume the arbitral

6339.text: proceedings or to take such other action as may eliminate the grounds for setting aside.

6341.text: The written evidence in support of the application must set out any evidence relied on by the party for the purpose of satisfying the Court:

6342.text: (1) of the matters referred to in Article 41(2) of the Arbitration Law; and

6343.text: (2) that the award should be set aside.

6345.text: The written evidence filed by the respondent to the application must:

6346.text: (1) state the grounds on which the Respondent opposes the award being set aside;

6347.text: (2) state whether the Respondent requests that the setting aside proceedings be suspended under Article 41(4) of the Arbitration Law in order to give the Arbitral Tribunal an opportunity to resume the arbitral proceedings or to take such other action as may eliminate the grounds for setting aside; and

6348.text: (3) set out any evidence relied on by him relating to the matters mentioned in Article 41(2) of the Arbitration Law.

6350.text: On receipt of an application to set aside an arbitration award under Article 41(2) of the Arbitration Law the Court may give such directions as it considers necessary.

6352.text: If the Court considers it appropriate to suspend the setting aside proceedings in accordance with a request made under Rule 43.55(2) or 43.57(2) it may suspend the setting aside proceedings for a period of time determined by it.

6355.text: No appeal lies from a decision of the Court under Articles 17(3), 17(4), 19(3), 20(1), 21(2) or 23(3) of the Arbitration Law.

6356.text: II RECOGNITION AND ENFORCEMENT OF ARBITRAL AWARDS

6358.text: Rules 43.62 to 43.74 apply to awards made in arbitration proceedings wherever the seat.

6360.text: An application under Article 42(1) of the Arbitration Law to enforce an award or under Article 43 of the

6361.text: Arbitration Law for recognition of an award may be made without notice in an Arbitration Claim Form.

6363.text: The Court may specify parties to the arbitration on whom the Arbitration Claim Form must be served, for example where the relevant time period for setting aside the award from the date of service of the award on the losing party has not yet expired, or there is some doubt about the validity of the award or its service upon the respondent.

6365.text: The parties on whom the Arbitration Claim Form is served must acknowledge service and the recognition or enforcement proceedings will continue as an arbitration claim.

6367.text: The Arbitration Claim Form may be served out of the jurisdiction irrespective of where the award was, or is treated to have been, made.

6369.text: The application must be supported by written evidence:

6373.text: or copies of those documents certified in accordance with Article 42(3) of the Arbitration Law;

6374.text: (2) (if the award or agreement is not made in English), producing a translation of the award or agreement certified in accordance with Article 42(3) of the Arbitration Law;

6375.text: (3) stating the name and the usual or last known place of residence or business of the parties or, if a party is a body corporate, its registered or principal address;

6376.text: (4) (in the case of an application to enforce an award) stating either:

6377.text: (a) that the award has not been complied with; or (b) the extent to which it has not been complied with at the date of the application.

6379.text: A draft order in both English and Arabic must accompany the application unless the Court orders otherwise.

6381.text: The Order enforcing or recognising the award must be served on the defendant in accordance with RDC Part 9.

6383.text: The Order may be served outside Dubai:

6384.text: (1) without permission; and (2) in accordance with Section III of Part 9 of the RDC as if the Order were an Arbitration Claim Form.

6386.text: Within 14 days after service of an Order made without notice or, if the Order is to be served outside Dubai, within such other period as the Court may set:

6387.text:
(1) the defendant may apply to set aside the Order; and   
(2) the award must not be enforced until after: (a) the end of that period; or (b) any application made by the defendant within that period has been finally disposed of.

6389.text: An Order made without notice must contain a statement of

6390.text: (1) the right to make an application to set the Order aside; and (2) the restrictions on enforcement under Rule 43.70(2).

6393.text: (1) be made in accordance with RDC Part 23;

6394.text: (2) set out the grounds under Article 44(1) of the Arbitration Law on which the applicant alleges that the Order should be set aside;

6395.text: (3) set out any grounds under Article 44(2) of the Arbitration Law on which the applicant alleges that the decision to set aside the Order should be adjourned;

6396.text: (4) be accompanied by written evidence setting out any evidence relied on by the party for the purpose of satisfying the Court:

6397.text: (a) of the matters referred to in Article 44 of the Arbitration Law; and (b) that the Order should be set aside.

6399.text: If the Respondent to the application wishes to rely on evidence in reply to written evidence filed under Rule 43.72(4) he must file and serve his written evidence within 7 days after service of the Applicant’s evidence.

6401.text: The written evidence filed by the Respondent to the application must:

6402.text: (1) state the grounds on which the Respondent opposes the recognition or enforcement of the award being set aside; and

6403.text: (2) set out any evidence relied on by him relating to:

6404.text: (a) the matters referred to in Article 44(1) of the Arbitration Law; and (b) the provision by the applicant of appropriate security in accordance with Article 44(2).

6406.text: After conclusion of the period referred to Rule 43.70(2), in relation to any award in respect of which the Court has made an Order enforcing the award:

6407.text: (1) the award may be enforced within the DIFC in the same manner as a Judgment or Order of the Court to the same effect; and

6408.text: (2) the Court may enter Judgment in the terms of the award.

6411.text: Where an applicant seeks to enforce an award of interest the whole or any part of which relates to a period after the date of the award, he must file a statement giving the following particulars:

6414.text: A statement under Rule 43.76 must be filed whenever the amount of interest has to be quantified for the purpose of:

6415.text: (1) obtaining a Judgment or Order under Rule 43.75; or

6416.text: (2) enforcing such a Judgment or Order.

6419.text: These Rules Will Apply To Judgments And Orders Issued On Or After 25 May 2017.

6420.text: Scope Of This Part And Interpretation

6422.text: The Rules in this Part apply to appeals to:

6423.text: (1) the Court of Appeal; and (2) the Court of First Instance.

6426.text: (1) ‘appeal Court’ means the Court to which an appeal is made;

6427.text: (2) ‘lower Court’ means the Court, tribunal or other person or body from whose decision an appeal is brought;

6428.text: (3) unless the use or context otherwise requires, ‘decision’ means an order or direction or, where a matter is to be disposed of otherwise than by order or direction, the pronouncement of the disposal;

6429.text: (4) ‘appellant’ means a person who brings or seeks to bring an appeal;

6431.text: (a) a person other than the appellant who was a party to the proceedings in the lower Court and who is affected by the appeal; and

6432.text: (b) a person who is permitted by the appeal Court to be a party to the appeal; and

6433.text: (6) ‘appeal notice’ means an appellant’s or respondent’s notice.

6435.text: This Part is subject to any Rule, enactment or Practice Direction which sets out special provisions with regard to any particular category of appeal.

6437.text:
Unless the appeal Court or the lower Court orders otherwise, an appeal shall not operate as a stay of any decision of the lower Court.   
Permission to appeal

6439.text: An appellant or respondent requires permission to appeal:

6440.text: (1) where the appeal is to the Court of Appeal, except where the appeal is against a committal order;

6441.text: (2) where the appeal is to the Court of First Instance, except where the appeal is against:

6442.text: (a) a refusal by the Registrar of Companies to grant authorisation to transfer incorporation under Article 122(1) of the Companies Law (Amended and Restated), DIFC Law 3 of 2006; or

6443.text: (b) a refusal by the Registrar of Companies to grant authorisation to transfer a Limited Partnership under Article 63(1) of the Limited Partnership Law, DIFC Law 4 of 2006.

6446.text: An appellant’s application for permission to appeal must be made to the lower Court:

6447.text: (1) orally at the hearing at which the decision to be appealed was made; or

6450.text: Where an oral application as referred to in Rule 44.6(1) above has not been made, but a party requests further time to make such an application, the lower Court may adjourn the hearing to give that party the opportunity to do so.

6452.text: The lower Court may refer an application for permission to appeal to the appeal Court for decision.

6454.text: Where the lower Court refuses permission to appeal, a further application for permission to appeal may be made to the appeal Court in an appellant’s notice.

6457.text: The appellant must file the appellant’s notice as referred to in Rule 44.6(2):

6458.text: (1) within such period as may be directed by the lower Court; or

6459.text: (2) where the lower Court makes no such direction, within 21 days after the date of the decision.

6461.text: The appellant must file the appellant’s notice as referred to in Rule 44.9:

6462.text:
(1) within such period as may be directed by the lower Court; or   
(2) where the lower Court makes no such direction; within 21 days after receipt of the notification of the decision refusing permission to appeal.

6464.text: The parties may not agree to extend the time for appeal.

6466.text: Where the time for appeal has expired, the appellant must file the appellant’s notice and include therein –

6467.text: (1) an application for an extension of time; and (2) a statement of the reason for the delay and the steps taken prior to the application being made.

6470.text: (1) A respondent may make written submissions in opposition to an application for permission to appeal.

6471.text: (2) A respondent wishing to make submissions in opposition to permission to appeal must file and serve the submissions:

6472.text: (a) within 21 days of the service upon him of the appellant’s notice; or,

6473.text: (b) in the event that the grounds of appeal and/or skeleton argument are filed within 21 days of the date of the appellant’s notice pursuant to RDC 44.30, within 21 days of the service upon him of the appellant’s grounds of appeal and/or skeleton argument. (see RDC 44.34).

6476.text: An application for permission to appeal referred to the appeal Court by the lower Court or made to the appeal Court in an appellant’s notice may not be decided by the Judge against whose decision permission to appeal is sought.

6478.text: An application for permission to appeal not made orally to the lower Court at the hearing will ordinarily be decided without an oral hearing.

6480.text: The appellant may request that the application for permission to appeal be considered at an oral hearing, supported by grounds as to why it would be in the interests of justice to do so.

6482.text: The lower Court or the appeal Court may direct:

6483.text:
(1) the filing of further submissions by the appellant or any respondent;   
(2) that the application for permission to appeal be considered at an oral hearing.

6485.text: Permission to appeal may only be given where the lower Court or the appeal Court considers that:

6486.text: (1) the appeal would have a real prospect of success; or (2) there is some other compelling reason why the appeal should be heard.

6488.text: If permission to appeal is granted without an oral hearing, the parties will be notified of that decision.

6490.text:
If permission to appeal is refused without an oral hearing, the parties will be notified of that decision and brief reasons for it.   
Limited permission

6492.text: An order giving permission to appeal may:

6493.text: (1) limit the issues to be heard; and (2) be made subject to conditions.

6495.text: Where the lower Court or the appeal Court gives permission to appeal on some issues only, it will:

6496.text:
(1) refuse permission to appeal on any remaining issues; or   
(2) reserve the question of permission to appeal on any remaining issues to the Court hearing the appeal.

6498.text: If the lower Court reserves the question of permission to appeal under Rule 44.23(2), the appellant must, within 14 days after receipt of the notification of the decision, inform the appeal Court and the respondent

6499.text: in writing whether he intends to pursue the reserved issues. If the appellant does intend to pursue the reserved issues, the parties must include in any time estimate for the appeal hearing, their time estimate for the reserved issues.

6502.text: The lower Court or the appeal Court will normally allow the respondent his costs of an application for permission to appeal if permission to appeal is refused.

6505.text: Case management decisions include decisions made under Rule 4.2 and decisions about:

6508.text: Where the application is for permission to appeal from a case management decision, the lower Court or the appeal Court deciding the application may take into account whether:

6512.text: An appellant’s notice must be filed in all cases other than an application for permission to appeal made orally as referred to in Rule 44.6(1).

6514.text: Subject to Rule 44.30, an appellant’s notice must:

6515.text: (1) set out the grounds of appeal relied on and

6516.text: (2) include or be accompanied by a skeleton argument.

6518.text: Where it is impracticable to comply with Rule 44.29, a statement of the grounds of appeal and the skeleton argument must be filed within 21 days of filing the appellant’s notice.

6521.text: (1) set out clearly the reasons why it is said the decision of the lower Court was:

6523.text: (b) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings in the lower Court;

6524.text: (2) specify, in respect of each ground, whether the ground raises an appeal on a point of law or is an appeal against a finding of fact; and

6525.text: (3) state the orders sought on appeal.

6527.text: An appellant’s notice which includes a skeleton argument or a skeleton argument filed in compliance with Rule 44.30 must be accompanied by –

6528.text: (1) where an application for permission to appeal is made in an appellant’s notice filed pursuant to Rule 44.6(2), any witness statement and affidavit in support of the application;

6529.text: (2) where an application for permission to appeal is made in an appellant’s notice filed pursuant to Rule 44.9 –

6530.text: (a) a copy of the order refusing permission to appeal, together with a copy of the Judge’s reasons for refusing permission to appeal;

6531.text: (b) the claim form and statements of case (where relevant to the subject of the appeal);

6532.text: (c) a suitable record of the judgment;

6533.text: (d) a copy or record of the decision appealed from;

6534.text: (e) those parts of the documentary evidence and any transcript of evidence reasonable considered necessary to enable the appeal Court to decide whether or not to grant permission to appeal; and

6535.text: (f) any witness statement and affidavit in support of the application.

6537.text: Where the lower Court refers an application for permission to appeal to the appeal Court for decision, the appellant must file the documents referred to in Rule 44.32(2) within 14 days of receipt of notification of

6540.text: Unless the Court otherwise orders, the appellant must serve on each respondent – (a) a sealed copy of the appellant’s notice; (b) copies of any statement of the grounds of appeal and any skeleton argument; and (c) copies of the documents accompanying the appellant’s notice or the skeleton argument as referred to in Rule 44.32 as soon as practicable and in any event within 7 days after they are filed.

6542.text: The appellant must, as soon as practicable, file a certificate of service of the documents referred to in Rule 44.34.

6544.text: The Court may dispense with the requirement for service of the appellant’s notice on a respondent. Any application notice seeking an order under Rule 9.32 to dispense with service should set out the reasons relied on and be verified by a statement of truth.

6547.text: An application to be made to the appeal Court for a remedy incidental to the appeal may be included in the appellant’s notice or made in a Part 23 application notice .

6549.text: Where an application for a remedy incidental to the appeal is included in the appellant’s notice, the rules relating to Part 23 application notices apply mutatis mutandis to the application.

6552.text: (1) Where permission to appeal is not required or has been given, the appellant must file an appeal bundle not less than 21 days before the appeal hearing and serve a copy of the appeal bundle on each respondent at the same time as he files it.

6553.text: (2) The appellant must provide two additional copies of the appeal bundle for the appeal Court.

6555.text: (a) a sealed copy of the appellant’s notice;

6556.text: (b) a sealed copy of the decision appealed from;

6557.text: (c) a copy of any order giving or refusing permission to appeal, together with a copy of the Judge’s reasons for allowing or refusing permission to appeal;

6558.text: (d) any affidavit or witness statement filed in support of any application included in the appellant’s notice;

6559.text: (e) a copy of his skeleton argument;

6560.text: (f) the claim form and statements of case (where relevant to the subject of the appeal);

6561.text: (g) a suitable record of the judgment, and those parts of any transcript of evidence which are directly relevant to any question at issue on the appeal;

6562.text: (h) those parts of any documentary evidence and any transcript of evidence reasonably considered necessary to enable the appeal Court to decide the appeal;

6563.text: (i) any application notice (or case management documentation) relevant to the subject of the appeal;

6564.text: (j) in the case of judicial review or a statutory appeal, the original decision which was the subject of the application to the lower Court;

6565.text: (k) in cases where the appeal is from a tribunal, a copy of the tribunal’s reasons for the decision, a copy of the decision reviewed by the tribunal and the reasons for the original decision and any document filed with the tribunal setting out the grounds of appeal from that decision;

6566.text: (l) any other documents which the appellant reasonably considers necessary to enable the appeal Court to decide the appeal and any application; and

6567.text: (m) such other documents as the Court may direct.

6569.text: All documents that are extraneous to the issues to be considered must be excluded. The appeal bundle may include affidavits, witness statements, summaries, experts’ reports and exhibits but only where these are directly relevant to the subject matter of the appeal.

6571.text: The appeal bundle must contain a certificate signed by the appellant’s legal representatives to the effect that they have read and understood Rules 44.39 and 44.40 and that the composition of the appeal bundle complies with it.

6573.text: Where it is not possible to file all the above documents, the appellant must indicate which documents have not yet been filed and the reasons why they are not currently available. The appellant must then provide a reasonable estimate of when the missing document or documents can be filed and file them as soon as reasonably practicable.

6576.text: In cases where the appeal bundle comprises more than 500 pages, exclusive of transcripts, the appellant’s legal representatives must, after consultation with the respondent’s legal representatives, also prepare and file with the Court, in addition to copies of the appeal bundle (as amended in accordance with Rule 44.89) the requisite number of copies of a core bundle.

6578.text: The core bundle must be filed within 28 days of receipt of the order giving permission to appeal or, where permission to appeal was granted by the lower Court or is not required, within 28 days of the date of service of the appellant’s notice on the respondent.

6581.text: (1) must contain the documents which are central to the appeal; and

6585.text: Rules 44.47 to 44.58 apply to the preparation of appeal bundles, supplemental respondents’ bundles where the parties are unable to agree amendments to the appeal bundle, and core bundles.

6588.text: Where documents are copied unnecessarily or bundled incompletely, costs may be disallowed. Where the provisions of this Part as to the preparation or delivery of bundles are not followed the bundle may be rejected by the Court or be made the subject of a special costs order.

6591.text: No more than one copy of any document should be included unless there is a good reason for doing otherwise (such as the use of a separate core bundle in Rule 44.45).

6594.text: The following rules regarding pagination shall apply:

6595.text: (1) Bundles must be paginated, each page being numbered individually and consecutively. The pagination used at trial must also be indicated. Letters and other documents should normally be included in chronological order. (An exception to consecutive page numbering arises in the case of core bundles where it may be preferable to retain the original numbering).

6596.text: (2) Page numbers should be inserted in bold figures at the bottom of the page and in a form that can be clearly distinguished from any other pagination on the document.

6599.text: The following rules regarding format and presentation shall apply:

6600.text: (1) Where possible the documents should be in A4 format and may be printed single or double sided (unless the Court directs a particular format). Where a document has to be read across rather than down the page, it should be so placed in the bundle as to ensure that the text starts nearest the spine.

6601.text: (2) Where any marking or writing in colour on a document is important, the document must be copied in colour or marked up correctly in colour.

6602.text: (3) Documents which are not easily legible should be transcribed and the transcription marked and placed adjacent to the document transcribed.

6603.text: (4) Documents in a language other than English should be translated and the translation marked and placed adjacent to the document translated. The translation should be agreed or, if it cannot be agreed, each party’s proposed translation should be included.

6604.text: (5) The size of any bundle should be tailored to its contents. A large lever arch file should not be used for just a few pages nor should files be overloaded.

6605.text: (6) Where it will assist the Court, different sections of the file may be separated by cardboard or other tabbed dividers so long as these are clearly indexed. Where, for example, a document is awaited when the appeal bundle is filed, a single sheet of paper can be inserted after a divider, indicating the nature of the document awaited. For example, ‘Transcript of evidence of Mr J Smith (to follow)’.

6608.text: The following rules regarding binding shall apply:

6609.text: (1) All documents, with the exception of transcripts, must be bound together. This may be in a lever arch file, ring binder or plastic folder. Plastic sleeves containing loose documents must not be used. Binders and files must be strong enough to withstand heavy use.

6610.text: (2) Large documents such as plans should be placed in an easily accessible file. Large documents which will need to be opened up frequently should be inserted in a file larger than A4 size.

6613.text: The following rules regarding indices and labels shall apply:

6614.text: (1) An index must be included at the front of the bundle listing all the documents and providing the page references for each. In the case of documents such as letters, invoices or bank statements, they may be given a general description.

6615.text: (2) Where the bundles consist of more than one file, an index to all the files should be included in the first file and an index included for each file. Indices should, if possible, be on a single sheet. The full name of the case should not be inserted on the index if this would waste space. Documents should be identified briefly but properly.

6618.text: The following rules regarding identification shall apply:

6619.text: (1) Every bundle must be clearly identified, on the spine and on the front cover, with the name of the case and the Court’s reference. Where the bundle consists of more than one file, each file must be numbered on the spine, the front cover and the inside of the front cover.

6620.text: (2) Outer labels should use large lettering e.g. ‘Appeal Bundle A’ or ‘Core Bundle’. The full title of the appeal and legal representatives’ names and addresses should be omitted. A label should be used on the front as well as on the spine.

6623.text: All staples, heavy metal clips, etc., must be removed.

6626.text: The following rules regarding statements of case shall apply:

6627.text: (1) Statements of case should be assembled in ‘chapter’ form — i.e. claim followed by particulars of claim, followed by further information, irrespective of date.

6628.text: (2) Redundant documents, e.g. particulars of claim overtaken by amendments, requests for further information recited in the answers given, should generally be excluded.

6631.text: The following rules regarding new documents shall apply:

6632.text: (1) Before a new document is introduced into bundles which have already been delivered to the Court, steps should be taken to ensure that it carries an appropriate bundle/page number so that it can be added to the Court documents. It should not be stapled and it should be prepared with punch holes for immediate inclusion in the binders in use.

6633.text: (2) If it is expected that a large number of miscellaneous new documents will from time to time be introduced, there should be a special tabbed empty loose-leaf file for that purpose. An index should be produced for this file, updated as necessary.

6636.text: Since correspondence between legal representatives is unlikely to be required for the purposes of an appeal, only those letters which will need to be referred to should be copied.

6639.text: If the appellant fails to comply with the requirements as to the provision of bundles of documents, the application or appeal will be referred for consideration to be given as to why it should not be dismissed for failure so to comply.

6642.text: A skeleton argument filed on behalf of the appellant should contain in paragraph 1 the legal representatives’ time estimate for the hearing of the appeal.

6644.text: A skeleton argument must contain a numbered list of the points which the party wishes to make. These should both define and confine the areas of controversy. Each point should be stated as concisely as the nature of the case allows.

6646.text: Skeleton arguments submitted in the context of appeals and supplementary skeleton arguments for appeals are not to exceed 35 pages in length. Requests for permission to submit lengthier skeleton arguments should be made via letter application.

6648.text: A numbered point must be followed by a reference to any document on which the party wishes to rely.

6650.text: A skeleton argument must state, in respect of each authority cited:

6651.text: (1) the proposition of law that the authority demonstrates; and (2) the parts of the authority (identified by page or paragraph references) that support the proposition.

6653.text: If more than one authority is cited in support of a given proposition, the skeleton argument must briefly state the reason for taking that course.

6655.text: The statement referred to in Rule 44.64 should not materially add to the length of the skeleton argument but should be sufficient to demonstrate, in the context of the argument:

6656.text: (1) the relevance of the authority or authorities to that argument; and

6657.text: (2) that the citation is necessary for a proper presentation of that argument.

6659.text: The cost of preparing a skeleton argument which:

6660.text: (1) does not comply with the requirements set out in Rules 44.59 to 44.64; or

6661.text: (2) was not filed within the time limits provided by this Part (or any further time granted by the Court);

6662.text: Will Not Be Allowed On Assessment Except To The Extent That The Court Otherwise Directs.

6664.text: The appellant should consider what other information the appeal Court will need. This may include a list of persons who feature in the case or glossaries of technical terms. A chronology of relevant events will be necessary in most appeals.

6667.text: A suitable record of the judgment is –

6668.text: (1) where the Court has issued the judgment sealed by the Court, a copy of the judgment;

6669.text: (2) In other cases of Court judgments –

6670.text: (a) where the judgment has been officially recorded by the Court, a transcript of that record certified by the appellant’s legal representative as a correct record;

6671.text: (b) subject to Rule 44.68(3), where the judgment has not been officially recorded by the Court, a note of the judgment agreed between the legal representatives of the appellant and the respondent approved by the Judge whose decision is being appealed.

6672.text: (3) If the parties cannot agree on a single note of the judgment, both versions should be provided to the judge with an explanatory letter. For the purposes of an application for permission to appeal the note need not be agreed by the respondent or approved by the Judge.

6673.text: (4) In tribunal cases, a sealed copy of the tribunal’s reasons for the decision.

6675.text: An appellant may not be able to obtain a suitable record of the lower Court’s judgment within the time within which the appellant’s notice must be filed. In such cases the appellant’s notice must still be completed to the best of the appellant’s ability on the basis of the documentation available. However it may be amended subsequently with the permission of the appeal Court.

6677.text: When the evidence is relevant to the appeal an official transcript of the relevant evidence must be obtained. Transcripts or notes of evidence are generally not needed for the purpose of determining an application for permission to appeal.

6679.text: If evidence relevant to the appeal was not officially recorded, a typed version of the Judge’s notes of evidence must be obtained.

6680.text: Procedure After Permission To Appeal Is Given

6682.text: Rules 44.73 to 44.74 set out the procedure where permission to appeal is given.

6684.text: The appeal Court will send the parties

6685.text: (1) notification of the date of the hearing; (2) a copy of the order giving permission to appeal; and (3) any other directions given by the Court.

6688.text: The legal representatives who will argue the appeal must provide a time estimate within 14 days of the appellant receiving the order giving permission to appeal. It should exclude the time required by the

6689.text: Court to give judgment. If the respondent disagrees with the time estimate, the respondent must inform the Court within 7 days of receipt of the estimate. In the absence of such notification the respondent will be deemed to have accepted the estimate proposed on behalf of the appellant.

6692.text: A respondent may file and serve a respondent’s notice –

6693.text:
(1) applying for permission to appeal; or   
(2) asking the appeal Court to uphold the decision of the lower Court for reasons different from or additional to those given by the lower Court.

6695.text: (1) A respondent who wishes to ask the appeal Court to vary the decision of the lower Court in any way must appeal and permission will be required on the same basis as for an appellant.

6696.text: (2) A respondent’s application for permission to appeal must be made to the appeal Court.

6697.text: (3) The rules in relation to applying for permission to appeal in an appellant’s notice and decision of the application apply mutatis mutandis to an application for permission to appeal in a respondent’s notice.

6699.text: A respondent who wishes only to request that the appeal Court upholds the decision of the lower Court for reasons different from or additional to those given by the lower Court does not appeal and does not require permission to appeal.

6701.text: If the respondent does not file a respondent’s notice, he will not be entitled, except with the permission of the Court, to rely on any reason not relied on in the lower Court.

6704.text: A respondent’s notice must be filed within:

6705.text: (1) such period as may be directed by the Court; or (2) where the Court makes no such direction, 21 days after the date in Rule 44.80.

6707.text: The date referred to in Rule 44.79 is –

6708.text: (1) where permission to appeal is not required, the date the respondent is served with the appellant’s

6711.text: (a) the date the respondent receives a copy of the order giving permission to appeal; or (b) the date the respondent receives notification that the application for permission to appeal and the appeal itself are to be heard together.

6713.text: Rule 44.13 (extension for time for appeal) applies mutatis mutandis to a respondent and a respondent’s notice. Accordingly, where an extension of time is required the extension must be requested in the respondent’s notice and the reasons why the respondent failed to act within the specified time must be included.

6715.text: The respondent must file a skeleton argument in all cases where he proposes to address arguments to the Court. The respondent’s skeleton argument may be included within a respondent’s notice.

6717.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise a respondent’s notice must be served on the appellant and any other respondent:

6719.text: (2) in any event not later than 7 days;

6723.text: (1) files a respondent’s notice; but (2) does not include his grounds of appeal and skeleton argument within that notice;

6724.text: may file his grounds of appeal and skeleton argument accompanying the notice but in any event must file his skeleton argument within 21 days of filing the notice.

6726.text: A respondent who does not file a respondent’s notice but who files a skeleton argument must file that skeleton argument no later than 28 days after the appellant’s skeleton has been filed.

6732.text: at the same time as he files it at the Court; and
