6736.text: A respondent’s skeleton argument must conform to the directions at Rules 44.59 to 44.67 with any necessary modifications. It should, where appropriate, answer the arguments set out in the appellant’s skeleton argument.

6739.text: A respondent may include an application within a respondent’s notice in accordance with Rule 44.37 above.

6742.text: If the respondent wishes to rely on any documents which he reasonably considers necessary to enable the appeal Court to decide the appeal and any application in addition to those filed by the appellant, he must make every effort to agree amendments to the appeal bundle with the appellant.

6744.text: If the legal representatives of the parties are unable to reach agreement, the respondent may prepare a supplemental bundle.

6746.text: If the respondent prepares a supplemental bundle he must file three copies of it within 21 days after he is served with the appeal bundle, and must provide two additional copies for the appeal Court.

6748.text: The respondent must serve any supplemental bundle on the appellant and any other respondent at the same time as he files it.

6751.text: An appeal notice may not be amended without the permission of the appeal Court. An application to amend and any application in opposition will normally be dealt with at the appeal hearing unless that course would cause unnecessary expense or delay in which case a request should be made for the application to amend to be heard in advance.

6752.text: Striking Out Appeal Notices And Setting Aside Or Imposing Conditions On Permission To Appeal

6757.text: The Court will only exercise its powers under Rule 44.94 where there is a compelling reason for doing so.

6759.text: Where a party was present at an oral hearing at which permission to appeal was given he may not subsequently apply for an order that the Court exercise its powers under Rule 44.94(2) or 44.94(3)

6762.text: Once the parties have been notified of the date fixed for the hearing, the appellant’s legal representative must, after consultation with his opponent, file a bundle containing photocopies of the authorities upon which each side will rely at the hearing.

6764.text: The bundle of authorities should, in general:

6767.text: The bundle of authorities must be filed:

6768.text: (1) at least 7 days before the hearing; or

6769.text: (2) where the period of notice of the hearing is less than 7 days, immediately.

6771.text: If, through some oversight, a party intends, during the hearing, to refer to other authorities the parties may agree a second agreed bundle., the appellant’s legal representatives must file this bundle at least 48 hours before the hearing commences.

6773.text: A bundle of authorities must bear a certification by the legal representatives responsible for arguing the case that the requirements of Rule 44.62 or 44.64 have been complied with in respect of each authority included.

6776.text: A supplementary skeleton argument on which the appellant wishes to rely must be filed no later than 14 days after the respondent’s skeleton has been filed.

6778.text: A supplementary skeleton argument on which the respondent wishes to rely must be filed 14 days after the appellant’s supplementary skeleton and at least 14 days before the hearing.

6780.text: All supplementary skeleton arguments must comply with the requirements set out in Rules 44.59 to 44.67.

6782.text: At the hearing the Court may refuse to hear argument from a party not contained in a skeleton argument filed within the relevant time limit set out in this paragraph.

6785.text: All the documents which are needed for the appeal hearing must be filed at least 7 days before the hearing.

6787.text: Any party who fails to comply with the provisions of Rule 44.106 may be required to attend before the Chief Justice to seek permission to proceed with, or to oppose, the appeal.

6788.text: Appeals To The Court Of Appeal

6790.text: On hearing an appeal from a decision of the Court of First Instance, the Court of Appeal may:

6792.text: Appeals To The Court Of First Instance

6794.text: Unless another Rule, Practice Direction or enactment provides otherwise, on hearing an appeal, the Court of First Instance may:

6798.text: Every appeal will be limited to a review of the decision of the lower Court unless:

6799.text: (1) the Court considers that in the circumstances of an individual appeal it would be in the interests of justice to hold a re-hearing;

6801.text: (3) any other Rule or enactment requires a re-hearing.

6803.text: The hearing of an appeal will be a re-hearing (as opposed to a review of the decision of the lower Court) if the appeal is from the decision of a person or other body and the person or other body:

6804.text: (1) did not hold a hearing to come to that decision; or

6805.text: (2) held a hearing to come to that decision, but the procedure adopted did not provide for the consideration of evidence.

6807.text: The appeal Court may exercise its powers in relation to the whole or part of an order of the lower Court.

6809.text: At the hearing of the appeal a party may not rely on a matter not contained in his appeal notice unless the appeal Court gives permission.

6812.text: Unless it orders otherwise, the appeal Court will not receive:

6813.text: (1) oral evidence; or (2) evidence which was not before the lower Court.

6815.text: Subject to Rule 44.114, the Court may receive further evidence, including:

6818.text: The appeal Court may draw any inference of fact which it considers justified on the evidence.

6821.text: The Court of Appeal will allow an appeal from a decision of the Court of First Instance where the decision of the lower Court was:

6822.text: (1) wrong; or (2) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings in the lower Court.

6824.text: The Court of First Instance will allow an appeal from a decision of a tribunal provided for in the Law, DIFC Law or Rules of Court where the decision was:

6825.text: (1) wrong in relation to a question of law; (2) unjust because of procedural unfairness or a miscarriage of justice; and/or (3) wrong in relation to any other matter provided for in or under DIFC Law.

6827.text: The Court of First Instance will allow an appeal not referred to in Rule 44.118 where the decision was:

6828.text: (1) wrong; or (2) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings.

6831.text: The fact that a Part 32 offer or payment into Court has been made must not be disclosed to any Judge of the appeal Court who is to hear or determine:

6832.text: (1) an application for permission to appeal; or

6834.text: until all questions (other than costs) have been determined.

6836.text: Rule 44.120 does not apply if the Part 32 offer or payment into Court is relevant to the substance of the appeal.

6838.text: Rule 44.120 does not prevent disclosure in any application in the appeal proceedings if disclosure of the fact that a Part 32 offer or payment into Court has been made is properly relevant to the matter to be decided.

6839.text: Who May Exercise The Powers Of The Court Of Appeal

6841.text: The Registrar may exercise the jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal with the consent of the Chief Justice to do all things necessary or convenient for the purpose of assisting the Judges of the Court of Appeal in the exercise of their powers or duties.

6843.text: A single Judge may exercise the appellate jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal relating to an application:

6844.text: (1) for permission to appeal to the Court of Appeal (and to grant such permission subject to such conditions as may be ordered by the single judge);

6845.text: (2) for an extension or reduction of time for any document to be filed in an appeal to the Court of Appeal (including a general power to amend the times fixed by the rules or by order of the court, as may be the case);

6846.text: (3) for leave to amend the grounds of appeal;

6848.text: (5) to strike out an appeal in the event that:

6849.text: (a) the appellant does not file a document within the time ordered and notice has been given by the Court in the order for filing that the appeal will be struck out if filing is not made strictly on time; or

6850.text: (b) the appellant does not attend the hearing of the appeal in person or by his Counsel on the date fixed for hearing; or

6851.text: (6) to strike out an appeal in the event that a skeleton is not submitted.

6852.text: (7) The single judge shall also have the power to decide any matter of case management relating to the appeal on behalf of the full bench of the Court of Appeal.

6854.text: Decisions of the Registrar or a single Judge and under Rules 44.123 or 11.124 may be made without an oral hearing.

6856.text: A party may request any decision of the Registrar or the single Judge under Rules 44.123 or 44.124 (3) or (4) made without a hearing to be reviewed by the Court of Appeal at an oral hearing.

6858.text: A request under Rule 44.126 must be filed within 7 days after the party is served with notice of the decision.

6860.text: A single Judge may refer any matter for a decision by a Court consisting of two or more judges .

6861.text: Dismissal Of Applications Or Appeals By Consent

6863.text: Where an appellant does not wish to pursue an application or an appeal, he may request the appeal

6864.text: Court for an order that his application or appeal be dismissed. If such a request is granted it will usually be on the basis that the appellant pays the costs of the application or appeal.

6866.text: If the appellant wishes to have the application or appeal dismissed without costs, his request must be accompanied by a consent signed by the respondent or his legal representative stating that the respondent consents to the dismissal of the application or appeal without costs.

6868.text: Where a settlement has been reached disposing of the application or appeal, the parties may make a joint request to the Court asking that the application or appeal be dismissed by consent. If the request is granted the application or appeal will be dismissed.

6869.text: Allowing Unopposed Appeals Or Applications Without A Hearing

6871.text: The appeal Court will not normally make an order allowing an appeal unless satisfied that the decision of the lower Court was wrong, but the appeal Court may set aside or vary the order of the lower Court with consent and without determining the merits of the appeal, if it is satisfied that there are good and sufficient reasons for doing so. Where the appeal Court is requested by all parties to allow an application or an appeal the Court may consider the request without a hearing. The request should set out the relevant history of the proceedings and the matters relied on as justifying the proposed order and be accompanied by a copy of the proposed order.

6872.text: Availability Of Reserved Judgments Before Hand Down

6874.text: Rules 44.134 and 44.135 apply where the presiding Judge is satisfied that the result of the appeal will attract no special degree of confidentiality or sensitivity.

6876.text: A copy of the written judgment may be made available to the parties’ legal representatives by 4 p.m. two clear working days before judgment is due to be pronounced or such other period as the Court may direct. This can be shown, in confidence, to the parties but only for the purpose of obtaining instructions and on the strict understanding that the judgment, or its effect, is not to be disclosed to any other person. A working day is any day on which the Court Office is open for business.

6878.text: The appeal will be listed for judgment and the judgment handed down at the appropriate time.

6879.text: Attendance Of Legal Representatives On The Handing Down Of A Reserved

6882.text: Where any consequential orders are agreed, the parties’ legal representatives need not attend on the handing down of a reserved judgment. Where a legal representative does attend the Court may, if it considers such attendance unnecessary, disallow the costs of the attendance. If the parties do not indicate that they intend to attend, where the appeal was heard by more than one Judge, the judgment may be handed down by a single member of the Court.

6885.text: The parties must, in respect of any draft agreed orders file 4 copies in the Court Office, no later than 12 noon on the working day before the judgment is handed down.

6887.text: A copy of a draft order must bear the case reference, the date the judgment is to be handed down and the name of the presiding Judge.

6890.text: Any proposed correction to the draft judgment should be sent to the Registry with a copy to any other party.

6893.text: Costs are likely to be assessed by way of immediate assessment at the following hearings:

6896.text: Parties attending any of the hearings referred to in Rule 44.140 should be prepared to deal with the immediate assessment.

6897.text: Appeals To The Court Of First Instance From The Director Of Employment Standards

6899.text: Rules 44.143 to 44.152 apply to appeals to the Court of First Instance from a determination, decision, or fine of the Director of Employment Standards pursuant to Article 83(1) of the Employment Law, DIFC Law 4 of 2005.

6901.text: An appeal referred to in Rule 44.142 shall be filed within 30 days of receipt of the determination, decision, or fine appealed and in accordance with the requirements of this Part.

6903.text: The Director of Employment Standards must be named as a party to an appeal referred to in Rule 44.142.

6905.text: The Court may grant a stay of the decision appealed from until the disposition of the appeal.

6907.text: The Court may attach conditions to the stay, including requiring a party to deposit as security part or all of a monetary order.

6909.text: The Director of Employment Standards shall provide the Court with the record that was before him at the time the determination, order or fine was made, including any witness statement and documents considered by him.

6911.text: The Court may consider any other relevant evidence, in addition to the record.

6913.text: Before considering the appeal, the Court of First Instance may:

6914.text: (1) refer the matter back to the Director of Employment Standards for further investigation; or (2) recommend that an attempt be made to settle the matter.

6916.text: The Court may dismiss the appeal without a hearing if satisfied that:

6917.text:
(1) the appeal is not within the Court’s jurisdiction;   
(2) the appeal is frivolous or trivial or is not brought in good faith.

6919.text: On hearing the appeal the Court may:

6922.text: The Director of Employment Standards shall comply with any directions the Court gives to him.

6924.text: 44.153 No appeal lies from a decision of the Court of First Instance or the Court of Appeal on an appeal.

6927.text: The Court of Appeal or the Court of First Instance will not reopen a final determination of any appeal unless:

6928.text:
(1) it is necessary to do so in order to avoid real injustice;   
(2) the circumstances are exceptional and make it appropriate to reopen the appeal; and (3) there is no alternative effective remedy.

6930.text: In Rules 44.154, 44.156, 44.159 and 44.163, “appeal” includes an application for permission to appeal.

6932.text: Permission is needed to make an application under Rule 44.154 to reopen a final determination of an appeal even in cases where permission was not needed for the original appeal.

6934.text: Permission to reopen a final determination of an appeal must be sought from the Court whose decision the applicant wishes to reopen.

6936.text: The application for permission must be made by application notice and supported by written evidence, verified by a statement of truth.

6938.text: A copy of the application for permission must not be served on any other party to the original appeal unless the Court so directs.

6940.text: Where the Court directs that the application for permission is to be served on another party, that party may within 14 days of the service on him of the copy of the application file and serve a written statement either supporting or opposing the application.

6942.text: The application for permission, and any written statements supporting or opposing it, will be considered on paper by a single Judge.

6944.text: There is no right to an oral hearing of an application for permission unless, exceptionally, the Judge so directs.

6946.text: The Judge will not grant permission without directing the application to be served on the other party to the original appeal and giving him an opportunity to make representations.

6948.text: There is no right of appeal or review from the decision of the Judge on the application for permission, which is final.

6951.text: THESE RULES WILL APPLY TO JUDGMENTS AND ORDERS ISSUED BEFORE 25 MAY 2017.

6952.text: Scope Of This Part And Interpretation

6954.text: The Rules in this Part apply to appeals to:

6955.text: (1) the Court of Appeal; and (2) the Court of First Instance.

6960.text: This Part is subject to any Rule, enactment or Practice Direction which sets out special provisions with regard to any particular category of appeal.

6963.text: Unless the appeal CourtG or the lower CourtG orders otherwise, an appeal shall not operate as a stayG of any order or decision of the lower CourtG .

6966.text: An appellantG or respondentG requires permission to appeal:

6967.text: (1) where the appeal is to the Court of Appeal, except where the appeal is against a committal order; (2) where the appeal is to the Court of First Instance, except where the appeal is against: (a) a refusal by the Registrar of Companies to grant authorisation to transfer incorporation under Article 122(1) of the Companies Law (Amended and Restated), DIFC Law 3 of 2006; or

6968.text: (b) a refusal by the Registrar of Companies to grant authorisation to transfer a Limited Partnership under Article 63(1) of the Limited Partnership Law, DIFC Law 4 of 2006.

6970.text: An application for permission to appeal may be made:

6971.text: (1) to the lower CourtG at the hearing at which the decision to be appealed was made; or (2) to the appeal CourtG in an appeal noticeG .

6973.text: An application to the appeal Court for permission to appeal under RDC 44.71(2) may not be heard by the judge against whose decision permission to appeal is sought.

6975.text: Permission to appeal may be given only where:

6976.text: (1) the CourtG considers that the appeal would have a real prospect of success; or (2) there is some other compelling reason why the appeal should be heard.

6978.text: Where a party applies for permission to appeal against a decision at the hearing at which the decision was made, the JudgeG making the decision shall state:

6979.text:
(1) whether or not the judgmentG or order is final;   
(2) whether an appeal lies from the judgmentG or order; and   
(3) whether the CourtG gives permission to appeal; and   
(4) if not, the appropriate appeal CourtG to which any further application for permission may be made.

6981.text: Where no application for permission to appeal has been made in accordance with Rule 44.6(1) but a party requests further time to make such an application, the CourtG may adjourn the hearing to give that party the opportunity to do so.

6983.text: Where the lower CourtG refuses an application for permission to appeal, a further application for permission to appeal may be made to the appeal CourtG .

6984.text: Consideration Of Permission Without A Hearing

6986.text: Applications for permission to appeal may be considered by the appeal CourtG without a hearing.

6988.text: If permission is granted without a hearing the parties will be notified of that decision and the procedure in Rules 44.93 to 44.97 will then apply.

6989.text: 44.14 If permission is refused without a hearing the parties will be notified of that decision with the reasons for it.

6991.text: Where the appeal CourtG , without a hearing, refuses permission to appeal, the person seeking permission may request the decision to be reconsidered at a hearing. This may be before the same JudgeG .

6993.text: Where the appeal CourtG refuses permission to appeal without a hearing, it may, if it considers that the application is totally without merit, make an order that the person seeking permission may not request the decision to be reconsidered at a hearing.

6995.text: A request for the decision to be reconsidered at an oral hearing must be filed at the appeal CourtG within 7 days after serviceG of the notice that permission has been refused. A copy of the request must be served by the appellantG on the respondentG at the same time.

6998.text: Rules 44.19 to 44.21 apply where an appellantG makes a request for a decision to be reconsidered at an oral hearing.

7000.text: The appellant $\mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ legal representativeG must, at least 4 days before the hearing, in a brief written statement:

7001.text: (1) inform the CourtG and the respondentG of the points which he proposes to raise at the hearing; and (2) set out his reasons why permission should be granted notwithstanding the reasons given for the refusal of permission.

7003.text: Notice of a permission hearing will be given to the respondentG but he is not required to attend unless the CourtG requests him to do so.

7005.text: If the CourtG requests the respondent $\mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ attendance at the permission hearing, the appellantG must supply the respondentG with a copy of the appeal bundle within 7 days of being notified of the request, or such other period as the CourtG may direct. The costs of providing that bundle shall be borne by the appellantG initially, but will form part of the costs of the permission application.

7009.text: (1) limit the issues to be heard; and (2) be made subject to conditions.

7011.text: Where a CourtG under Rule 44.22 gives permission to appeal on some issues only, it will:

7012.text:
(1) refuse permission on any remaining issues; or   
(2) reserve the question of permission to appeal on any remaining issues to the CourtG hearing the appeal.

7014.text: If the CourtG reserves the question of permission under Rule 44.23(2), the appellantG must, within 14 days after serviceG of the Court’sG order, inform the appeal CourtG and the respondentG in writing whether he intends to pursue the reserved issues. If the appellantG does intend to pursue the reserved issues, the parties must include in any time estimate for the appeal hearing, their time estimate for the reserved issues.

7016.text: If the appeal CourtG refuses permission to appeal on the remaining issues without a hearing and the applicant wishes to have that decision reconsidered at an oral hearing, the time limit in Rule 44.17 shall

7017.text: apply. Any application for an extension of this time limit should be made promptly. The CourtG hearing the appeal on the issues for which permission has been granted will not normally grant, at the appeal hearing, an application to extend the time limit in Rule 44.17 for the remaining issues.

7019.text: If the appeal CourtG refuses permission to appeal on remaining issues at or after an oral hearing, the application for permission to appeal on those issues cannot be renewed at the appeal hearing.

7022.text: In most cases, applications for permission to appeal will be determined without the CourtG requesting:

7023.text: (1) submissions from; or (2) if there is an oral hearing, attendance by; the respondentG .

7025.text: Where the CourtG does not request submissions from or attendance by the respondentG , costs will not normally be allowed to a respondentG who volunteers submissions or attendance.

7028.text: (1) submissions from; or (2) attendance by the respondentG ;

7029.text: the CourtG will normally allow the respondentG his costs if permission is refused.

7032.text: Case management decisions include decisions made under Rule 4.2 and decisions about:

7035.text: Where the application is for permission to appeal from a case management decision, the CourtG dealing with the application may take into account whether:

7036.text:
(1) the issue is of sufficient significance to justify the costs of an appeal;   
(2) the procedural consequences of an appeal (e.g. loss of trial date) outweigh the significance of the case management decision; and

7037.text: (3) it would be more convenient to determine the issue at or after trial.

7040.text: An appellant $\mathsf { \mathsf { S } } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice must be filed and served in all cases.

7042.text: Where the appellantG seeks permission from the appeal CourtG it must be requested in the appellant’sG notice.

7044.text: The appellant $\mathsf { \mathsf { S } } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice must set out the grounds of appeal relied on.

7047.text:
(1) set out clearly the reasons why it is said the decision of the lower CourtG was: (a) wrong; or (b) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings in the lower CourtG ; and   
(2) specify, in respect of each ground, whether the ground raises an appeal on a point of law or is an   
appeal against a finding of fact.

7048.text: Filing And Service Of Appellant’S Notice

7050.text: The appellantG must file the appellant $\cdot \mathsf { s } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice at the appeal CourtG within:

7051.text: (1) such period as may be directed by the lower CourtG which should not normally exceed 28 days and which may be longer or shorter than the period referred to in (2); or

7052.text: (2) where the CourtG makes no such direction, 14 days after the date of the decision of the lower CourtG that the appellantG wishes to appeal.

7054.text: Where the lower CourtG JudgeG announces his decision and reserves the reasons for his judgmentG or order until a later date, he should, in the exercise of powers under Rule 44.36(1), fix a period for filingG the appellant’sG notice at the appeal CourtG that takes this into account.

7056.text: Subject to Rule 44.39 and unless the appeal CourtG orders otherwise, a sealed copy of the appellant’sG notice, together with any skeleton arguments, must be served on each respondentG :

7060.text: Where the requirement to serve a skeleton argument is modified by Rule 44.75 the skeleton argument should be served as soon as it is filed.

7062.text: The appellantG must, as soon as practicable, file a certificate of serviceG of the documents referred to in Rule 44.38.

7064.text: Where the time for filingG an appellant’sG notice has expired, the appellantG must:

7065.text: (1) file the appellant’sG notice; and (2) include in that appellant $\cdot \mathsf { s } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice an application for an extension of time.

7067.text: The appellant $\mathsf { \mathsf { S } } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice should state the reason for the delay and the steps taken prior to the application being made.

7069.text: Where the appellantG is applying for permission to appeal in his appellant $\mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice, he must serve on the respondentsG his appellant’sG notice and skeleton argument (but not the appeal bundle), unless the appeal CourtG directs otherwise.

7072.text: (1) has been given by the lower CourtG ; or

7074.text: the appellantG must serve the appeal bundle on the respondentsG with the appellant’sG notice.

7076.text: Where the appellant $\mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice includes an application for an extension of time and permission to appeal has been given or is not required the respondentG has the right to be heard on that application. He must be served with a copy of the appeal bundle. However, a respondentG who unreasonably opposes an extension of time runs the risk of being ordered to pay the appellant’sG costs of that application.

7078.text: If an extension of time is given following such an application the procedure at Rules 44.93 to 44.97 applies.

7080.text: The CourtG may dispense with the requirement for serviceG of the notice on a respondentG . Any application noticeG seeking an order under Rule 9.32 to dispense with serviceG should set out the reasons relied on and be verified by a statement of truth.

7082.text: Unless the CourtG otherwise directs a respondentG need not take any action when served with an appellant’sG notice until such time as notification is given to him that permission to appeal has been given.

7085.text: An application to vary the time limit for filingG an appeal noticeG must be made to the appeal CourtG .

7087.text: The parties may not agree to extend any date or time limit set by:

7091.text: Notice of an application to be made to the appeal CourtG for a remedy incidental to the appeal may be included in the appeal noticeG or in a Part 23 application noticeG .

7093.text: The applicant must file the following documents with the notice:

7094.text: (1) one additional copy of the application noticeG for the appeal CourtG and one copy for each of the

7096.text: (2) where applicable a sealed copy of the order which is the subject of the main appeal; and

7097.text: (3) a bundle of documents in support which should include:

7099.text: (b) any witness statements and affidavitsG filed in support of the application noticeG .

7102.text: The appellantG must file the following documents together with an appeal bundle with his appellant’sG notice:

7103.text: (1) two additional copies of the appellant $\cdot \mathsf { s } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice for the appeal CourtG ; (2) one copy of the appellant’sG notice for each of the respondentsG ; and (3) one copy of his skeleton argument for each copy of the appellant’sG notice that is filed.

7105.text: An appellantG must include in his appeal bundle the following documents:

7106.text: (1) a sealed copy of the appellant’sG notice;

7107.text: (2) a sealed copy of the order being appealed;

7108.text: (3) a copy of any order giving or refusing permission to appeal, together with a copy of the Judge’sG reasons for allowing or refusing permission to appeal;

7109.text: (4) any affidavitG or witness statement filed in support of any application included in the appellant’sG notice;

7110.text: (5) a copy of his skeleton argument;

7111.text: (6) a transcript or note of judgmentG , and in cases where permission to appeal was given by the lower CourtG or is not required those parts of any transcript of evidence which are directly relevant to any question at issue on the appeal;

7112.text: (7) the claim form and statements of case (where relevant to the subject of the appeal);

7113.text: (8) any application noticeG (or case management documentation) relevant to the subject of the appeal;

7114.text: (9) in the case of judicial review or a statutory appeal, the original decision which was the subject of the

7116.text: (10) in cases where the appeal is from a tribunalG , a copy of the tribunal’sG reasons for the decision, a copy of the decision reviewed by the tribunalG and the reasons for the original decision and any document filed with the tribunalG setting out the grounds of appeal from that decision;

7117.text: (11) any other documents which the appellantG reasonably considers necessary to enable the appeal CourtG to reach its decision on the hearing of the application or appeal; and

7118.text: (12) such other documents as the CourtG may direct.

7120.text: All documents that are extraneous to the issues to be considered on the application or the appeal must be excluded. The appeal bundle may include affidavitsG , witness statements, summaries, experts’ reports and exhibits but only where these are directly relevant to the subject matter of the appeal.

7122.text: The appeal bundle must contain a certificate signed by the appellant’sG legal representativesG to the effect that they have read and understood Rule 44.55 above and that the composition of the appeal bundle complies with it.

7124.text: Where it is not possible to file all the above documents, the appellantG must indicate which documents have not yet been filed and the reasons why they are not currently available. The appellantG must then provide a reasonable estimate of when the missing document or documents can be filed and file them as soon as reasonably practicable.

7127.text: In cases where the appeal bundle comprises more than 500 pages, exclusive of transcripts, the appellant’sG legal representativesG must, after consultation with the respondent’sG legal representativesG , also prepare and file with the CourtG , in addition to copies of the appeal bundle (as amended in accordance with Rule 44.119) the requisite number of copies of a core bundle.

7129.text: The core bundle must be filed within 28 days of receipt of the order giving permission to appeal or, where permission to appeal was granted by the lower CourtG or is not required, within 28 days of the date of serviceG of the appellant’sG notice on the respondentG .

7132.text: (1) must contain the documents which are central to the appeal; and

7136.text: Rules 44.62 to 44.73 apply to the preparation of appeal bundles, supplemental respondents’G bundles where the parties are unable to agree amendments to the appeal bundle, and core bundles.

7139.text: Where documents are copied unnecessarily or bundled incompletely, costs may be disallowed. Where the provisions of this Part as to the preparation or delivery of bundles are not followed the bundle may be

7140.text: rejected by the CourtG or be made the subject of a special costs order.

7143.text: No more than one copy of any document should be included unless there is a good reason for doing otherwise (such as the use of a separate core bundle in Rule 44.58).

7146.text: The following rules regarding pagination shall apply:

7147.text: (1) Bundles must be paginated, each page being numbered individually and consecutively. The pagination used at trial must also be indicated. Letters and other documents should normally be included in chronological order. (An exception to consecutive page numbering arises in the case of core bundles where it may be preferable to retain the original numbering).

7148.text: (2) Page numbers should be inserted in bold figures at the bottom of the page and in a form that can be clearly distinguished from any other pagination on the document.

7151.text: The following rules regarding format and presentation shall apply:

7152.text: (1) Where possible the documents should be in A4 format. Where a document has to be read across rather than down the page, it should be so placed in the bundle as to ensure that the text starts nearest the spine.

7153.text: (2) Where any marking or writing in colour on a document is important, the document must be copied in colour or marked up correctly in colour.

7154.text: (3) Documents which are not easily legible should be transcribed and the transcription marked and placed adjacent to the document transcribed.

7155.text: (4) Documents in a language other than English should be translated and the translation marked and placed adjacent to the document translated. The translation should be agreed or, if it cannot be agreed, each party’s proposed translation should be included.

7156.text: (5) The size of any bundle should be tailored to its contents. A large lever arch file should not be used for just a few pages nor should files be overloaded.

7157.text: (6) Where it will assist the CourtG , different sections of the file may be separated by cardboard or other tabbed dividers so long as these are clearly indexed. Where, for example, a document is awaited when the appeal bundle is filed, a single sheet of paper can be inserted after a divider, indicating the nature of the document awaited. For example, ‘Transcript of evidence of Mr J Smith (to follow)’.

7160.text: The following rules regarding binding shall apply:

7161.text: (1) All documents, with the exception of transcripts, must be bound together. This may be in a lever arch file, ring binder or plastic folder. Plastic sleeves containing loose documents must not be used. Binders and files must be strong enough to withstand heavy use.

7162.text: (2) Large documents such as plans should be placed in an easily accessible file. Large documents which will need to be opened up frequently should be inserted in a file larger than A4 size.

7165.text: The following rules regarding indices and labels shall apply:

7166.text: (1) An index must be included at the front of the bundle listing all the documents and providing the page references for each. In the case of documents such as letters, invoices or bank statements, they may be given a general description.

7167.text: (2) Where the bundles consist of more than one file, an index to all the files should be included in the first file and an index included for each file. Indices should, if possible, be on a single sheet. The full name of the case should not be inserted on the index if this would waste space. Documents should be identified briefly but properly.

7170.text: The following rules regarding identification shall apply:

7171.text: (1) Every bundle must be clearly identified, on the spine and on the front cover, with the name of the case and the Court $' \mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ reference. Where the bundle consists of more than one file, each file must be numbered on the spine, the front cover and the inside of the front cover.

7172.text: (2) Outer labels should use large lettering e.g. ‘Appeal Bundle A’ or ‘Core Bundle’. The full title of the appeal and legal representatives’G names and addresses should be omitted. A label should be used on the front as well as on the spine.

7175.text: All staples, heavy metal clips, etc., must be removed.

7178.text: The following rules regarding statements of case shall apply:

7179.text: (1) Statements of case should be assembled in ‘chapter’ form — i.e. claim followed by particulars of claim, followed by further information, irrespective of date.

7180.text: (2) Redundant documents, e.g. particulars of claim overtaken by amendments, requests for further information recited in the answers given, should generally be excluded.

7183.text: The following rules regarding new documents shall apply:

7184.text: (1) Before a new document is introduced into bundles which have already been delivered to the CourtG , steps should be taken to ensure that it carries an appropriate bundle/page number so that it can be added to the CourtG documents. It should not be stapled and it should be prepared with punch holes for immediate inclusion in the binders in use.

7185.text: (2) If it is expected that a large number of miscellaneous new documents will from time to time be introduced, there should be a special tabbed empty loose-leaf file for that purpose. An index should be produced for this file, updated as necessary.

7188.text: Since correspondence between legal representativesG is unlikely to be required for the purposes of an appeal, only those letters which will need to be referred to should be copied.

7191.text: If the appellantG fails to comply with the requirements as to the provision of bundles of documents, the application or appeal will be referred for consideration to be given as to why it should not be dismissed for failure so to comply.

7194.text: The appellant $\mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice must, subject to Rule 44.75, be accompanied by a skeleton argument. Alternatively the skeleton argument may be included in the appellant’sG notice. Where the skeleton argument is so included it will not form part of the notice for the purposes of Rule 44.88.

7196.text: Where it is impracticable for the appellant’sG skeleton argument to accompany the appellant’sG notice it must be filed and served on all respondentsG within 14 days of filingG the notice.

7199.text: A skeleton argument filed on behalf of the appellantG should contain in paragraph 1 the legal representatives’G time estimate for the hearing of the appeal.

7201.text: A skeleton argument must contain a numbered list of the points which the party wishes to make. These should both define and confine the areas of controversy. Each point should be stated as concisely as the nature of the case allows.

7203.text: A numbered point must be followed by a reference to any document on which the party wishes to rely.

7205.text: A skeleton argument must state, in respect of each authority cited:

7206.text: (1) the proposition of law that the authority demonstrates; and

7207.text: (2) the parts of the authority (identified by page or paragraph references) that support the proposition.

7209.text: If more than one authority is cited in support of a given proposition, the skeleton argument must briefly state the reason for taking that course.

7211.text: The statement referred to in Rule 44.80 should not materially add to the length of the skeleton argument

7212.text: but should be sufficient to demonstrate, in the context of the argument:

7213.text: (1) the relevance of the authority or authorities to that argument; and (2) that the citation is necessary for a proper presentation of that argument.

7215.text: The cost of preparing a skeleton argument which:

7216.text: (1) does not comply with the requirements set out in Rules 44.76 to 44.81; or

7217.text: (2) was not filed within the time limits provided by this Part (or any further time granted by the Court);

7218.text: will not be allowed on assessment except to the extent that the CourtG otherwise directs.

7220.text: The appellantG should consider what other information the appeal CourtG will need. This may include a list of persons who feature in the case or glossaries of technical terms. A chronology of relevant events will be necessary in most appeals.

7223.text: Where the judgmentG to be appealed has been officially recorded by the CourtG , an approved transcript of that record should accompany the appellant’sG notice. Photocopies will not be accepted for this purpose. However, where there is no officially recorded judgmentG , the following documents will be acceptable:

7225.text: Where the judgmentG was made in writing a copy of that judgmentG endorsed with the Judge’sG signature.

7227.text: When judgmentG was not officially recorded or made in writing a note of the judgmentG (agreed between the appellant’sG and respondent’sG legal representativesG ) should be submitted for approval to the JudgeG whose decision is being appealed. If the parties cannot agree on a single note of the judgmentG , both versions should be provided to that JudgeG with an explanatory letter. For the purpose of an application for permission to appeal the note need not be approved by the respondentG or the lower CourtG JudgeG .

7228.text: (3) Reasons for judgment in tribunal cases

7229.text: A sealed copy of the tribunal’sG reasons for the decision.

7231.text: An appellantG may not be able to obtain an official transcript or other suitable record of the lower Court’sG

7232.text: decision within the time within which the appellant’sG notice must be filed. In such cases the appellant’sG notice must still be completed to the best of the appellant’sG ability on the basis of the documentation available. However it may be amended subsequently with the permission of the appeal CourtG .

7235.text: When the evidence is relevant to the appeal an official transcript of the relevant evidence must be obtained. Transcripts or notes of evidence are generally not needed for the purpose of determining an application for permission to appeal.

7237.text: If evidence relevant to the appeal was not officially recorded, a typed version of the Judge’sG notes of evidence must be obtained.

7240.text: An appeal noticeG may not be amended without the permission of the appeal CourtG . An application to amend and any application in opposition will normally be dealt with at the appeal hearing unless that course would cause unnecessary expense or delay in which case a request should be made for the application to amend to be heard in advance.

7246.text: The CourtG will only exercise its powers under Rule 44.88 where there is a compelling reason for doing so.

7371.text: Appeals To The Court Of Appeal

7373.text: On hearing an appeal from a decision of the Court of First Instance, the Court of Appeal may:

7375.text: Appeals To The Court Of First Instance

7377.text: Unless another Rule, Practice Direction or enactment provides otherwise, on hearing an appeal, the Court of First Instance may:

7381.text: Every appeal will be limited to a review of the decision of the lower CourtG unless:

7382.text: (1) the CourtG considers that in the circumstances of an individual appeal it would be in the interests of justice to hold a re-hearing;

7384.text: (3) any other Rule or enactment requires a re-hearing.

7386.text: The hearing of an appeal will be a re-hearing (as opposed to a review of the decision of the lower CourtG ) if the appeal is from the decision of a person or other body and the person or other body:

7387.text:
(1) did not hold a hearing to come to that decision; or   
(2) held a hearing to come to that decision, but the procedure adopted did not provide for the consideration of evidence.

7389.text: The appeal CourtG may exercise its powers in relation to the whole or part of an order of the lower CourtG .

7391.text: At the hearing of the appeal a party may not rely on a matter not contained in his appeal noticeG unless the appeal CourtG gives permission.

7394.text: Unless it orders otherwise, the appeal CourtG will not receive:

7395.text: (1) oral evidence; or (2) evidence which was not before the lower CourtG .

7397.text: Subject to Rule 44.139, the CourtG may receive further evidence, including:

7400.text: The appeal CourtG may draw any inference of fact which it considers justified on the evidence.

7403.text: The Court of Appeal will allow an appeal from a decision of the Court of First Instance where the decision of the lower CourtG was:

7404.text: (1) wrong; or (2) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings in the lower CourtG .

7406.text: The Court of First Instance will allow an appeal from a decision of a tribunalG provided for in the LawG , DIFC Law or Rules of CourtG where the decision was:

7407.text: (1) wrong in relation to a question of law; (2) unjust because of procedural unfairness or a miscarriage of justice; and/or (3) wrong in relation to any other matter provided for in or under DIFC Law.

7409.text: The Court of First Instance will allow an appeal not referred to in Rule 44.143 where the decision was:

7411.text: (2) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings.

7414.text: The fact that a Part 32 offer or payment into CourtG has been made must not be disclosed to any JudgeG of the appeal CourtG who is to hear or determine:

7415.text: (1) an application for permission to appeal; or

7417.text: until all questions (other than costs) have been determined.

7419.text: Rule 44.145 does not apply if the Part 32 offer or payment into CourtG is relevant to the substance of the appeal.

7421.text: Rule 44.145 does not prevent disclosure in any application in the appeal proceedings if disclosure of the fact that a Part 32 offer or payment into CourtG has been made is properly relevant to the matter to be decided.

7422.text: Who May Exercise The Powers Of The Court Of Appeal

7424.text: The RegistrarG may exercise the jurisdictionG of the Court of Appeal with the consent of the Chief JusticeG to do all things necessary or convenient for the purpose of assisting the JudgesG of the Court of Appeal in the exercise of their powers or duties.

7426.text: A single JudgeG may exercise the appellate jurisdictionG of the Court of Appeal relating to an application for:

7429.text: Decisions of the RegistrarG or a single JudgeG and under Rules 44.148 or 44.149 may be made without a hearing.

7431.text: A party may request any decision of the RegistrarG or the single JudgeG under Rules 44.148 or 44.149 made without a hearing to be reviewed by the Court of Appeal at a hearing.

7433.text: A request under Rule 44.151 must be filed within 7 days after the party is served with notice of the decision.

7435.text: A single JudgeG may refer any matter for a decision by a Court consisting of two or more judgesG .

7436.text: Dismissal Of Applications Or Appeals By Consent

7438.text: Where an appellantG does not wish to pursue an application or an appeal, he may request the appeal CourtG for an order that his application or appeal be dismissed. If such a request is granted it will usually be on the basis that the appellantG pays the costs of the application or appeal.

7440.text: If the appellantG wishes to have the application or appeal dismissed without costs, his request must be accompanied by a consent signed by the respondentG or his legal representativeG stating that the respondentG consents to the dismissal of the application or appeal without costs.

7442.text: Where a settlement has been reached disposing of the application or appeal, the parties may make a joint request to the CourtG asking that the application or appeal be dismissed by consent. If the request is granted the application or appeal will be dismissed.

7443.text: Allowing Unopposed Appeals Or Applications Without A Hearing

7445.text: The appeal CourtG will not normally make an order allowing an appeal unless satisfied that the decision of the lower CourtG was wrong, but the appeal CourtG may set asideG or vary the order of the lower CourtG with consent and without determining the merits of the appeal, if it is satisfied that there are good and sufficient reasons for doing so. Where the appeal CourtG is requested by all parties to allow an application or an appeal the CourtG may consider the request without a hearing. The request should set out the relevant history of the proceedings and the matters relied on as justifying the proposed order and be accompanied by a copy of the proposed order.

7446.text: Availability Of Reserved Judgments Before Hand Down

7448.text: Rules 44.159 and 44.160 apply where the presiding JudgeG is satisfied that the result of the appeal will attract no special degree of confidentiality or sensitivity.

7450.text: A copy of the written judgmentG will be made available to the parties’ legal representativesG by 4 p.m. on the second working day before judgmentG is due to be pronounced or such other period as the CourtG may direct. This can be shown, in confidence, to the parties but only for the purpose of obtaining instructions and on the strict understanding that the judgmentG , or its effect, is not to be disclosed to any other person. A working day is any day on which the Court Office is open for business.

7452.text: The appeal will be listed for judgmentG and the judgmentG handed down at the appropriate time.

7453.text: Attendance Of Legal Representatives On The Handing Down Of A Reserved Judgment

7455.text: Where any consequential orders are agreed, the parties’ legal representatives need not attend on the handing down of a reserved judgmentG . Where a legal representative does attend the CourtG may, if it considers such attendance unnecessary, disallow the costs of the attendance. If the parties do not indicate that they intend to attend, where the appeal was heard by more than one JudgeG , the judgmentG may be handed down by a single member of the CourtG .

7458.text: The parties must, in respect of any draft agreed orders file 4 copies in the Court Office, no later than 12 noon on the working day before the judgmentG is handed down.

7460.text: A copy of a draft order must bear the case reference, the date the judgmentG is to be handed down and the name of the presiding JudgeG .

7463.text: Any proposed correction to the draft judgmentG should be sent to the JudgeG who prepared the draft with a copy to any other party.

7466.text: Costs are likely to be assessed by way of immediate assessment at the following hearings:

7469.text: Parties attending any of the hearings referred to in Rule 44.165 should be prepared to deal with the immediate assessment.

7470.text: Appeals To The Court Of First Instance From The Director Of Employment Standards

7472.text: Rules 44.168 to 44.177 apply to appeals to the Court of First Instance from a determination, decision, or fine of the Director of Employment Standards pursuant to Article 83(1) of the Employment Law, DIFC Law 4 of 2005.

7474.text: An appeal referred to in Rule 44.167 shall be filed within 30 days of receipt of the determination, decision, or fine appealed and in accordance with the requirements of this Part.

7476.text: The Director of Employment Standards must be named as a party to an appeal referred to in Rule 44.167.

7478.text: The CourtG may grant a stayG of the decision appealed from until the disposition of the appeal.

7480.text: The CourtG may attach conditions to the stayG , including requiring a party to deposit as security part or all of a monetary order.

7482.text: The Director of Employment Standards shall provide the CourtG with the record that was before him at the time the determination, order or fine was made, including any witness statement and documents considered by him.

7484.text: The CourtG may consider any other relevant evidence, in addition to the record.

7486.text: Before considering the appeal, the Court of First Instance may:

7487.text: (1) refer the matter back to the Director of Employment Standards for further investigation; or (2) recommend that an attempt be made to settle the matter.

7489.text: The CourtG may dismiss the appeal without a hearing if satisfied that:

7490.text:
(1) the appeal is not within the Court’sG jurisdictionG ;   
(2) the appeal is frivolous or trivial or is not brought in good faith.

7492.text: On hearing the appeal the CourtG may:

7495.text: The Director of Employment Standards shall comply with any directions the CourtG gives to him.

7497.text: 44.178 No appeal lies from a decision of the Court of First Instance or the Court of Appeal on an appeal.

7500.text: The Court of Appeal or the Court of First Instance will not reopen a final determination of any appeal unless:

7501.text:
(1) it is necessary to do so in order to avoid real injustice;   
(2) the circumstances are exceptional and make it appropriate to reopen the appeal; and (3) there is no alternative effective remedy.

7503.text: In Rules 44.179, 44.181, 44.184 and 44.188 “appeal” includes an application for permission to appeal.

7505.text: Permission is needed to make an application under Rule 44.179 to reopen a final determination of an appeal even in cases where permission was not needed for the original appeal.

7507.text: Permission must be sought from the CourtG whose decision the applicant wishes to reopen.

7509.text: The application for permission must be made by application noticeG and supported by written evidence, verified by a statement of truth.

7511.text: A copy of the application for permission must not be served on any other party to the original appeal unless the CourtG so directs.

7513.text: Where the CourtG directs that the application for permission is to be served on another party, that party may within 14 days of the serviceG on him of the copy of the application file and serve a written statement either supporting or opposing the application.

7515.text: The application for permission, and any written statements supporting or opposing it, will be considered on paper by a single JudgeG , and will be allowed to proceed only if the JudgeG so directs.

7517.text: There is no right to an oral hearing of an application for permission unless, exceptionally, the JudgeG so directs.

7519.text: The JudgeG will not grant permission without directing the application to be served on the other party to the original appeal and giving him an opportunity to make representations.

7521.text: There is no right of appeal or review from the decision of the JudgeG on the application for permission, which is final.

7522.text: PART 45 General Rules About Enforcement Of Judgments And Orders

7524.text: Scope Of This Part And Interpretation

7525.text: 45.1 This Part contains general Rules about enforcement of judgments and orders.

7527.text: In this Part and in Parts 46 to 50:

7528.text: (1) ‘judgment creditor’ means a person who has obtained or is entitled to enforce a judgment or order ;

7529.text: (2) ‘judgment debtor’ means a person against whom a judgment or order was given or made;

7530.text: (3) ‘judgment or order’ includes an award which the Court has:

7531.text: (a) ratified for enforcement; (b) ordered to be enforced; or (c) given permission to enforce;

7532.text: as if it were a judgment or order of the Court ; and

7533.text: (4) ‘judgment or order for the payment of money’ includes a judgment or order for the payment of costs, but does not include a judgment or order for the payment of money into Court .

7534.text: Methods Of Enforcing Judgments Or Orders

7536.text: A judgment creditor may enforce a judgment or order for the payment of money by any of the following methods:

7539.text: In addition the Court may make an order of committal for contempt of Court against a judgment debtor but

7540.text: only if permitted by a Rule.

7542.text: The enforcement of a judgment or order may be affected by the Laws and Regulations relating to insolvency.

7544.text: A judgment creditor may, except where an enactment, Rule or Practice Direction provides otherwise:

7545.text: (1) use any method of enforcement which is available; and

7546.text: (2) use more than one method of enforcement, either at the same time or one after another.

7547.text: Enforcement Of Judgment Or Order By Or Against A Non-Party

7549.text: If a judgment or order is given or made in favour of or against a person who is not a party to proceedings, it may be enforced by or against that person by the same methods as if he were a party.

7550.text: Enforcement Of Awards Of Bodies Other Than The Court

7552.text: Rules 45.10 to 45.17 apply, subject to Rule 45.9, if:

7553.text: (1) an award of a sum of money or other decision is made by any court, tribunal , body or person other than the Court ; and

7554.text: (2) an enactment provides that the award may be enforced as if payable under a Court order, or that the decision may be enforced as if it were a Court order.

7556.text: Rules 45.10 to 45.17 do not apply to arbitration awards.

7558.text: If the enactment provides that an award of a sum of money is enforceable if a Court so orders, an application for such an order must be made in accordance with Rules 45.11 to 45.15.

7560.text: An application for an order that an award may be enforced as if payable under a Court order may be made without notice.

7562.text: The application notice must be made by filing an application notice in Form P45/01.

7565.text: (1) the name and address of the person against whom it is sought to enforce the award; and (2) how much of the award remains unpaid.

7567.text: A copy of the award must be filed with the application notice .

7569.text: The application may be dealt with by the Registrar without a hearing.

7571.text: If an enactment provides that an award or decision may be enforced in the same manner as an order of the Court if it is registered, any application to the Court must be made in writing to the Registrar .

7574.text: (1) specify the statutory provision under which the application is made;

7575.text: (2) state the name and address of the person against whom it is sought to enforce the decision;

7576.text: (3) if the decision requires that person to pay a sum of money, state the amount which remains unpaid.

7577.text: Enforcement Outside The Difc Of Difc Courts Judgments, Decisions, Orders And Awards Ratified By The Courts

7579.text: Rules 45.19 to 45.21 apply where a party seeks to enforce a DIFC Courts judgment, decision or order, or ratified arbitral award outside the DIFC in accordance with Article 7(2) and / or 7(3) of the Judicial Authority Law.

7581.text: An application must be made by filing an application notice in Form P45/01.

7583.text: Where a party seeks to enforce a DIFC Courts judgment, decision, order, or ratified arbitral award outside of the DIFC, they must file an application notice in Form P45/01 requesting the Court to affix the executory formula on the judgment, decision, order or ratified arbitral award in the following form:

7584.text: “Authorities must take the initiative to enforce this document and assist in implementing it even forcibly

7586.text: Where the party intends to seek enforcement in Dubai outside the DIFC, the application should also request that the Court issue an execution letter pursuant to Article 7(3)(a) of the Judicial Authority Law addressed to the Chief Justice of the Court of First Instance of the Dubai Courts stating the procedure to be carried out.

7588.text: The Court will only affix the executory formula to a judgment, decision, order or ratified arbitral award when the following criteria are met:

7589.text: (1) The judgment, decision, order or ratified arbitral award to be executed is final and executory; and (2) The judgment, decision, order or ratified arbitral award is translated into the official language of the entity through which execution is carried out;

7591.text: The Court will only issue an execution letter in respect of a judgment, decision, order or ratified arbitral award to which it has affixed the executory formula.

7593.text: An application under [RDC 45.20] should be accompanied by the following:

7594.text: (1) A copy of the judgment, decision, order or ratified arbitral award which the applicant seeks to enforce;

7595.text: (2) Where the applicant seeks to enforce an arbitral award, a copy of any order of the DIFC Courts ratifying or recognising the award;

7596.text: (3) A draft of any execution letter which is sought;

7597.text: (4) Translations of the documents referred to at sub-paragraphs (1) to (3) above into the official language of theentity through which enforcement is to be carried out; and

7598.text: (5) A statement that the judgment, decision, order or arbitral award is final and executory and the grounds on which that is said to be the case.

7599.text: Effect Of Setting Aside Judgment Or Order

7601.text: If a judgment or order is set aside , any enforcement of the judgment or order shall cease to have effect unless the Court otherwise orders.

7605.text: If a judgment creditor is claiming interest on a judgment debt, he must include in his application or request to issue enforcement proceedings in relation to that judgment details of:

7607.text: Enforcing A Judgment Or Order Against A Partnership

7609.text: A judgment or order made against a partnership may be enforced against any property of the partnership within the DIFC .

7611.text: Subject to Rule 45.29, a judgment or order made against a partnership may be enforced against any person who is not a limited partner and who:

7612.text: (1) acknowledged service of the claim form as a partner; (2) having been served as a partner with the claim form, failed to acknowledge service of it; (3) admitted in his statement of case that he is or was a partner at a material time; or (4) was found by the Court to have been a partner at a material time.

7614.text: A judgment or order made against a partnership may not be enforced against a limited partner or a member of the partnership who was ordinarily resident outside the DIFC and Dubai when the claim form was issued unless he:

7617.text: A judgment creditor wishing to enforce a judgment or order against a person in circumstances not set out in Rules 45.28 or 45.29 must apply to the Court for permission to enforce the judgment or order .

7618.text: Payment Of Debt After Issue Of Enforcement Proceedings

7620.text: If a judgment debt or part of it is paid:

7621.text: (1) after the judgment creditor has issued any application or request to enforce it; but

7623.text: (a) any writ or warrant has been executed; or

7624.text: (b) in any other case, the date fixed for the hearing of the application;

7625.text: the judgment creditor must immediately notify the Court in writing.

7626.text: PART 46 Charging Orders, Stop Orders And Stop Notices

7629.text: Scope Of This Part And Interpretation

7630.text:
46.1   
This Section of this Part applies to an application by a judgment creditor for a charging order under Article 43 of the Law of Damages and Remedies 2005.

7632.text: 46.2 An application for a charging order may be made without notice.

7634.text: A judgment creditor may apply for a single charging order in respect of more than one judgment or order against the same debtor.

7636.text: A judgment creditor may apply in a single application notice for charging orders over more than one asset, but if the Court makes interim charging orders over more than one asset, it will draw up a separate order relating to each asset.

7638.text: An application for a charging order must be made by filing an application notice in Form P46/01.

7640.text: The application notice must contain the following information:

7643.text: The application notice must be verified by a statement of truth.

7645.text: 46.8 An application for a charging order will initially be dealt with by the Registrar without a hearing.

7647.text: The Registrar may make an order (an ‘interim charging order’):

7648.text: (1) imposing a charge over the judgment debtor’s interest in the asset to which the application relates; and

7649.text: (2) fixing a hearing to consider whether to make a final charging order as provided by 46.17(1).

7652.text: Copies of the interim charging order, the application notice and any documents filed in support of it must, not less than 28 days before the hearing, be served by the judgment creditor on the following persons:

7654.text: (2) such other creditors as the Court directs;

7655.text: (3) if the order relates to an interest under a trust, on such of the trustees as the Court directs;

7656.text: (4) if the interest charged is in securities other than securities held in Court , then:

7657.text: (a) in the case of government stock, the keeper of the register;

7658.text: (b) in the case of stock of any body incorporated within the DIFC, that body;

7659.text: (c) in the case of stock of any body incorporated outside DIFC or of any state or territory outside the DIFC , which is registered in a register kept in DIFC , the keeper of that register;

7660.text: (d) in the case of units of any unit trust in respect of which a register of the unit holders is kept in DIFC , the keeper of that register;

7661.text: (5) if the interest charged is in funds in Court , the Registrar ; and

7662.text: (6) if the interest charged is subject to a security interest filed in the Security Registry under the Law of Security 2005, the Security Registrar.

7665.text: (1) file a certificate of service not less than 2 days before the hearing; or (2) produce a certificate of service at the hearing.

7666.text: Effect Of Interim Order In Relation To Securities

7668.text: If a judgment debtor disposes of his interest in any securities, while they are subject to an interim charging order which has been served on him, that disposition shall not, so long as that order remains in force, be valid as against the judgment creditor .

7670.text: A person served under Rule 46.10(4) with an interim charging order relating to securities must not, unless the Court gives permission:

7671.text: (1) permit any transfer of any of the securities; or (2) pay any dividend, interest or redemption payment relating to them.

7673.text: If a person acts in breach of Rule 46.13, he will be liable to pay to the judgment creditor :

7674.text: (1) the value of the securities transferred or the amount of the payment made (as the case may be); or

7675.text: (2) if less, the amount necessary to satisfy the debt in relation to which the interim charging order was made.

7676.text: Effect Of Interim Order In Relation To Funds In Court

7678.text: If a judgment debtor disposes of his interest in funds in Court while they are subject to an interim charging order which has been served on him and on the Registrar in accordance with Rule 46.10, that disposition shall not, so long as that order remains in force, be valid as against the judgment creditor .

7681.text: If any person objects to the Court making a final charging order, he must:

7684.text: written evidence stating the grounds of his objections, not less than 7 days before the hearing.

7686.text: At the hearing the Court may:

7687.text: (1) make a final charging order confirming that the charge imposed by the interim charging order shall continue, with or without modification;

7688.text: (2) discharge the interim charging order and dismiss the application;

7689.text: (3) decide any issues in dispute between the parties, or between any of the parties and any other person who objects to the Court making a final charging order; or

7690.text: (4) direct a trial of any such issues, and if necessary give directions.

7692.text: If the Court makes a final charging order which charges securities other than securities held in Court , the order will include a stop notice unless the Court otherwise orders.

7694.text: Any order made at the hearing must be served on all the persons on whom the interim charging order was required to be served.

7697.text: A charging order shall be discharged upon satisfaction of the judgment or upon further order of the Court .

7699.text: Any application to discharge or vary a charging order must be made in accordance with Part 23.

7702.text: (1) any interested person should be joined as a party to such an application; or

7703.text: (2) the application should be served on any such person.

7705.text: An order discharging or varying a charging order must be served on all the persons on whom the charging order was required to be served.

7706.text: Enforcement Of Charging Order By Sale

7708.text: Subject to the provisions of any enactment, the Court may, upon a claim by a person who has obtained a charging order over an interest in property, order the sale of the property to enforce the charging order.

7710.text: The claimant must use the Part 8 procedure.

7712.text: A copy of the charging order must be filed with the claim form.

7714.text: The written evidence in support of a claim under 46.24 must:

7717.text: (b) the amount owed to each such creditor;

7718.text: (5) give an estimate of the price which would be obtained on sale of the property; and (6) if the claim relates to land, give details of every person who to the best of the claimant’s knowledge is in possession of the property.

7720.text: The claimant must take all reasonable steps to obtain the information required by 46.27(4) before issuing the claim.

7722.text: Sample forms of orders for sale are set out in Schedule A to this Part for guidance. They may be adapted or varied by the Court to meet the requirements of individual cases.

7723.text: Charging Order Against Property Belonging To A Partnership

7725.text: A charging order or interim charging order may be made against any property, within the DIFC , belonging to a judgment debtor that is a partnership.

7727.text: For the purposes of Rule 46.10(1) (service of the interim order), the specified documents must be served on:

7728.text: (1) a member of the partnership within the DIFC or Dubai;

7729.text: (2) a person authorised by a partner; or

7730.text: (3) some other person having the control or management of the partnership business.

7732.text: Where an order requires a partnership to appear before the Court , it will be sufficient for a partner to appear before the Court .

7736.text: In this Section, ‘stop order’ means an order of the Court not to take, in relation to funds in Court or securities specified in the order, any of the following steps:

7741.text: (1) a stop order relating to funds in Court , on the application of any person:

7742.text: (a) who has a mortgage or charge on the interest of any person in the funds; or (b) to whom that interest has been assigned; or (c) who is a judgment creditor of the person entitled to that interest; or

7743.text: (2) a stop order relating to securities other than securities held in Court , on the application of any person claiming to be beneficially entitled to an interest in the securities.

7745.text: An application for a stop order must be made:

7746.text: (1) by application notice in existing proceedings; or (2) by Part 8 claim form if there are no existing proceedings in the Court .

7748.text: The application notice or claim form must be served on:

7749.text:
(1) every person whose interest may be affected by the order applied for; and   
(2) either: (a) the Registrar , if the application relates to funds in Court ; or (b) the person specified in Rule 46.10(4) if the application relates to securities other than securities held in Court .

7750.text: Stop Order Relating To Funds In Court

7752.text: A stop order relating to funds in Court shall prohibit the transfer, sale, delivery out, payment or other dealing with:

7753.text: (1) the funds or any part of them; or (2) any income on the funds.

7756.text: A stop order relating to securities other than securities held in Court may prohibit all or any of the following steps:

7757.text:
(1) the registration of any transfer of the securities;   
(2) the making of any payment by way of dividend, interest or otherwise in respect of the securities; and (3) in the case of units of a unit trust, any acquisition of or other dealing with the units by any person or body exercising functions under the trust.

7763.text: The Court may, on the application of any person claiming to have a beneficial interest in the funds or securities to which a stop order relates, make an order discharging or varying the order.

7765.text: An application notice seeking the variation or discharge of a stop order must be served on the person who obtained the order.

7770.text: (1) ‘stop notice’ means a notice issued by the Court which requires a person or body not to take, in relation to securities specified in the notice, any of the steps listed Rule 46.33, without first giving notice to the person who obtained the notice; and

7771.text: (2) ‘securities’ does not include securities held in Court .

7774.text: The Court may, on the request of any person claiming to be beneficially entitled to an interest in securities , issue a stop notice .

7775.text: (A stop notice may also be included in a final charging order under Rule 46.18)

7777.text: A request for a stop notice must be made by filing :

7781.text: (b) describes the applicant’s interest in the securities ; and

7782.text: (c) gives an address for service for the applicant.

7783.text: (A sample form of stop notice is annexed to this Part at Schedule B)

7785.text: If the Court considers that the request complies with Rule 46.44, it will issue a stop notice .

7787.text: The applicant must serve copies of the stop notice and his written evidence on the person to whom the stop notice is addressed.

7791.text: (1) takes effect when it is served in accordance with Rule 46.46; and

7792.text: (2) remains in force unless it is withdrawn or discharged in accordance with Rules 46.53 and 46.54.

7794.text: While a stop notice is in force, the person on whom it is served:

7796.text: (a) register a transfer of the securities described in the notice; or (b) take any other step restrained by the notice; without first giving 14 days’ notice to the person who obtained the stop notice ; but

7797.text: (2) must not, by reason only of the notice, refuse to register a transfer or to take any other step, after he has given 14 days’ notice under Rule 46.48(1) and that period has expired.

7800.text: If any securities are incorrectly described in a stop notice which has been obtained and served in accordance with Rules 46.43 to 46.46 the applicant may request an amended stop notice .

7802.text: The amended stop notice takes effect when it is served.

7805.text: A person who has obtained a stop notice may withdraw it by serving a request for its withdrawal on:

7806.text: (1) the person or body on whom the stop notice was served; and (2) the Court .

7808.text: The request must be signed by the person who obtained the stop notice , and his signature must be witnessed by a practising lawyer.

7809.text: Discharge Or Variation Of Stop Notice

7811.text: The Court may, on the application of any person claiming to be beneficially entitled to an interest in the securities to which a stop notice relates, make an order discharging or varying the notice.

7812.text: The application notice must be served on the person who obtained the stop notice .

7813.text: IV APPLICATIONS FOR ORDERS CHARGING PARTNER’S INTEREST IN PARTNERSHIP PROPERTY

7815.text: Rules 46.56 to 46.60 relate to orders made under Article 37 of the General Partnership Law 2004 (“Article 37”).

7817.text: The following applications must be made in accordance with Part 23:

7818.text: (1) an application for an order under Article 37 made by a judgment creditor of a partner; (2) an application for any order by a partner of the judgment debtor in consequence of any application made by the judgment creditor under Article 37.

7820.text: The powers conferred on the Court by Article 37 may be exercised by the Registrar .

7822.text: Every application notice filed under Rule 46.56(1) by a judgment creditor , and every order made following such an application, must be served on the judgment debtor and on any of the other partners that are within the DIFC or Dubai.

7824.text: Every application notice filed under Rule 46.56(2) by a partner of a judgment debtor , and every order made following such an application, must be served:

7825.text:
(1) on the judgment creditor and the judgment debtor ; and   
(2) on the other partners of the judgment debtor who are not joined in the application and who are within the DIFC and Dubai.

7827.text: An application notice or order served under Rule 46.57 or 46.59 on one or more, but not all, of the partners of a partnership shall be deemed to have been served on all the partners of that partnership.

7833.text: Scope Of This Part And Interpretation

7835.text: This Part contains rules which provide for a judgment creditor to obtain an order for the payment to him of money which a third party, who is within the DIFC , owes to the judgment debtor .

7837.text: In this Part, ‘bank’ includes any person carrying on a business in the course of which he lawfully accepts deposits in the DIFC .

7840.text: Upon the application of a judgment creditor , the Court may make an order (a ‘final third party debt order’) requiring a third party to pay to the judgment creditor :

7841.text: (1) the amount of any debt due or accruing due to the judgment debtor from the third party; or

7842.text: (2) so much of that debt as is sufficient to satisfy the judgment debt and the judgment creditor’s costs of the application.

7844.text: The Court will not make an order under Rule 47.3 without first making an order (an ‘interim third party debt order’) as provided by Rules 47.11 to 47.15.

7846.text: In deciding whether money standing to the credit of the judgment debtor in an account may be made the subject of a third party debt order, the following conditions will be disregarded:
