8695.text: Parts 19 and 28 shall, with the necessary modifications, apply in relation to an interpleader issue as they apply in relation to any other proceedings.

8698.text: Part 35 shall, with the necessary modifications, apply to the trial of an interpleader issue as it applies to the trial of a claim.

8700.text: The Court may give such judgment or make such order as is necessary finally to dispose of all questions arising in the interpleader proceedings.

8704.text:
52.1   
The power of the Court of First Instance or Court of Appeal to punish for contempt of Court may be exercised by an order of committal.

8706.text: An order of committal may be made by a single Judge of the Court .

8708.text:
52.3   
An application for an order of committal must be made by claim form or application notice and be supported by an affidavit .

8709.text: 52.4 A committal application must, subject to Rule 52.5, be commenced by the issue of a Part 8 claim form.

8710.text:
52.5   
If the committal application is made in existing proceedings it must be commenced by the filing of an application notice in those proceedings.

8712.text: An application to commit for breach of an undertaking or order must be commenced by the filing of an

8713.text: application notice in the proceedings in which the undertaking was given or the order was made.

8715.text: The application notice must state that the application is made in the proceedings in question and its title and reference number must correspond with the title and reference number of those proceedings.

8717.text: If the committal application is commenced by the issue of a claim form, Part 8 shall, subject to the provisions of this Part, apply as though references to ‘claimant’ were references to the person making the committal application and references to ‘defendant’ were references to the person against whom the committal application is made (in this practice direction referred to as ‘the respondent’) but:

8718.text: (1) the claim form together with copies of all written evidence in support must, unless the Court otherwise directs, be served personally on the respondent;

8719.text: (2) the claim form must set out in full the grounds on which the committal application is made and must identify, separately and numerically, each alleged act of contempt including, if known, the date of each alleged act;

8720.text: (3) an amendment to the claim form can be made with the permission of the Court but not otherwise;

8722.text: (5) the claim form must contain a prominent notice stating the possible consequences of the Court making a committal order and of the respondent not attending the hearing. A form of notice, which may be used, is annexed to this Part at Schedule A.

8724.text: If a committal application is commenced by the filing of an application notice , Part 23 shall, subject to the provisions of this Part, apply, but:

8725.text: (1) the application notice together with copies of all written evidence in support must, unless the Court otherwise directs, be served personally on the respondent;

8726.text: (2) the application notice must set out in full the grounds on which the committal application is made and must identify, separately and numerically, each alleged act of contempt including, if known, the date of each of the alleged acts;

8727.text: (3) an amendment to the application notice can be made with the permission of the Court but not otherwise;

8728.text: (4) the Court may not dispose of the committal application without a hearing; and

8729.text: (5) the application notice must contain a prominent notice stating the possible consequences of the Court making a committal order and of the respondent not attending the hearing. A form of notice, which may be used, is annexed to this Part at Schedule A.

8731.text: Without prejudice to its powers under Part 9, the Court may dispense with service under Rule 52.8(1) or Rule 52.9(1) if it thinks it just to do so.

8732.text: Saving For Power To Commit Without Application

8734.text: Nothing in this Part shall be taken as affecting the power of the Court of First Instance or Court of Appeal to make an order of committal on its own initiative against a person guilty of contempt of Court .

8737.text: Written evidence in support of or in opposition to a committal application must be given by affidavit .

8739.text: Written evidence served in support of or in opposition to a committal application must, unless the Court otherwise directs, be filed.

8741.text: A respondent may give oral evidence at the hearing, whether or not he has filed or served any written evidence. If he does so, he may be cross-examined.

8743.text: A respondent may, with the permission of the Court , call a witness to give oral evidence at the hearing whether or not the witness has sworn an affidavit .

8746.text: The applicant for the committal order must, when lodging the claim form or application notice with the Court for issuing or filing , as the case may be, obtain from the Court a date for the hearing of the committal application.

8748.text: Unless the Court otherwise directs, the hearing date of a committal application shall be not less than 14 days after service of the claim form or of the application notice , as the case may be, on the respondent. The hearing date must be specified in the claim form or application notice or in a Notice of Hearing or Application attached to and served with the claim form or application notice .

8750.text: The Court may, however, at any time give case management directions, including directions for the service

8751.text: of written evidence by the respondent and written evidence in reply by the applicant, or may hold a directions hearing.

8753.text: The Court may on the hearing date:

8754.text: (1) give case management directions with a view to a hearing of the committal application on a future date; or

8755.text: (2) if the committal application is ready to be heard, proceed to hear it.

8757.text: In dealing with any committal application, the Court will have regard to the need for the respondent to have details of the alleged acts of contempt and the opportunity to respond to the committal application.

8759.text: The Court should also have regard to the need for the respondent to be:

8760.text: (1) allowed a reasonable time for responding to the committal application including, if necessary, preparing a defence;

8761.text: (2) given the opportunity, if unrepresented, to obtain legal advice; and

8762.text: (3) if unable to understand English, allowed to make arrangements, seeking the assistance of the Court if necessary, for an interpreter to attend the hearing.

8765.text: The Court may, on application by the respondent or on its own initiative, strike out a committal application if it appears to the Court:

8766.text: (1) that the committal application and the evidence served in support of it disclose no reasonable ground for alleging that the respondent is guilty of a contempt of Court ;

8767.text: (2) that the committal application is an abuse of the Court’s process or, if made in existing proceedings, is otherwise likely to obstruct the just disposal of those proceedings; or

8768.text: (3) that there has been a failure to comply with a Rule, Practice Direction or Court order.

8771.text: Rules 31.29 to 31.49 do not apply to committal applications.

8773.text: An order under Rule 19.1 may not be made against a respondent to a committal application.

8775.text: A committal application may not be discontinued without the permission of the Court .

8777.text: The Court may waive any procedural defect in the commencement or conduct of a committal application if satisfied that no injustice has been caused to the respondent by the defect.

8780.text: A committal application should normally be heard in public.

8782.text: The Court hearing an application for an order of committal may sit in private in the following cases, that is to say:

8785.text: If the Court hearing an application in private by virtue of Rule 52.28 decides to make an order of committal against the person sought to be committed, it shall state in public:

8786.text:
(1) the name of the respondent;   
(2) in general terms the nature of the contempt or contempts found proved; and (3) the penalty (if any) imposed.

8788.text: Except with the permission of the Court hearing an application for an order of committal, no grounds shall be relied upon at the hearing except the grounds set out in the claim form or application notice .

8790.text: If on the hearing of the application the person sought to be committed expresses a wish to give oral

8791.text: evidence on his own behalf, he shall be entitled to do so.

8792.text: Power To Suspend Execution Of Committal Order

8794.text: The Court may by order direct that the execution of the order of committal shall be suspended for such period or on such terms or conditions as it may specify.

8796.text: Where execution of an order of committal is suspended by an order under Rule 52.32, the applicant for the order of committal must, unless the Court otherwise directs, serve on the person against whom it was made a notice informing him of the making and terms of the order under that Rule.

8797.text: Contempt In The Face Of The Court

8799.text: Where the committal application relates to a contempt in the face of the Court the following matters should be given particular attention. Normally, it will be appropriate to defer consideration of the behaviour to allow the respondent time to reflect on what has occurred. The time needed for the following procedures should allow for such a period of reflection.

8801.text: A Part 8 claim form and an application notice are not required for committal for contempt in the face of the Court , but other provisions of this Part should be applied, as necessary, or adapted to the circumstances. In addition the Judge should:

8803.text: (5) allow the respondent an opportunity to:

8805.text: (b) explain his actions and behaviour; and

8806.text: (c) if the contempt is proved, address the Court on the penalty to be imposed on him;

8807.text: (6) if there is a risk of the appearance of bias, ask another Judge to hear the committal application; and (7) where appropriate, nominate a suitable person to give the respondent the information.

8808.text: (It is likely to be appropriate to nominate a person where the effective communication of information by the Judge to the respondent was not possible when the incident occurred).

8810.text: Where the committal application is to be heard by another Judge , a written statement by the Judge before whom the actions and behaviour of the respondent which have given rise to the committal application took place may be submitted as evidence of those actions and behaviour.

8813.text: Nothing in this Part shall be taken as affecting the power of the Court:

8814.text: (1) to make an order referring the matter to the Attorney General of Dubai; or

8815.text: (2) to make any other order it considers necessary in the interests of justice (including the giving security for his good behaviour), pursuant to Article 43 of the Court Law 2004; or

8816.text: (3) to make an order requiring a person guilty of contempt of Court , or a person punishable by virtue of any enactment in like manner as if he had been guilty of contempt of Court , to pay a fine or to give security for his good behaviour;

8817.text: and the provisions of this Part, so far as applicable, and with the necessary modifications, shall apply in relation to an application for such an order as they apply in relation to an application for an order of committal.

8823.text: (1) special procedure for dealing with claims (“small claims”) which are issued in or have been transferred to the Small Claims Tribunal (“the SCT”); and

8824.text: (2) limits the amount of costs that can be recovered in respect of a small claim.

8826.text: The SCT will hear and determine claims within the jurisdiction of the DIFC Courts:

8827.text: (1) where the amount of the claim or the value of the subject matter of the claim does not exceed AED 500,000 or;

8828.text: (2) where the claim relates to the employment or former employment of a party; and

8829.text: all parties elect in writing that it be heard by the SCT (there is no value limit for the SCT’s elective jurisdiction in the context of employment claims); or

8830.text: (3) which do not fall within the provisions of sub-paragraphs (1) or (2) above, but in respect of which:

8831.text: (a) the amount of the claim or the value of the subject matter of the claim does not exceed AED 1,000,000; and

8832.text: (b) all parties to the claim elect in writing that it be heard by the SCT, and such election is made in the underlying contract (if any) or subsequent to execution of that contract

8833.text: (4) such other claims as may be ordered or directed by the Chief Justice to be heard by the SCT from time to time.

8835.text: (1) Where parties have elected the SCT’s jurisdiction in writing, neither party shall be able to withdraw such election without the approval of an SCT Judge.

8836.text: (2) Where a claimant issues multiple claims against the same defendant, the SCT Judge may, where appropriate treat those claims as a single consolidated claim under Rule 4.2(7) for the purposes of deciding whether the amount in dispute exceeds the limits of the SCT’s jurisdiction.

8838.text: Reference in this Part to “claims” shall include reference to counterclaims, save that, where a counterclaim would not otherwise be within the SCT’s jurisdiction, an SCT Judge may direct that the proceedings be transferred to the Court of First Instance

8841.text: References in this Part to filing a document are references to filing that document with the SCT Registrar, unless stated otherwise.

8842.text: SCT Judge’s power to grant a final remedy

8844.text: The SCT Judge may grant any final remedy in relation to a small claim which a Judge of the Court of First Instance of the DIFC Courts could grant if the proceedings were before that Court.

8845.text: Extent To Which Other Parts Apply

8847.text: The following Parts of the RDC shall apply to small claims except to the extent that a Rule limits such application or the SCT Judge orders otherwise:

8850.text: The SCT of its own initiative may order a party to provide further information if it considers it appropriate to do so.

8853.text: A small claim must be started by a Claim Form using Form P53/01.

8855.text: A small claim is started when the SCT Registrar issues a Claim Form at the request of a party.

8857.text: The claimant must set out in or attach to the Claim Form a statement summarising the remedy sought and the claimant’s reasons for claiming that he is entitled to that remedy. The claimant must include on the face of the Claim Form a statement of the monetary value of the small claim.

8859.text: The claimant must include in the Claim Form the name and address for service of the defendant, together with any other available contact information. Where the defendant is a company, the claimant shall state the address where, to the best of his knowledge, the company carries on its business.

8861.text: The SCT will serve the claim form on defendant, unless otherwise directed by the Registry.

8864.text: Within 7 days after he is served with a claim form in respect of a matter which has been referred to the SCT, a defendant must:

8865.text: (1) Admit the claim by filing and serving on the claimant an admission in accordance with RDC Part 15;

8866.text: (2) File a defence to the claim setting out (a) which parts of the claim are admitted; (b) which parts are denied and his reasons for denying those parts; and (c) the details of any counterclaim; or

8867.text: (3) Make an application to dispute the jurisdiction of the SCT, supported by evidence.

8869.text: Where the defendant admits the claim in accordance with Rule 53.14(1), the SCT shall issue an order giving judgment on the claim.

8871.text: Where the defendant files and serves an application to dispute the jurisdiction in accordance with Rule 53.14(3), the application will be heard by an SCT Judge in accordance with RDC Part 23.”

8874.text: After the defendant has filed and served a defence in accordance with Rule 53.14(2) and/or after the time for filing such a statement has passed, the SCT will fix a time for the parties to attend before an SCT Judge for a consultation.

8876.text: The primary purpose of the consultation will be to allow the parties to attempt to resolve their dispute.

8878.text: The SCT may treat the consultation as the final hearing of the claim if all the parties agree.

8880.text:
The SCT will generally fix the consultation within 7 days after the time for filing a defence has expired (i.e.   
within 14 days after service of the claim form).

8882.text: When filing the Claim Formthe claimant should inform the SCT of any dates within that period on which he is unable to attend the consultation and his reasons for being unable to attend.

8884.text: When filing a defence, the defendant should inform the SCT of any dates within that period on which he is unable to attend the consultation and his reasons for being unable to attend.

8886.text: The SCT will attempt to fix the consultation on a date on which both parties are able to attend.

8888.text: If any party is unable to attend the consultation on the date fixed, he must notify the SCT and the other party as soon as possible and give his reasons for being unable to attend. If it appears to the SCT that the party has a good reason for being unable to attend the consultation, the SCT may adjourn the consultation and fix a new date.

8890.text: It is essential that each party attends the consultation in person. Parties should not be represented by a lawyer. A party may be represented by a non-lawyer where it appears to the SCT that it is reasonably necessary.

8892.text: Any of its full-time officers or employees may represent a corporate party at the consultation.

8894.text: Each party should file with his claim form or defence any documents on which he wishes to rely at the consultation.

8896.text: If a party fails to attend the consultation, the SCT Judge may:

8897.text: (1) decide the small claim against that party; or (2) adjourn the consultation.

8899.text: If neither party attends the consultation, the SCT Judge may:

8900.text: (1) dismiss the claim; or (2) adjourn the consultation.

8902.text: A party who was neither present nor represented at the consultation and against whom the claim has been decided in accordance with Rule 53.28 or Rule 53.29 may apply for that order to be set aside and the claim reinstated.

8904.text: A party who applies for an Order to be set aside in accordance with Rule 53.30, must make the application not more than 7 days after the day on which notice of the Order was served on him.

8906.text: The SCT may grant an application under Rule 53.30 only if the applicant:

8907.text: (1) had a good reason for not attending the consultation; and (2) has a real prospect of success in the small claim.

8909.text: If the SCT grants an application to set aside an Order under Rule 53.30:

8910.text:
(1) the SCT will fix a new date for the consultation; and   
(2) the consultation may take place immediately after the hearing of the application to set aside the Order and may be dealt with by the SCT Judge who set aside the Order.

8912.text: Unless the SCT Judge orders otherwise, the consultation shall take place in private.

8914.text: If the claim is settled at the consultation, the SCT Judge conducting the consultation will issue a consent order, recording the terms of the settlement.

8916.text: If the claim is not settled at the consultation, the SCT Judge may either:

8917.text: (1) fix a date for a further consultation; or

8918.text: (2) make arrangements for the hearing of the claim in accordance with RDC Rules 53.38 to 53.42 below.

8921.text: Where appropriate, the SCT Judge may order that the small claim be transferred to the Court of First Instance. When deciding whether to do so, the SCT Judge shall have regard to the following matters:

8925.text: If the claim is not settled at the consultation, unless he fixes a further consultation or Rule 53.37 applies, the SCT Judge will give directions for the preparation of the small claim for trial.

8927.text: The SCT Judge at the consultation will generally:

8930.text: A party may ask the SCT Judge at the consultation to give particular directions about the conduct of the case.

8932.text: In deciding whether to make an order for exchange of witness statements the SCT Judge will have regard to the following:

8935.text: No expert may give evidence, whether written or oral, at a hearing without the permission of the SCT.

8936.text: Power Of The Sct To Add To, Vary Or Revoke Directions

8938.text: The SCT may add to, vary or revoke directions.

8941.text: Unless the parties agree otherwise, the hearing of the claim will not be conducted by the same SCT Judge who conducted the consultation.

8943.text: The SCT Judge may adopt any method of proceeding at a hearing that he considers to be fair.

8947.text: The general rule is that a small claim hearing will be in private, unless the parties agree or the SCT Judge orders otherwise.

8949.text: The strict rules of evidence do not apply.

8951.text: The SCT Judge may take evidence on oath but is not required to do so.

8953.text: The SCT Judge may limit cross-examination.

8955.text: The SCT Judge may in particular:

8956.text: (1) ask questions of all or any of the witnesses himself before allowing any other person to ask questions of any witnesses;

8957.text: (2) refuse to allow cross-examination of any witness until all the witnesses have given evidence in chief; and/or

8958.text: (3) limit cross-examination of a witness to a fixed time or to a particular subject or issue, or both.

8962.text: (1) Unless the SCT Judge orders otherwise, a party should present his own case at a hearing.

8963.text: (2) A party may be represented at the hearing by a non-lawyer or lawyer[1] only after obtaining permission from the SCT which is to be given where it appears to the SCT on reasonable grounds that it is necessary in the circumstances.

8964.text: (3) If a party is allowed to be represented by a lawyer, or in house Counsel as permitted under Rule 53.53, the opposing party shall be informed and given the opportunity to be represented at the hearing.

8965.text: (4) Parties’ attention is drawn to RDC Part 53.70.

8968.text: Any of its full-time officers or employees (including in house Counsel) may represent a corporate party.

8970.text: The SCT will take a minute of or otherwise record by such means as the SCT Judge considers appropriate any hearing that takes place at the SCT. A party may obtain a copy of that minute or other recording on payment of the proper charges specified by the SCT.

8972.text: It is not permissible for a party to use its own recording devices in the SCT.

8974.text: The SCT Judge must give reasons for his decision.

8976.text: The SCT Judge may give reasons for his judgment as briefly and simply as the nature of the case requires.

8978.text: He will normally do so orally at the hearing, but he may give them later at a hearing either orally or in writing.

8980.text: Where the SCT Judge decides the case without a hearing under Rule 53.64, the SCT Judge will prepare a note of his reasons and the SCT will send a copy to each party.

8981.text: Non-attendance of parties at a final hearing

8983.text: If a claimant does not attend the hearing, the SCT may strike out the claim.

8985.text: If a defendant does not attend the hearing and the claimant does attend the hearing, the SCT may decide the claim on the basis of the evidence of the claimant alone.

8987.text: If neither party attends the hearing, the SCT may strike out the claim and any defence and counterclaim.

8989.text: Nothing in these provisions affects the general power of the SCT to adjourn a hearing, for example where a party who wishes to attend a hearing on the date fixed cannot do so for a good reason.

8992.text: The SCT may, if all parties agree, deal with the claim without a hearing.

8995.text: A party who was not present at the hearing of the claim may apply for an order that a Judgment under this Part shall be set aside and the claim re-heard.

8997.text: A party who applies for an order setting aside a Judgment under Rule 53.65 must make the application not more than 7 days after the day on which notice of the Judgment was served on him.

8999.text: The SCT may grant an application under Rule 53.65 only if the applicant:

9000.text: (1) had a good reason for not attending the hearing; and (2) has a real prospect of success at the hearing.

9002.text: If a Judgment is set aside:

9003.text: (1) the SCT must fix a new hearing for the claim; and (2) the hearing may take place immediately after the hearing of the application to set the Judgment aside and may be dealt with by the SCT Judge who set aside the Judgment.

9005.text: A party may not apply to set aside a Judgment under this rule if the SCT dealt with the claim without a hearing under Rule 53.64.

9008.text: The SCT may not order a party to a small claim to pay a sum to another party in respect of that other party’s costs, fees and expenses, including those relating to an appeal, except:

9009.text: (1) such part of any Court or Tribunal fees paid by that other party as the SCT may consider appropriate; (2) such further costs as the SCT may assess by the summary procedure and order to be paid by a party who has behaved unreasonably.

9011.text: A party’s rejection of an offer in settlement will not of itself constitute unreasonable behaviour under Rule 53.70(2), but the SCT may take it into consideration when it is applying the unreasonableness test.

9012.text: Claim re-allocated from the SCT to the Court of First Instance

9014.text: Where a claim is transferred from the SCT to the Court of First Instance, Rule 53.70 (costs in the SCT) will cease to apply after the claim has been transferred and the Costs Rules set out in RDC Parts 38–40 will apply from the date of transfer.

9017.text: An Order of the SCT shall have the same status as an Order f the Court of First Instance of the DIFC Courts and may be enforced in accordance with the rules and procedures set out in RDC Parts 45–50.

9019.text: Applications for the enforcement of an Order of the SCT should be made to the Court of First Instance.

9024.text: Attention is drawn to FRDC 44.36 / ARDC 44.10 which specifies that, subject to any Order of the SCT, an appellant should file his appellant’s notice seeking permission to appeal with the Court of First Instance within 14 days (FRDC) or 21 days (ARDC) after the date of the decision which the appellant wishes to appeal.

9026.text: Attention is also drawn to FRDC 44.143 / ARDC 44.118 which sets out the circumstances in which the Court of First Instance will allow an appeal from a decision of a Tribunal.

9028.text: An appellant’s Notice must be filed and served in Form P53/02.

9030.text: Where the SCT dealt with the claim to which the appellant is a party under Rule 53.64 without a hearing, an application for permission to appeal must be made to the Court of First Instance.

9032.text: Where an appeal is allowed the Appeal Court will, if possible, dispose of the case at the same time without referring the claim to the lower court or ordering a new hearing. It may do so without hearing further evidence.

9034.text: Permission to appeal an SCT Judgment may be made subject to conditions when the Judge granting permission considers it appropriate to do so. Such conditions may include the costs of appeal.

9035.text: Waiver of fees or fee deferral

9037.text: Where a party seeks an order that the Court fee associated with his claim should be waived:

9038.text: (1) He must state this in his claim form;

9039.text: (2) He must set out in his claim form or attach to his claim form a statement of the grounds on which he seeks waiver of the court fees;

9040.text: (3) The SCT Registrar shall decide on paper whether to waive the fees or defer payment of the fees until the end of the case;

9041.text: (4) Where Court fees are waived, the SCT Registry shall record that fact on the case file.

9048.text: “block transfer order” has the meaning in Rule 54.228;

9049.text: “the Companies Law” means the Companies Law 2009;

9050.text: “the Court Law” means the DIFC Courts Law 2004;

9051.text: “insolvency proceedings” means any proceedings under the Law, the Regulations or this Part of these Rules ;

9053.text: “office-holder” means in relation to insolvency proceedings any person who by virtue of any provision of the Law or the Regulations holds an office in relation to those proceedings;

9054.text: “outgoing office-holder” has the meaning in Rule 54.227;

9055.text: “the petitioner” or “the petitioning creditor” includes, in winding up, any person who has been substituted as such, or has been given carriage of the petition;

9056.text: “the Regulations” means the Insolvency Regulations 2009 (and any reference to a numbered regulation is to that regulation within the Regulations);

9057.text: “replacement office-holder” has the meaning in Rule 54.227; and

9058.text: “substantive application” is that part of the application in Rule 54.230(1) and 54.230(2)

9060.text: For the purposes of this Part a “Company” includes:

9061.text: 54.2.1 a Recognised Company in respect of which a winding-up order is sought or which is being wound up by the Court pursuant to Part 6 of the Law save as otherwise provided or the context otherwise requires; and

9062.text: 54.2.2 a Foreign Company where and insofar as the context requires.

9064.text: Other terms which have a defined meaning in the Law, the Companies Law, the Regulations and these Rules have the same meaning in this Part.

9067.text: The provisions of Rules 2.11 to 2.15 (Time) apply, as regards computation of time, to anything required or authorised to be done in this Part.

9069.text: The provisions of Rule 4.2(1) (The Court’s general powers of management) apply so as to enable the Court to extend or shorten the time for compliance with anything required or authorised to be done by this Part.

9072.text: Save where these Rules provide otherwise, anything to be done under or by virtue of the Law, the Regulations or this Part of the Rules by, to or before the Court may be done by, to or before a Judge or a Registrar.

9076.text: This Section applies to any application made to the Court under the Law or the Regulations except (save as otherwise provided):

9077.text: (1) a petition for a winding-up order under Article 50 of the Law (see Section III);

9078.text: (2) an application for the appointment of a provisional liquidator (see Section IV); and

9079.text: (3) an application for a cell receivership order (see Section V).

9082.text: Each application shall be in writing and shall state:

9083.text: (1) that the application is made under the Law or the Regulations (as the case may be);

9085.text: (3) the name of the Company which is the subject of the insolvency proceedings to which the application relates;

9086.text: (4) where the Court has previously allocated a reference number to the insolvency proceedings within which the application is made, that number;

9087.text: (5) the nature of the remedy or order applied for or the directions sought from the Court ;

9088.text: (6) the names and addresses of the persons (if any) on whom it is intended to serve the application or that no person is intended to be served;

9089.text: (7) where the Law, the Regulations or these Rules require that notice of the application is to be given to specified persons, the names and addresses of all those persons (so far as known to the applicant); and

9093.text: On receipt of an application notice containing a request for a hearing the Court will notify the applicant of the time and date for the hearing of the application.

9095.text: Unless the Court otherwise directs, the applicant shall serve a sealed copy of the application, endorsed with the time and date for the hearing, on the respondent named in the application (or on each respondent if more than one).

9097.text: The Court may give any of the following directions:

9098.text: (1) that the application be served upon persons other than those specified by the relevant provision of the Law, the Regulations or these Rules ;

9099.text: (2) that the giving of notice to any person may be dispensed with; and

9100.text: (3) that notice be given in some way other than that specified in Rule 54.10.

9102.text: An application must be served at least fourteen (14) calendar days before the date fixed for its hearing unless:

9103.text: (1) the provision of the Law or the Regulations under which the application is made makes different provision; or

9104.text: (2) the case is one of urgency to which Rule 54.13 applies.

9106.text: Where the case is one of urgency, the Court may (without prejudice to its general power to extend or abridge time limits):

9107.text: (1) hear the application immediately, either with or without notice to, or the attendance of, other parties; or

9108.text: (2) authorise a shorter period of service than that provided for by Rule 54.12;

9109.text: and any such application may be heard on terms providing for the filing or service of documents, or the carrying out of other formalities, as the Court thinks fit.

9112.text: Where the relevant provisions of the Law or the Regulations do not require service of the application on, or notice of it to be given to, any person:

9113.text: (1) the Court may hear the application as soon as reasonably practicable without fixing a time and date for the hearing as required by Rule 54.9; or

9114.text: (2) it may fix a time and date for the hearing in which case Rules 54.9 to 54.13 shall apply to the extent that they are relevant;

9115.text: but nothing in those provisions is to be taken as prohibiting the applicant from giving such notice if the applicant wishes to do so.

9118.text: Subject to Rules 54.20 to 54.21, where evidence is required by the Law or the Regulations as to any matter, such evidence may be provided in the form of a witness statement unless:

9119.text: 54.15.1 in any specific case, the Act, a Regulation or a Rule makes different provision; or

9122.text: Witness statements and affidavits must comply with the requirements of Part 29 (Evidence), save that photocopy documents may be used unless the Court orders otherwise.

9124.text: Where in insolvency proceedings a witness statement is made by an office-holder, the witness statement must state:

9125.text: (1) the capacity in which that office-holder is acting; and (2) the address at which that office-holder works.

9127.text: The Court may, on the application of any party to the matter in question order the attendance for crossexamination of the person making the witness statement.

9129.text: Where, after such an order has been made, the person in question does not attend, that person’s witness statement must not be used in evidence without the permission of the Court .

9132.text: Unless the application involves other parties or the Court otherwise orders, a report may be filed in Court instead of a witness statement by:

9135.text: In any case where a report is filed instead of a witness statement, the report shall be treated for the purposes of Rule 54.22 and any hearing before the Court as if it were a witness statement.

9136.text: Filing And Service Of Witness Statements

9138.text: Unless the provision of the Law or the Regulations under which the application is made provides otherwise, or the Court otherwise allows:

9139.text: (1) if the applicant intends to rely at the first hearing on evidence in a witness statement, he shall file the witness statement with the Court and serve a copy on the respondent (or on each respondent if more than one);

9140.text: (2) where a respondent to an application intends to oppose it and to rely for that purpose on evidence in a witness statement, he shall file the witness statement with the Court and serve a copy on the applicant (or on each applicant if more than one) and on every other respondent; and

9141.text: (3) the timetable for the filing and service of witness statements shall be as set out in Rules 23.40 to 23.48.

9144.text: Unless the Court otherwise directs, the application will be heard in public in accordance with Article 13 of the Court Law .

9146.text: The jurisdiction of the Court to hear and determine an application may be exercised by the Registrar (to whom any application should be made in the first instance) unless:

9147.text:
(1) a direction to the contrary has been given; or   
(2) it is not within the Registrar’s power to make the order required (whether pursuant to Part 3 (Organisation of the Court) or otherwise).

9149.text: Nothing in Rule 54.24 precludes an application being made directly to the Judge in a proper case.

9151.text: Where an application is made to the Registrar , the Registrar may refer to the Judge any matter which the Registrar thinks should properly be decided by the Judge , and the Judge may either dispose of the matter or refer it back to the Registrar with such directions as that Judge thinks fit.

9154.text: The Court may adjourn the hearing of an application on such terms as it thinks fit.

9156.text: The Court may at any time give such directions as it thinks fit as to:

9157.text: (1) service or notice of the application on or to any person; (2) whether particulars of claim and defence are to be delivered and generally as to the procedure on

9158.text: the application, including whether a hearing is necessary; and

9159.text: (3) the matters to be dealt with in evidence.

9161.text: The Court may give directions as to the manner in which any evidence is to be adduced at a resumed hearing and in particular as to:

9162.text: (1) the taking of evidence wholly or partly by witness statement or orally; (2) the cross-examination of the maker of a witness statement; and (3) any report to be made by an office-holder.

9165.text: In any insolvency proceedings, orders of the Court may be enforced in the same manner as a judgment to the same effect in accordance with Part 45 (General Rules About Enforcement of Judgments and Orders).

9166.text: III PETITION FOR A WINDING-UP ORDER

9169.text: The Rules in this Section apply in relation to a winding up by the Court under Part 4, Chapter 5, or Part 6 of the Law.

9171.text: For the purposes of this Section, the term “a Company” includes a Recognised Company (with such modifications as may be necessary).

9172.text: Injunction To Restrain Presentation Or Advertisement Of Petition

9174.text: An application may at any time be made by a Company for an injunction to restrain a person from:

9175.text: (1) presenting a petition for the winding up of a Company; or (2) advertising a petition for the winding up of a Company.

9177.text: Such application should be made in accordance with Section II of this Part.

9180.text: The petition, verified by a statement of truth in accordance with Part 22 (Statements of Truth) and Rules 54.40 to 54.45, shall be filed in Court.

9182.text: There must be annexed to the petition (and each copy thereof) a copy of any written demand which has been served on the Company by the petitioner pursuant to Article 51.1(a) of the Law and Regulation 5.1.

9184.text: There shall be delivered with the petition:

9185.text: (1) if the petitioner is a person other than the Company, one copy for service on the Company;

9186.text: (2) if any office-holder has been appointed in respect of the Company or the assets of the Company (or any of them), one copy for service on each such office-holder; and

9187.text: (3) if the Company is an Authorised Person and the petitioner is not the DFSA , one copy for service on the DFSA for notification purposes.

9189.text: Each of the copies delivered shall have applied to it the seal of the Court , and shall be issued to the petitioner.

9191.text: The Court shall fix a date and time for the hearing of the petition, and this shall be endorsed on any copy of the petition issued to the petitioner under Rule 54.38.

9194.text: The petition shall be verified by a statement of truth in such form as the Court may specify.

9196.text: If the petition is in respect of debts due to different creditors, the debts to each creditor must be separately verified.

9198.text: A statement of truth which is not contained in or endorsed upon the petition which it verifies must be sufficient to identify the petition and must specify:

9199.text: (1) the Court reference number of the petition;

9200.text: (2) the name and registered number of the Company; and

9203.text: The statement of truth must be made:

9204.text: (1) by the petitioner (or if there are two or more petitioners, any one of them); (2) by some person such as a director, company secretary or similar company officer, or a legal representative, who has been concerned in the matters giving rise to the presentation of the petition; or (3) by some responsible person who is duly authorised by the petitioner to make the statement of truth and has the requisite knowledge of those matters.

9206.text: Where the person making the statement of truth is not the petitioner himself, or one of the petitioners, he must in the statement of truth identify himself and state:

9207.text: (1) the capacity in which, and the authority by which, he makes it; and

9208.text: (2) the means of his knowledge of the matters verified in the statement of truth.

9210.text: A statement of truth verifying more than one petition shall include in its title the names of the Companies to which it relates and shall set out, in respect of each Company, the statements relied on by the petitioner; and a clear and legible photocopy of the statement of truth must be filed with each petition which it verifies.

9211.text: Consent Of The Insolvency Practitioner Proposed For Appointment As Liquidator

9213.text: The petition shall contain the name and address of the insolvency practitioner whom the petitioner proposes should be appointed as liquidator or provisional liquidator of the Company in the event that a winding-up order is made by the Court and a statement that the person so nominated is (to the best of the petitioner’s belief) a registered insolvency practitioner for the purposes of Article 89 of the Law and has consented to such appointment. There shall be attached to the statement of truth a copy of the written consent to such appointment signed by the insolvency practitioner so nominated.

9216.text: Rules 54.48 to 54.52 apply as regards service of the petition on the Company (where the petitioner is other than the Company itself); and references to the petition are to a copy of the petition bearing the seal of the Court .

9218.text: Subject as follows, the petition shall be served at the Company’s registered office.

9220.text: Service of the petition at the registered office may be effected in any of the following ways:

9221.text: (1) it may be handed to a person who there and then acknowledges himself to be, or the best of the server’s knowledge, information and belief is, a director or other officer, or employee, of the Company;

9222.text: (2) it may be handed to a person who there and then acknowledges himself to be authorised to accept service of documents on the Company’s behalf; or

9223.text: (3) in the absence of any such person as is mentioned in paragraphs 54.49.1 or 54.49.2, it may be deposited at or about the registered office in such a way that it is likely to come to the notice of a person attending at the office.

9225.text: If for any reason service at the registered office is not practicable, or the Company has no registered office, the petition may be served on the Company by leaving at the Company’s last known principal place of business in such a way that it is likely to come to the attention of a person attending there, or by delivering it to the secretary or some director, manager or principal officer of the Company, wherever that person may be found.

9227.text: If for any reason it is impracticable to effect service as provided by Rules 54.49 or 54.50, the petition may be served in such other manner as the Court may by order approve or direct.

9229.text: Application for an order of the Court under Rule 54.51 may be made by an application without notice to any other party, supported by a witness statement, stating what steps have been taken to comply with Rules 54.49 and 54.50 and the reasons why it is impracticable to effect service as there provided.

9232.text: Service of the petition must be proved by a certificate of service.

9234.text: The certificate of service must be sufficient to identify the petition served and must specify:

9235.text: (1) the name and registered office of the Company;

9236.text: (2) the address of the registered office of the Company;

9239.text: Where substituted service has been ordered under Rule 54.51, the certificate of service must have attached to it a sealed copy of the order.

9241.text: The certificate of service must be filed in Court as soon as reasonably practicable after service, and in any event not less than five (5) business days before the hearing of the petition.

9242.text: Other Persons To Receive Copies Of Petition Or Entitled To Copy Of Petition

9244.text: If to the petitioner’s knowledge any office-holder has been appointed in respect of the Company or the assets of the Company (or any of them), a copy of the petition shall be sent by him to such office-holder.

9246.text: If to the petitioner’s knowledge the Company is an Authorised Person and the petitioner is not the DFSA itself, a copy of the petition shall be sent by the petitioner to the DFSA .

9248.text: A copy of the petition which is required to be sent pursuant to Rules 54.57 or 54.58 shall be despatched on the next business day after the day on which the petition is served on the Company.

9250.text: Subject to any order of the Court to the contrary every director, contributory and creditor of the Company is entitled to be furnished by the legal representative of the petitioner (or by the petitioner himself, if acting in person) with a copy of the petition within two (2) business days after requiring it on payment of reasonable copying charges.

9253.text: The petitioner shall cause the petition to be advertised, in accordance with Regulation 5.3, in the English

9254.text: language newspaper(s) and in the Arabic language newspaper(s) as the Court may by Practice Direction designate.

9256.text: Unless the Court otherwise directs, the notice must be made to appear:

9257.text: (1) if the petitioner is the Company itself, not less than seven (7) business days before the day appointed for the hearing; or

9258.text: (2) otherwise, not less than seven (7) business days after service of the petition on the Company, nor less than seven (7) business days before the day so appointed.

9260.text: In addition to the standard contents (as set out in Section VII), the notice must state:

9261.text: (1) that a petition has been presented for the winding up of the Company;

9262.text: (2) the name and address of the petitioner;

9263.text: (3) the date on which the petition was presented;

9264.text: (4) the date and time fixed for the hearing of the petition;

9265.text: (5) the name and address of the petitioner’s legal representative (if any);

9266.text: (6) the name and address of the insolvency practitioner whom the petitioner proposes for appointment as liquidator; and

9267.text: (7) that any person intending to appear at the hearing (whether to support or oppose the petition or the appointment of the insolvency practitioner proposed for appointment as liquidator) must give notice of that intention in accordance with Rules 54.70 to 54.73.

9269.text: Non-compliance with Rules 54.61 to 54.63, is a ground on which the Court may, if it thinks fit, dismiss the petition.

9272.text: The petitioner or his legal representative shall, at least five (5) business days before the hearing of the petition, file in Court a certificate of compliance with the Rules , in such form as the Court may specify, relating to service and advertisement.

9277.text: A copy or, where this is not reasonably practicable, a description of the form and content of any notice given shall be filed in Court with the certificate.

9279.text: Non-compliance with Rules 54.65 to 54.67 is a ground on which the Court may, if it thinks fit, dismiss the petition.

9282.text: If at least five (5) business days before the hearing the petitioner, on an application without notice to any other party in such form as the Court may specify, satisfies the Court that:

9284.text: the Court may order that the petitioner has permission to withdraw the petition on such terms as to costs as the parties may agree or the Court may think fit.

9287.text: Every person who intends to appear on the hearing of the petition shall give to the petitioner notice of his intention in accordance with Rules 54.71 to 54.73 in such form as the Court may specify.

9290.text: (1) the name and address of the person giving it, and any telephone number and reference which may

9291.text: be required for communication with him or with any other person (to be also specified in the notice) authorised to speak or act on his behalf;

9292.text: (2) whether his intention is to support or oppose the petition;

9293.text: (3) whether or not he objects (in the event that a winding-up order is made by the Court) to the appointment as liquidator or provisional liquidator of the insolvency practitioner proposed for such appointment by the petitioner;

9294.text: (4) whether, if he does so object, he proposes that (in the event that a winding-up order is made by the Court) some other insolvency practitioner be appointed liquidator or provisional liquidator; and

9295.text: (5) the amount and nature of his debt (if any).

9297.text: The notice shall be sent to the petitioner at the address shown for him in the Court records or in the advertisement of the petition required by Regulation 5.3, or it may be sent to his legal representative.

9299.text: The notice shall be sent so as to reach the addressee not later than 4 p.m. on the business day before that which is appointed for the hearing (or, where the hearing has been adjourned, for the adjourned hearing).

9301.text: A person who fails to comply with Rules 54.70 to 54.73 may appear on the hearing of the petition only with the permission of the Court .

9304.text: The petitioner shall prepare for the Court a list of the persons (if any) who have given notice under Rule 54.70 in such form as the Court may specify, specifying their names and addresses and (if known to him) their respective legal representatives.

9306.text: Against the name of each creditor in the list it shall be stated whether his intention is to support the petition, or to oppose it and whether he objects (in the event that a winding-up order is made by the Court ) to the appointment as liquidator or provisional liquidator of the insolvency practitioner proposed for such appointment by the petitioner.

9308.text: On the date appointed for the hearing of the petition, a copy of the list shall be lodged at Court or handed to the Court before the commencement of the hearing.

9310.text: If any permission is given under Rule 54.74, the petitioner shall add to the list the same particulars in respect of each person to whom permission has been given.

9313.text: If the Company intends to oppose the petition, it shall file in Court a witness statement in opposition not less than five (5) business days before the date fixed for the hearing.

9315.text: A copy of the witness statement shall be sent by the Company to the petitioner forthwith after filing.

9318.text: If the Court adjourns the hearing of the petition, unless the Court otherwise directs, the petitioner must forthwith send:

9319.text: (1) to the Company; and (2) where any creditor has given notice under Rule 54.70 but was not present at the hearing, to that creditor; notice of the order adjourning the petition, stating the date and time of the adjourned hearing.

9322.text: Where a person petitions and is subsequently found not entitled to do so, or where the petitioner:

9323.text: (1) fails to advertise his petition within the time prescribed by the Regulations or this Part of the Rules or such extended time as the Court may allow;

9324.text: (2) consents to withdraw his petition, or to allow it to be dismissed, consents to an adjournment, or fails to appear in support of his petition when it is called on in Court on the day originally fixed for the hearing, or on a day to which it is adjourned; or

9325.text: (3) appears, but does not apply for an order in the terms of the prayer of his petition;

9326.text: the Court may, on such terms as it thinks just, substitute as petitioner any person who in its opinion would have a right to present a petition, and who is desirous of prosecuting it.

9328.text: An order of the Court under Rule 54.82 may, where a petitioner fails to advertise his petition within the

9329.text: time prescribed by the Regulations or this Part of the Rules , or consents to withdraw his petition, be made at any time.

9332.text: Rules 54.85 to 54.91 apply when a winding-up order has been made by the Court .

9334.text: A winding-up order shall be in such form as the Court may specify, subject to any amendments or further provisions as the Court considers appropriate.

9336.text: The petitioner shall, not later than the business day following that on which the order is made, lodge with the Court a draft of the form in which he proposes the order be drawn up; and the petitioner and every other person who has appeared on the hearing of the petition shall leave at the Court all the documents required for enabling the order to be completed as soon as reasonably practicable.

9338.text: It is not necessary for the Court to appoint a date and time for any person to attend to settle the order, unless in any particular case the special circumstances make an appointment necessary.

9340.text: A winding-up order must name a person as the liquidator or provisional liquidator in respect of the conduct of the winding-up in accordance with Article 58(1) of the Law and Regulation 5.4(2).

9342.text: Three copies of the winding-up order, sealed with the seal of the Court , shall be sent as soon as reasonably practicable by the Court to the liquidator or provisional liquidator (as the case may be) in respect of the conduct of the winding-up.

9344.text: The liquidator or provisional liquidator shall cause:

9345.text: (1) a sealed copy of the order to be served on the Company by prepaid letter addressed to it at its registered office (if any) or, if there is no registered office, at its principal or last known principal place of business; and

9346.text: (2) a sealed copy of the order to be sent to the Registrar of Companies.

9348.text: Upon the making of a winding-up order, the Court shall forthwith in accordance with Regulation 5.4(1):

9349.text: (1) give notice of the fact to the Company, the petitioner, the DFSA (in the case of an Authorised Person) and any other person represented at the hearing of the petition; and

9350.text: (2) cause notice of the fact that the order has been made to be advertised, in accordance with Regulation 5.3, in the English language newspaper(s) and in the Arabic language newspaper(s) as the Court may by Practice Direction designate.

9353.text: Unless the Court otherwise directs, when a petition is dismissed, the petitioner shall forthwith give notice of the dismissal. Such notice shall be advertised in the same manner as the petition was advertised under Rules 54.61 to 54.64 or otherwise as the Court may direct.

9355.text: The notice advertised under Rule 54.92 must state:

9358.text: Where the petitioner is not the Company itself and the petitioner has not complied with Rules 54.92 and 54.93 within 21 days of the date of the hearing at which the petition was dismissed, the Company may advertise notice of the dismissal itself. Where the Company advertises notice of the dismissal itself, then unless the Court orders otherwise:

9359.text:
(1) the petitioner shall pay to the Company the costs reasonably incurred by the Company in advertising such notice; and   
(2) the amount of the costs so payable, unless agreed between the Company and the petitioner, must be decided by detailed assessment and the Company may commence detailed assessment proceedings in accordance with Part 40 (Procedure for Detailed Assessment of Costs).

9361.text: Rules 54.92 to 54.94 above do not apply in the case where a petition is withdrawn pursuant to Rule 54.69 above.

9364.text: The Court may at any time after an order for winding up, on the application of the provisional liquidator, the liquidator or the DFSA or any creditor or contributory, and on proof to the satisfaction of the Court that all proceedings in the winding up ought to be stayed make an order staying the proceedings, either altogether or for a limited time, on such terms and conditions as the Court thinks fit.

9366.text: The Court may, before making an order, give notice of the application to the DFSA , who may furnish the Court with a report with respect to any facts or matters which are in its opinion relevant to the application.

9368.text: A copy of every order made staying a winding up shall forthwith be forwarded by the Company to the Registrar of Companies.

9372.text: An application to the Court for the appointment of a provisional liquidator under Article 59 of the Law may be made by:

9375.text: The application must be supported by a witness statement stating:

9376.text: (1) the grounds on which it is proposed that a provisional liquidator should be appointed;

9377.text: (2) that the person proposed to be appointed has consented to act and, to the best of the applicant’s belief, is a registered insolvency practitioner for the purposes of Article 89 of the Law;

9379.text: (a) there has been proposed or is in force for the Company a voluntary arrangement under Part 2 of the Law;

9380.text: (b) an administrative receiver is acting in relation to the Company;

9381.text: (c) a liquidator has been appointed for the Company’s voluntary winding-up; or

9382.text: (d) a cell receivership order under Regulation 8.2 has been made in respect of the Company; and

9383.text: (4) the applicant’s estimate of the value of the assets in respect of which the provisional liquidator is to be appointed, and the facts and bases upon which such estimate is made.

9385.text: The Court may on the application, if satisfied that sufficient grounds are shown for the appointment, make it on such terms as it thinks fit.

9388.text: Where a provisional liquidator has been appointed, the Court shall as soon as reasonably practicable give notice of the fact to the provisional liquidator.

9390.text: Unless the Court otherwise directs, on receipt of the notice of appointment, as soon as reasonably practicable the provisional liquidator shall give notice of that appointment by advertisement once in the English language newspaper(s) and in the Arabic language newspaper(s) as the Court may by Practice Direction designate.

9392.text: In addition to the standard contents (as set out in Section VII), the notice under Rule 54.103 (to be in such form as the Court may specify) must state:

9393.text: (1) that a provisional liquidator has been appointed; and

9395.text: Order Of Appointment Of Provisional Liquidator

9397.text: The order appointing the provisional liquidator shall specify the functions to be carried out by him in relation to the Company’s affairs.

9399.text: The Court shall as soon as reasonably practicable after the order is made, send sealed copies of the orders as follows:

9400.text: (1) three copies to the person appointed as provisional liquidator; and

9401.text: (2) if there is an administrative receiver or cell receiver acting in relation to the Company, one copy to him.

9403.text: Of the three copies of the order sent to the provisional liquidator under Rule 54.106(1):

9404.text: (1) one copy shall be sent by him to the Company or, if a liquidator has been appointed for the Company’s voluntary winding-up, to him; and

9405.text: (2) one shall be sent to the Registrar of Companies.

9408.text: The cost of providing the security required under the Law upon the appointment of a provisional liquidator shall be paid in the first instance by the provisional liquidator; but:

9409.text: (1) if a winding-up order is not made, the person so appointed is entitled to be reimbursed out of the property of the Company, and the Court may make an order accordingly; and

9410.text: (2) if a winding-up order is made, he is entitled to be reimbursed as an expense of the liquidation in the prescribed order of priority.

9412.text: If the provisional liquidator fails to give or keep up his security, the Court may remove him and make such order as it thinks fit as to costs.

9414.text: If an order is made under Rule 54.109 removing the provisional liquidator, or discharging an order appointing him, the Court shall give directions as to whether any, and if so what, steps should be taken for the appointment of another person in his place.

9417.text: The remuneration of the provisional liquidator shall be fixed by the Court from time to time on his application.

9419.text: In fixing his remuneration, the Court shall take into account:

9420.text: (1) the time properly given by him (as provisional liquidator) and his staff in attending to the Company’s affairs;

9421.text: (2) the complexity (or otherwise) of the case;

9422.text: (3) any respects in which, in connection with the Company’s affairs, there falls on the provisional liquidator any responsibility of an exceptional kind or degree;

9423.text: (4) the effectiveness with which the provisional liquidator appears to be carrying out, or to have carried out, his duties; and

9424.text: (5) the value and nature of the property with which he has had to deal.

9426.text: Without prejudice to any order the Court may make as to costs, the provisional liquidator’s remuneration shall be paid to him, and the amount of any expenses incurred by him reimbursed:

9427.text: (1) if a winding-up order is not made, out of the property of the Company; and

9428.text: (2) if a winding-up order is made, as an expense of the liquidation, in the prescribed order of priority.

9430.text: Unless the Court otherwise directs, in a case falling within Rule 54.113(1) above the provisional liquidator may retain out of the company’s property such sums or property as are or may be required for meeting his remuneration and expenses.

9433.text: The appointment of the provisional liquidator shall be terminated without further order on the appointment of a liquidator and may be terminated by the Court on his application or on that of any of the persons specified in Rule 54.99.

9435.text: If the provisional liquidator’s appointment terminates, whether in consequence of the dismissal of the winding-up petition or otherwise, the Court may give such directions as it thinks fit with respect to the accounts of his administration or any other matters which it thinks appropriate.

9437.text: Notice of termination of the appointment of a provisional liquidator must be given by the provisional liquidator, unless the termination is on the making of a winding-up order or the Court otherwise directs. Such notice must as soon as reasonably practicable be:

9440.text: In addition to the standard contents (as set out in Section VII), a notice under Rule 54.117 must state:

9442.text: V APPLICATION FOR A CELL RECEIVERSHIP ORDER

9445.text: This Section applies in relation to the making of a cell receivership order under Regulation 8.2.

9446.text: Application For A Cell Receivership Order

9448.text: An application for a cell receivership application shall be in such form as the Court may specify.

9450.text: If made by the Company or by its directors, an application for a cell receivership order shall state the name of the Company and its address for service, which (in the absence of special reasons to the contrary) is that of the Company’s registered office.

9452.text: If the application is made by the directors, it shall state that it is made under Regulation 8.3.1(b); but from and after making it is to be treated for all purposes as the application of the Company.

9454.text: If made by any other person (including the DFSA ), an application for a cell receivership order shall state the name and address for service of the applicant.

9456.text: The application shall contain a statement of the applicant’s belief that the cellular assets attributable to a particular cell of the Company are or are likely to be insufficient to discharge the claims of creditors in respect of that cell.

9459.text: There shall be attached to the application a written statement which shall be in such form as the Court may specify by each of the persons proposed to be a cell receiver stating:

9460.text: (1) that he consents to accept appointment;

9461.text: (2) details of any prior professional relationship(s) that he has had with the applicant or the Company to which he is to be appointed as cell receiver; and

9462.text: (3) his opinion that it is reasonably likely that one or more of the purposes set out in Regulation 8.2.3 would be achieved.

9463.text: Witness Statement In Support Of Cell Receivership Application

9465.text: Where it is proposed to apply to the Court for a cell receivership order to be made in relation to a Company, a witness statement complying with Rules 54.127 to 54.129 must be prepared, with a view to its being filed at Court in support of the application.

9467.text: If the application is to be made by the Company or by its directors, the witness statement shall be made by one of the directors, or the secretary of the Company, stating himself to make it on behalf of the company or, as the case may be, on behalf of the directors.

9469.text: If the application is to be made by any other person, the witness statement shall be made by the applicant or a person acting under the authority of the applicant, and there must be stated in the witness statement his means of knowledge of the matters to which the witness statement relates and the nature of his authority (if applicable).

9471.text: The witness statement in support of an application for a cell receivership order shall contain:

9472.text: (1) a statement of the financial position in respect of that cell, specifying (to the best of the applicant’s knowledge and belief) the assets and liabilities of the cell, including contingent and prospective liabilities;

9473.text: (2) details of any security known or believed to be held by creditors in respect of that cell;

9474.text: (3) details of any debt or liability owing to the applicant by that cell and any security held by the applicant in respect of that cell;

9475.text: (4) details of the nature of the business of that cell and, if different, the business of the Company as a whole;

9476.text: (5) details of any insolvency proceedings in relation to that cell or to the Company, including any

9477.text: petition that has been presented for the winding up of the Company or any administrative receiver who has been appointed in respect of the Company so far as within the immediate knowledge of the applicant; and

9478.text: (6) any other matters which, in the opinion of those intending to make the application for a cell receivership order, will assist the Court in deciding whether to make such an order, so far as lying within the knowledge or belief of the witness.

9481.text: The application (and all supporting documents) shall be filed with the Court , with a sufficient number of copies for service and use as provided by Rule 54.134.

9483.text: Each of the copies filed shall have applied to it the seal of the Court and be issued to the applicant.

9485.text: The Court shall fix a venue for the hearing of the application and this shall be endorsed on each copy of the application issued under Rule 54.131.

9487.text: After the application is filed, it is the duty of the applicant to notify the Court in writing of the existence of any insolvency proceedings in relation to the Company as soon as he becomes aware of them.

9490.text: The application shall be served in addition to those persons referred to in Regulation 8.3.3:

9491.text: (1) if there is pending a petition for the winding-up of the Company, on the petitioner (and also on the provisional liquidator) if any;

9492.text: (2) on the person proposed as the cell receiver; and

9493.text: (3) if a supervisor of a voluntary arrangement under Part 2 of the Law has been appointed, on him.

9494.text: In this Rule, references to the application are to a copy of the application issued by the Court under Rule 54.131 together with the witness statement required by Rule 54.126 and the documents attached to the application.

9495.text: Notice To Officers Charged With Execution Of Legal Process, Etc.

9497.text: The applicant shall as soon as reasonably practicable after filing the application give notice of its being

9499.text: (1) any enforcement officer or other officer who to his knowledge is charged with an execution or other legal process either against that cell of the Company or its property or against the Company or its property generally; and

9500.text: (2) any person who to his knowledge has distrained either against that cell of the Company or its property or against the Company or its property generally.

9501.text: Manner In Which Service Is To Be Effected

9503.text: Service of the application in accordance with 54.134 shall be effected by the applicant, or his legal practitioner, or by a person instructed by him or his legal practitioner, not less than five (5) business days before the date fixed for the hearing.

9505.text: Service shall be effected as follows:

9506.text:
(1) on the Company (subject to Rule 54.138 below), by delivering the documents to its registered office; (2) on any other person (subject to Rule 54.139 below), by delivering the documents to his proper address; or   
(3) in either case, in such other manner as the Court may direct.

9508.text: If delivery to a Company’s registered office is not practicable, service may be effected by delivery to its last known principal place of business.

9510.text: For the purposes of Rule 54.137 (2) above, a person’s proper address is any which he has previously notified as his address for service; but if he has not notified any such address, service may be effected by delivery to his usual or last known address.

9513.text: Service of the application must be verified by a certificate of service. The certificate of service must be sufficient to identify the application served and must specify:

9516.text: The certificate of service shall be filed with the Court as soon as reasonably practicable after service, and in any event not less than one (1) business day before the hearing of the application.

9517.text: Application Where Company In Liquidation Or Administrative Receivership

9519.text: Where an application is made for a cell receivership order under Regulation 8.2.4, the witness statement required by Rule 54.126 shall contain:

9520.text: (1) full details of the existing insolvency proceedings, including the name and address of the liquidator or administrative receiver, the date he was appointed and by whom;

9521.text: (2) the reasons why it has subsequently been considered appropriate that an application for a cell receivership order should be made;

9522.text: (3) all other matters that would, in the opinion of the applicant, assist the Court in considered the need to make provisions in respect of matters arising in connection with the liquidation or administrative receivership; and

9526.text: At the hearing of an application for a cell receivership order, any of the following persons may appear or be represented:

9529.text: If the Court makes a cell receivership order, it shall be in such form as the Court may specify.

9531.text: If the Court makes a cell receivership order, the costs of the applicant and of any person whose costs are allowed by the Court , are payable as an expense of the cell receivership.

9533.text: Where the Court makes a cell receivership order in relation to a Company upon an application under Regulation 8.2.4, the Court shall include in the order (i) such provision as the Court thinks fit with respect to matters arising in connection with the liquidation or administrative receivership and (ii) such other provisions as the Court shall think fit.

9536.text: If the Court makes a cell receivership order, it shall as soon as reasonably practicable send two sealed copies of the order to the person who made the application.

9538.text: The applicant shall send a sealed copy of the order as soon as reasonably practicable to:

9539.text: (1) the person appointed as cell receiver; and (2) if the Company is an Authorised Person, the DFSA .

9541.text: If the Court makes an order under Regulation 8.3.2, it shall give directions as to the persons to whom, and how, notice of that order is to be given.

9545.text: Subject to Rule 54.151, this Section applies in relation to the service of:

9547.text: which are required to be served by any provision of the Law, the Regulations or this Part of the Rules (“court documents”).

9549.text: This Section does not apply to the service of:

9550.text:
(1) a winding up petition;   
(2) any document relating to such a petition; or (3) a winding-up order.

9553.text: Except where different provision is made in this Part and subject to any direction or order of the Court , Part 9 (Service ) applies in relation to the service of court documents, whether within or outside the jurisdiction.

9555.text: For the purpose of the application by Rule 54.152 of Part 9 (Service ) to the service of documents in insolvency proceedings an application commencing insolvency proceedings or an application within insolvency proceedings against a respondent is to be treated as a claim form.

9558.text: Where in insolvency proceedings the Court makes an order staying any action, execution or other legal process against the property of a Company, service of the order may be effected by sending a sealed copy of the order to whatever is the address for service of the party having the carriage of the proceedings to be stayed.

9561.text: Where there are joint office-holders in insolvency proceedings, service on one of them is to be treated as service on all of them.

9562.text: VII CONTENT OF NOTICES TO BE ADVERTISED

9564.text: Where under the Law, the Regulations or this Part of the Rules a notice is to be advertised, in addition to any content specifically required by the Law, the Regulations or any other provision of this Part of the Rules , the content of such a notice must be as set out in this Section.

9566.text: All notices published must specify insofar as it is applicable in relation to the particular notice:

9567.text: (1) the name and postal address of the office-holder acting in the proceedings;

9568.text: (2) the capacity in which the office-holder is acting and the date of appointment;

9569.text: (3) either an e-mail address, or a telephone number, through which the office-holder may be contacted;

9570.text: (4) the name of any person other than the office-holder (if any) who may be contacted regarding the proceedings;

9571.text: (5) the Court name and any number assigned to the proceedings by the Court ;

9572.text: (6) the registered name and number of the Company;

9573.text: (7) the registered office of the Company or, in the case of a Recognised Company, its place of business in the DIFC or, in the case of any other Foreign Company, its registered office and, if different, its principal place of business;

9574.text: (8) any principal trading address if this is different from its registered office;

9575.text: (9) any name under which it was registered in the 12 months prior to the date of the commencement of the proceedings which are the subject of the notice; and

9576.text: (10) any name or style (other than its registered name) under which:

9577.text: (a) the Company carried on business; or

9578.text: (b) any debt owed to a creditor was incurred.

9580.text: Information required under this Section to be included in a notice to be advertised may be omitted if it is not reasonably practicable to obtain it.

9584.text: This Section applies in relation to costs in connection with insolvency proceedings.

9586.text: In this Section a reference to costs includes charges and expenses.

9587.text: Requirement To Assess Costs By The Detailed Procedure

9589.text: Where the costs of any person are payable as an expense out of the insolvent estate in accordance with the Law, the Regulations or by order of the Court , the amount payable must be decided by detailed assessment unless agreed between the office-holder and the person entitled to payment.

9591.text: In the absence of such agreement as is mentioned in Rule 54.161, the office-holder:

9592.text: (1) may serve notice requiring that person to commence detailed assessment proceedings in accordance with Part 40 (Procedure for Detailed Assessment of Costs); and

9593.text: (2) must serve such notice (except in an administrative receivership) where a liquidation or creditors’ committee formed in relation to the insolvency proceedings resolves that the amount of the costs must be decided by detailed assessment.

9595.text: Where the costs of any person employed by an office-holder in insolvency proceedings are required to be decided by detailed assessment under an order of the Court directing that those costs are to be paid otherwise than out of the insolvent estate, the Registrar shall note on the final costs certificate by whom, or the manner in which, the costs are to be paid.

9597.text: Where an office-holder is made a party to any proceedings on the application of another party to the proceedings, he shall not be personally be liable for costs unless the Court otherwise directs.

9599.text: Nothing in Rules 54.161 to 54.164 above shall affect the power of the Court to order an immediate assessment of the costs of any proceedings, or part of any proceedings, before the Court pursuant to Part 38 (General Rules About Costs).

9602.text: This Section applies where a party to, or person affected by, any proceedings in an insolvency:

9603.text: (1) applies to the Court for an order allowing his costs, or part of them, incidental to the proceedings; and

9604.text: (2) that application is not made at the time of the proceedings.

9606.text: The person concerned shall serve a sealed copy of his or her application on the office-holder.

9608.text: The office-holder may appear on an application to which Rule 54.166 applies.

9610.text: No costs of or incidental to the application shall be allowed to the applicant unless the Court is satisfied that

9611.text: the application could not have been made at the time of the proceedings.
